research.topic: RealityKit Entity-Component-System architecture for building a large interactive 3D UI on visionOS
research.summary: RealityKit is an ECS: Entities are lightweight containers, Components are pure data attached per entity, and Systems hold per-frame behavior. The single most important finding for this app is that the RealityView `update:` closure is NOT a per-frame tick — it fires only when SwiftUI-observed state it reads changes — and Apple (WWDC25 274) explicitly warns that mutating an Observed entity property inside it causes a recursive loop. That is exactly the drag snap-back bug: the current `reconcile()` (called from `update:`) re-asserts every card's transform from stale model data each pass, fighting the live gesture, and the `@State draggingID` guard can't work because @State mutated in a RealityKit gesture isn't reliably visible to the next update pass. The fix is a real transform-ownership model: encode ownership in a Component a System respects, mutate transforms only in gesture closures / Systems / `Entity.animate()`, and restrict reconcile to entity identity (add/remove/attach) — never live transforms. The "x" control not highlighting is a geometric/hierarchy problem: the parent card's collision box (depth max(z,0.03)) encloses the child x at +0.006 z so the parent swallows hover/input, and a SwiftUI `.hoverEffect` inside a ViewAttachment is not a reliable RealityKit hover signal — use HoverEffectComponent (.highlight/.shader) on the control entity with its own non-enclosed collider and input/hover-group isolation. visionOS 26 ships turnkey APIs that make grab-drag near-trivial (ManipulationComponent with releaseBehavior=.stay), per-entity gestures (GestureComponent) that retire the brittle name-prefix parsing, Observable entities for clean scene→SwiftUI flow, and implicit Entity.animate for smooth motion. All of this preserves the locked "SwiftUI renders content, RealityKit places it" paradigm via ViewAttachmentComponent / PresentationComponent.

corePrinciples.principle: The RealityView update: closure is NOT a per-frame tick — it runs only when SwiftUI-observed state it reads changes.
corePrinciples.detail: "This update closure is not like an update or tick function in a game engine — those run every frame, whereas this only runs when the state it references changes." Because the current reconcile() is called from update: and reads viewModel.renderedEntities plus other @Observable fields, it re-fires on ANY observed change and rebuilds the whole scene (re-adds all edges/ports, re-sets every transform and collision box). Continuous/runtime behavior must NOT live here. This is the root of bug #1: the closure re-asserts each card to its stored (stale-during-drag) position every pass.
corePrinciples.source: Step Into Vision 'RealityKit Basics: update closure'; Apple Developer Forums thread 733020; WWDC23 10080

corePrinciples.principle: Never mutate observed entity state inside the update closure — it creates recursive feedback loops, the same failure mode as drag snap-back.
corePrinciples.detail: WWDC25 274 is explicit: entities are now Observable (position/scale/rotation/children/components), so reading them in a view body creates a dependency and writing them in update: re-triggers update: -> infinite loop. The session's stated SAFE places to mutate a transform are (a) gesture closures, (b) custom Systems, and (c) Entity.animate(...). The UNSAFE place is the update closure. Transform ownership must be explicit per instant; today the model (via reconcile) AND the gesture both write the same transform, so they fight. Make exactly one authority own an entity's transform at any moment.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 'Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit' (session 274)

corePrinciples.principle: Per-frame / continuous behavior belongs in a custom System, queried with EntityQuery and ticked via update(context:).
corePrinciples.detail: Pattern: struct FooSystem: System { static let query = EntityQuery(where: .has(FooComponent.self)); init(scene: Scene){}; func update(context: SceneUpdateContext){ for e in context.entities(matching: Self.query, updatingSystemWhen: .rendering){...} } }, then register once at launch with FooSystem.registerSystem(). updatingSystemWhen: takes .rendering (sync to display refresh — default choice) or .always; it exists because visionOS has high/variable refresh rates. Registered systems run automatically on all RealityKit content on the MainActor render loop. This is where jitter-on-highlight, smooth glide-to-layout-target, edge re-derivation, and drag inertia belong — not in reconcile.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC23 10080; swiftorbit.io ECS floating brick; Apple 'Implementing systems for entities in a scene'; daniloc/MyFirstECS

corePrinciples.principle: Decide behavior placement by lifetime: one-shot intent -> gesture handler; reactive identity/topology -> SwiftUI declarative; continuous/runtime -> System + Component.
corePrinciples.detail: Rule for this codebase: (1) Gesture handlers express user INTENT and write a target into a Component (set DragStateComponent.isDragging, or LayoutTargetComponent.target). (2) SwiftUI/RealityView reconcile owns ONLY entity existence/identity (add/remove cards, attach the right ViewAttachment, set name) — it must NOT touch live transforms. (3) Systems own continuous transform evolution (glide toward layout target unless dragging; apply jitter; recompute edges from live positions). This split is what makes grab-drag trivial: the drag System/gesture owns the transform while dragging, reconcile is forbidden from overwriting it, and on release a one-shot persists to the model.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 274 (safe mutation sites); WWDC23 10080 (systems for continuous behavior)

corePrinciples.principle: Drive SwiftUI from RealityKit through Observable entities/components, not by polling; keep model-owned data flowing the other way through your @Observable view model.
corePrinciples.detail: visionOS 26 makes Entity and components Observable: reading entity.position or entity.components[Foo.self] in a view body (or via entity.observable.components[...]) creates a reactive dependency, so a System mutating that component auto-updates SwiftUI with no glue. Use withObservationTracking { } onChange: for imperative observation. Clean bidirectional contract: model->scene via your view model + reconcile (identity only); scene->SwiftUI via Observable components (e.g. a HUD reading drag state/selection). New CoordinateSpace3D / GeometryProxy3D.coordinateSpace3D() replaces manual points-to-meters/axis math.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 274; swiftorbit.io 'Sync RealityKit ECS with SwiftUI for Collision Events'

corePrinciples.principle: InputTarget and Collision are hierarchical; child controls need explicit input/hover isolation so the parent card doesn't swallow them.
corePrinciples.detail: InputTargetComponent propagates to descendants with CollisionComponents: a child IS independently targetable, BUT a descendant's allowedInputTypes overrides ancestors and isEnabled=false hides a subtree from gestures. The current bug is geometric: the parent card collider has depth max(e.z,0.03) and the x sits only +0.006 z in front, so the parent physically encloses the child and gaze/taps resolve to the parent. Fixes: flatten the parent collider z, push the control clearly outside the parent box, and/or give the control its own non-enclosed collider. HoverEffectComponent also applies to a hierarchy and propagates broadly without an explicit group.
corePrinciples.source: Apple 'Responding to gestures on an entity'; Apple Developer Forums thread 747256; Apple HoverEffectComponent reference

corePrinciples.principle: Hover/highlight must be expressed with HoverEffectComponent styles (RealityKit-visible), not a SwiftUI .hoverEffect buried inside a ViewAttachment.
corePrinciples.detail: HoverEffectComponent (visionOS 1) gained styles in visionOS 2: .spotlight (default), .highlight (uniform tint via HoverEffectComponent.HighlightHoverEffectStyle(color:strength:)), and .shader (drives a ShaderGraphMaterial 'Hover State' node with an animatable 0->1 intensity — the right tool for playful jitter/glow). Hover grouping (HoverEffectGroup / GroupID, visionOS 2) controls whether grouped entities activate together; without an explicit group hover propagates broadly. The current DismissBadge uses SwiftUI .hoverEffect { effect.scaleEffect } inside a ViewAttachmentComponent — that scales the SwiftUI view in its own 2D space and is not a dependable RealityKit hover here. Use a .shader or .highlight HoverEffectComponent on the control entity (or a System reading HoverState applying jitter via a JitterComponent).
corePrinciples.source: WWDC24 session 10103; WWDC24 'Create custom hover effects in visionOS' (10152); WWDC25 'What's new in RealityKit' (287)

corePrinciples.principle: For grab-and-drag specifically, prefer the built-in ManipulationComponent over a hand-rolled DragGesture+reconcile.
corePrinciples.detail: visionOS 26's ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(entity, collisionShapes:[...]) auto-adds InputTarget + Collision + HoverEffect + Manipulation and gives pick-up, telescoping move, one/two-hand rotate, scale, and hand-off for free. Crucially the default releaseBehavior = .reset is exactly the snap-back symptom — set releaseBehavior = .stay to keep the moved position. Lifecycle via ManipulationEvents (WillBegin, DidUpdateTransform, WillRelease, WillEnd, DidHandOff); dynamics exposes inertia, scalingBehavior, secondaryRotationBehavior. Persist to backend on WillEnd/WillRelease. If you keep a custom gesture, the ownership rule still holds: while the manipulator owns the transform, reconcile must not write it.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 'What's new in RealityKit' (287); Step Into Vision 'Deep Dive into Manipulation on visionOS'

corePrinciples.principle: Use Entity.animate() and content.animate {} for smooth transitions; update topology incrementally, not from scratch.
corePrinciples.detail: visionOS 26 adds implicit animation for TransformComponent (and Audio/Model/Light color) via entity.animate(.easeInOut(duration:)) { entity.transform = ... } and a content.animate {} block usable in update. This replaces per-frame manual lerping for layout transitions and gives smooth 'fly to type-cluster slot' motion. Separately, the current reconcile deletes and re-adds ALL edges/ports every pass — for a scalable scene, edges should be persistent entities updated in place (or owned by an EdgeSystem repositioning a stable line/port entity per dependency), keyed by a stable id, so the scene doesn't thrash.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 274 (Entity.animate, content.animate); WWDC23 10080 (systems repositioning entities each frame)

corePrinciples.principle: SwiftUI-renders-content / RealityKit-places-it is fully preserved via ViewAttachmentComponent + PresentationComponent + GestureComponent (all visionOS 26).
corePrinciples.detail: ViewAttachmentComponent(rootView:) makes a SwiftUI view an entity (already used); PresentationComponent(presentation:.popover, binding:$x){...} attaches modals/popovers to entities (cleaner than .sheet on the volume for in-scene editing); GestureComponent(TapGesture()...) attaches SwiftUI gestures directly to an entity, removing the brittle name-prefix parsing ('ctl::role::owner', 'edge::') used today. This is the scalable replacement for the single top-level .targetedToAnyEntity() gesture + string dispatch.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 274 (ViewAttachmentComponent, PresentationComponent, GestureComponent); WWDC25 287

keyAPIs.name: EntityQuery / SceneUpdateContext.entities(matching:updatingSystemWhen:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Selects the entities a System operates on by component composition (e.g. .has(DragStateComponent.self)).
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Inside a System's update to iterate matching entities; scene.performQuery(query) for ad-hoc queries outside a tick.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Predicates compose with .has, &&, ||, !. Prefer marker components (DragStateComponent, LayoutTargetComponent, JitterComponent) so queries are cheap and intent is explicit.

keyAPIs.name: System protocol + registerSystem() + update(context: SceneUpdateContext)
keyAPIs.purpose: Hosts per-frame/continuous behavior; the right home for transform glide, jitter, inertia, edge updates.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: For anything that must run over time rather than once on a state change. Register once at app init.
keyAPIs.gotchas: init(scene:) required; runs on MainActor render loop. updatingSystemWhen: .rendering (default) vs .always. SystemDependency orders systems relative to each other.

keyAPIs.name: custom Component (struct: Component [, Codable])
keyAPIs.purpose: Pure-data runtime state owned by the SCENE, attached per entity — the clean separation from model-owned data.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Mark transform ownership (e.g. TransformOwner enum .model/.gesture/.animating), store layout targets, hover/jitter state, selection — anything a System reads/writes.
keyAPIs.gotchas: One component per type per entity. Mutate by copy-back: var c = e.components[T.self]; c.x=...; e.components[T.self]=c. registerComponent() auto for in-target Swift types; call explicitly for RCP/cross-module types.

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationComponent + ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(_:collisionShapes:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Turnkey pick-up / move / rotate / scale / hand-off — the 'near-trivial grab-and-drag' the developer wants.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Default choice for movable cards/nodes; replaces the hand-rolled DragGesture + manual coordinate conversion.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Default releaseBehavior = .reset causes snap-back; set .stay. Persist on ManipulationEvents.WillEnd/WillRelease. dynamics can disable scaling/secondary rotation for move-only. Auto-adds InputTarget/Collision/HoverEffect.

keyAPIs.name: HoverEffectComponent (.spotlight / .highlight / .shader) + HoverEffectGroup
keyAPIs.purpose: RealityKit-visible hover/highlight; .shader drives a ShaderGraph HoverState node (0->1 intensity) for glass glow + jitter.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: On the card AND independently on the x/control entity. .highlight for simple tint; .shader for playful animated effects; group with GroupID to control co-activation.
keyAPIs.gotchas: A SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside a ViewAttachment is NOT a reliable RealityKit hover. Without an explicit group, hover propagates across the hierarchy. Child needs its own collider not enclosed by the parent's.

keyAPIs.name: InputTargetComponent (isEnabled, allowedInputTypes) + CollisionComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: Hit-testing + gesture targeting. Hierarchical: descendants are independently targetable.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Per interactive entity. Give the x-control its own non-enclosed collider; use child allowedInputTypes/isEnabled to override the parent so the card doesn't swallow the control.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Parent collider depth max(z,0.03) enclosing a child at +0.006 z routes input to the parent. CollisionFilter (group/mask, 32 groups) controls collision pairing, but input precedence is geometric + hierarchy flags, not just filters.

keyAPIs.name: Observable Entity + entity.animate(_:) / content.animate { }
keyAPIs.purpose: Read entity/component state into SwiftUI reactively; animate transforms implicitly for smooth motion.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: scene->SwiftUI HUD/state without polling; smooth glide to layout slots; release animations after a drag.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Reading entity.position in a view body creates a dependency; writing it in update: causes an infinite loop. Animate only inside gesture/system/event closures or via animate().

keyAPIs.name: GestureComponent + ViewAttachmentComponent + PresentationComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: Per-entity SwiftUI gestures, SwiftUI-view-as-entity, and entity-anchored modals/popovers — preserves 'SwiftUI renders, RealityKit places.'
keyAPIs.whenToUse: GestureComponent to drop name-prefix gesture parsing; ViewAttachmentComponent for all cards (already used); PresentationComponent for in-scene edit popovers instead of volume-level .sheet.
keyAPIs.gotchas: ViewAttachmentComponent replaces visionOS 1's builder attachments API (attachments aren't ready in make: — the current setup-race workaround). Gesture values are in the entity's coordinate space by default.

antiPatterns.pattern: Letting two authorities (model/reconcile AND gesture) write the same entity transform, then gating with a SwiftUI @State flag like draggingID.
antiPatterns.why: @State mutated inside a RealityKit gesture .onChanged is not reliably visible to the update closure before the next pass (confirmed by the failed fix). Transform ownership must be encoded in the scene (a Component a System respects), not in SwiftUI view state.

antiPatterns.pattern: Mutating an Observable entity property (position/components) inside the update: closure.
antiPatterns.why: Writing observed state inside update: re-triggers update: -> recursive/infinite loop (explicitly warned in WWDC25 274). Mutate transforms only in gesture closures, Systems, or via Entity.animate().

antiPatterns.pattern: Encoding a parent collider deep enough to enclose its child control, then expecting the child to receive hover/taps.
antiPatterns.why: Input/hover resolves geometrically up the hierarchy; an enclosing parent collider swallows the child. The x at +0.006 z inside a max(z,0.03) box can never reliably get its own hover. Needs a non-enclosed child collider and/or input/hover-group isolation.

antiPatterns.pattern: Driving 3D highlight via a SwiftUI .hoverEffect placed inside a ViewAttachmentComponent's rootView.
antiPatterns.why: That effect operates in the attachment's 2D SwiftUI space and does not translate to a dependable RealityKit hover for an entity-targeted control. Use HoverEffectComponent (.highlight/.shader) on the entity, or a System reading HoverState.

antiPatterns.pattern: Disambiguating gestures by parsing entity names ('ctl::role::owner', 'edge::') under a single top-level .targetedToAnyEntity() gesture.
antiPatterns.why: Stringly-typed dispatch doesn't scale and is fragile. Per-entity GestureComponent (visionOS 26) or typed marker components + EntityQuery give type-safe, localized handling.

antiPatterns.pattern: Deleting and re-adding all edges and port spheres from scratch on every reconcile pass.
antiPatterns.why: O(edges) churn per observed change causes visible flicker and allocation pressure as the graph grows. Edges should be persistent, stably-keyed entities updated in place by an EdgeSystem reading live positions.

antiPatterns.pattern: Relying on the default ManipulationComponent releaseBehavior (.reset).
antiPatterns.why: .reset returns the entity to its original transform on release — indistinguishable from the snap-back bug. For a persistent placed workspace set releaseBehavior = .stay and persist on WillEnd.

recommendationsForOurApp: Adopt an explicit transform-ownership model. Add a scene-owned component (e.g. TransformAuthorityComponent { case model, gesture, animating }). reconcile() may set a card's transform ONLY when authority == .model and the entity is newly added; while a drag/manipulation owns the entity it is .gesture and reconcile is forbidden from writing position. This is the root fix for the drag snap-back, replacing the unreliable @State draggingID guard.

Shrink reconcile() to identity-only. It should add/remove card entities, attach the correct ViewAttachmentComponent, set the stable name/id, and set persistent components ONCE on creation. Remove the per-pass card.position re-assert (lines ~245-250 of SpatialSceneView.swift) and the per-pass CollisionComponent rebuild from visualBounds — set collision once and update only on real geometry change.

Replace the hand-rolled DragGesture with ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(card, collisionShapes:[...]) and set releaseBehavior = .stay. Subscribe to ManipulationEvents.WillEnd to call viewModel.endDrag and persist. Disable scaling/secondary-rotation via dynamics if you want move-only. This makes grab-drag near-trivial and eliminates manual coordinate conversion.

Introduce custom Systems for continuous behavior: a LayoutSystem that glides un-hand-moved entities toward their type-cluster layout target (LayoutTargetComponent) using Entity.animate or per-frame lerp, skipping entities whose authority is .gesture; an EdgeSystem that repositions persistent, stably-keyed edge/port entities from live positions instead of delete-and-re-add; and a JitterSystem that reads HoverState to apply the playful jitter on highlight.

Fix the x control hover at the root: give the control its own entity with a small, non-enclosed CollisionComponent placed clearly in front of the card, set the parent card collider to a shallow z (don't inflate to max(z,0.03) so it doesn't enclose the child), and use a HoverEffectComponent (.highlight or .shader) ON the control entity. Use HoverEffectGroup/GroupID to isolate the control's hover from the card. Remove the SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside DismissBadge — it doesn't drive RealityKit.

Retire name-prefix gesture parsing. Move to per-entity GestureComponent (visionOS 26) so each card/control/volume carries its own tap/drag handler, and use typed marker components instead of 'ctl::', 'edge::' string prefixes for dispatch and EntityQuery filtering.

Wire scene->SwiftUI via Observable entities/components rather than polling: a SelectionComponent or DragStateComponent read by SwiftUI HUD/banners auto-updates with no glue. Keep model->scene flowing through the existing @Observable view model + identity-only reconcile. Use CoordinateSpace3D/GeometryProxy3D.coordinateSpace3D() for any remaining coordinate conversions.

Use PresentationComponent(.popover, binding:) anchored to the tapped entity for in-scene edit/note editing instead of volume-level .sheet, keeping editing spatially attached to its card. Keep the locked SwiftUI-renders / RealityKit-places paradigm via ViewAttachmentComponent for all card visuals and ShaderGraphMaterial for glass + hover glow.

Keep the pure nonisolated SpatialLayoutEngine exactly as-is — it computes layout targets that a MainActor LayoutSystem applies. The engine stays Foundation/simd-only and unit-tested; the System is the only thing that touches entities. This satisfies SWIFT_DEFAULT_ACTOR_ISOLATION=MainActor while keeping the engine testable.

Verify with xcodebuild -scheme TaskPlannerVision -destination 'generic/platform=visionOS Simulator' (SourceKit reports false errors). Note these are visionOS 26 APIs (ManipulationComponent, GestureComponent, PresentationComponent, Observable entities, .highlight/.shader hover, HoverEffectGroup) — fine for the 26.2 target; the System/Component/EntityQuery/HoverEffectComponent/InputTarget foundations are battle-tested since visionOS 1.

research.topic: Reconciling a declarative data model into a retained 3D scene graph without a redraw-everything-every-pass loop (visionOS 26 / RealityKit, with cross-engine prior art from React-Three-Fiber, Unity, Bevy, Needle)
research.summary: The current architecture has a structural inversion: a single `reconcile()` runs on EVERY RealityView `update` pass and re-asserts every property of every entity from scratch (position, collision, controls, all edges), which makes it the authority over transforms it does not actually own during a live gesture or animation. This is exactly the failure mode Apple now warns about: in visionOS 26 (WWDC25 "Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit", session 274) entities are Observable, so reading entity state in `update` creates a SwiftUI dependency and mutating entities there can loop; Apple's explicit guidance is "Don't modify observed state in the update closure — modify in gesture closures, custom Systems, or the make closure." Every retained-scene reconciler studied (React-Three-Fiber, Unity DOTS Hybrid Renderer, Bevy ECS, Needle) shares one principle: diff and write ONLY the properties that changed, and never re-touch a value that another authority (gesture/physics/animation) currently owns — R3F's `applyProps` even filters identical props before writing. The canonical fix for the drag snap-back is to stop letting the reconciler own transforms: adopt visionOS 26's `ManipulationComponent` (which owns the transform during manipulation and keeps it on release via `releaseBehavior = .stay`), or at minimum cache the start transform on first `onChanged` and gate the reconciler with a per-entity "owned-by-gesture" dirty flag — not view `@State`, which isn't reliably visible to the update closure mid-gesture. The x-control hover bug is a hierarchical-collision/input-precedence problem: the parent card's CollisionComponent encloses the child badge (only +0.006 m away) and swallows its hover/tap; the fix is per-entity `GestureComponent` + `HoverEffectComponent` on the badge with non-overlapping collision (and the card itself should not enclose its controls). visionOS 26 provides first-class primitives — Observable entities, `GestureComponent`, `ManipulationComponent`, `PresentationComponent`, `Entity.animate`/`content.animate` — that let you replace the imperative reconcile loop with targeted, ownership-respecting updates plus an interpolation layer (lerp/critically-damped spring) between the data-model target transform and the presented transform.

corePrinciples.principle: A reconciler must write ONLY the properties it owns, and skip any property currently owned by a live gesture, physics, or animation
corePrinciples.detail: This is the root cause of the snap-back. Our reconcile() re-sets card.position from the view-model's STORED position on every update pass; during a drag that stored value is stale (written only on release), so the card is yanked back to its tray slot each frame and jerks back on release. Every studied engine obeys ownership: in Unity DOTS the transform is synced from the data component (LocalToWorld) only when that component is the source of truth, and the guidance is to 'avoid writing anyways even if the value is the same' so change-versioning stays meaningful; R3F's applyProps filters identical props and skips no-op writes. The transform of an entity has exactly ONE authority at any instant — model, gesture, or animation — and the reconciler must defer to the live one.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 session 274 'Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit'; Unity DOTS Hybrid Renderer change-filter docs; R3F applyProps technical breakdown (codyb.co)

corePrinciples.principle: In visionOS 26 the RealityView update closure is part of SwiftUI body evaluation; reading observed entity state there creates a dependency and mutating entities there causes loops
corePrinciples.detail: Apple's session 274 states this directly and shows the anti-pattern: setting entity.position inside `update` when entities are Observable creates an infinite re-evaluation loop. The recommended places to mutate entities are: the make closure (one-time setup, no dependency), gesture closures, and custom Systems. This is the framework-level statement of why a redraw-everything reconcile loop is the wrong foundation on visionOS 26.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 session 274 'Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit'; tomkrikorian visionOS 26 AGENTS.md ('Do not implement reconciliation loops in the update closure')

corePrinciples.principle: Stable, keyed entity identity is what makes minimal-mutation diffing possible — match by stable id, mutate in place, never recreate
corePrinciples.detail: React reconciliation uses stable keys so it can tell which items were added/removed/moved and preserve instance state instead of tearing down and rebuilding. R3F leans on this: <mesh/> maps to a persistent THREE.Mesh, and only commitUpdate mutates the existing instance — geometries/materials are never recreated on a prop change. Our code already has stable ids (SpatialEntity.id == attachment id == entity.name), which is the right foundation; the problem is we rebuild side-effects (collision, controls, ALL edges) every pass instead of diffing against what's already in the scene.
corePrinciples.source: React legacy reconciliation docs; R3F technical breakdown (codyb.co); pmndrs/react-three-fiber

corePrinciples.principle: Dirty-flag / change-detection systems exist precisely so you process only what changed, not the whole world every frame
corePrinciples.detail: Bevy's Changed<T> query and Ref<T> yield only entities whose component was mutated (flags double-buffered across two frames); Unity DOTS uses chunk-level change versions bumped on write so a filtered query touches only modified chunks. The lesson for us: maintain an explicit dirty set (e.g. ids whose model transform changed since last applied, plus an edges-dirty flag) so a reconcile pass writes a handful of transforms and rebuilds edges only when topology/positions actually moved — instead of O(n) full rewrite + full edge teardown every observed change.
corePrinciples.source: Bevy Unofficial Cheat Book (change-detection); Bevy DetectChanges docs.rs; Unity DOTS chunk versioning (5argon)

corePrinciples.principle: Put an interpolation layer between the target transform (data model) and the presented transform (scene)
corePrinciples.detail: Engines separate 'where it should be' from 'where it is drawn'. Instead of hard-setting card.position to the model value each pass (which fights gestures and looks jerky), drive the presented transform toward the target with a frame-rate-independent damped follow (SmoothDamp / critically-damped spring) so layout changes and returns-to-tray animate smoothly and never overshoot. visionOS 26 gives this natively via Entity.animate(to:relativeTo:animation:) and content.animate { } — set the TARGET, let RealityKit interpolate. This also cleanly decouples the auto-layout engine output (target) from live manipulation (presented), removing the snap-back by construction.
corePrinciples.source: Rory Driscoll 'Frame Rate Independent Damping using Lerp'; theorangeduck 'Spring-It-On'; Unity Vector3.SmoothDamp; WWDC25 session 274 (Entity.animate / content.animate)

corePrinciples.principle: Hierarchical collision and input precedence must be designed, not accidental — a parent collider that encloses a child swallows the child's hover/tap
corePrinciples.detail: HoverEffectComponent applies to a hierarchy and requires the collider it intersects to belong to the intended entity. Our x-badge sits only +0.006 m in front of a card whose CollisionComponent depth is max(z,0.03), so the parent box geometrically contains the badge and intercepts the gaze ray / pinch first; the badge's SwiftUI .hoverEffect never fires. Fix: give controls their own non-overlapping collision sitting clearly in front of the card, drive the highlight with a RealityKit-visible HoverEffectComponent (not only a SwiftUI scaleEffect inside the attachment), and scope each control's behavior with its own GestureComponent rather than name-parsing one global gesture.
corePrinciples.source: Apple HoverEffectComponent reference; askwwdc hover-effect; stepinto.vision GestureComponent article; WWDC25 session 287 'What's new in RealityKit'

keyAPIs.name: RealityView make / update / attachments closures
keyAPIs.purpose: make = one-time setup (no SwiftUI dependency); update = react to discrete state changes only; attachments = declare SwiftUI views surfaced as ViewAttachmentEntity
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Use make for initial entity creation/placement. Use update SPARINGLY for genuinely changed structure. Do NOT run a full reconcile-everything pass here on visionOS 26.
keyAPIs.gotchas: On visionOS 26 the update closure participates in body evaluation; reading Observable entity state creates a dependency and mutating entities here can loop. This is the framework's own warning against our current pattern.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 1.0 (RealityView); Observable-entity loop caveat is visionOS 26

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationComponent + ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(_:hoverEffect:allowedInputTypes:collisionShapes:)
keyAPIs.purpose: One-component drag/rotate/scale/two-hand/hand-off with built-in audio and hover; OWNS the entity transform during manipulation
keyAPIs.whenToUse: This is the canonical fix for grab-and-drag. Replace the hand-rolled DragGesture + transform caching for moving nodes. Set releaseBehavior = .stay to keep the entity where dropped.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Default releaseBehavior is .reset (snaps back) — you MUST set .stay. configureEntity auto-adds Collision+InputTarget+HoverEffect. Persist via ManipulationEvents.WillEnd; constrain (clamp to bounds) via DidUpdateTransform — constrain, don't replace. Other events: WillBegin, WillRelease, DidHandOff. dynamics lets you disable rotation/scaling if you only want translation.

keyAPIs.name: GestureComponent(_:) on an entity
keyAPIs.purpose: Attach a SwiftUI gesture (Tap/Drag/etc, targetedToEntity) directly to a specific entity so each entity carries its own handler
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Replace the two global .gesture(...).targetedToAnyEntity() + name-parsing ('ctl::', 'edge::') dispatch. Give the card, the x-badge, ports, and volumes their own gestures.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Entity still needs CollisionComponent + InputTargetComponent. Eliminates the brittle string-prefix routing entirely.

keyAPIs.name: Entity is Observable / entity.observe(_:) / entity.observable
keyAPIs.purpose: Two-way data flow: SwiftUI views re-render on entity property changes; code can observe entity property changes
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Use to surface live entity transforms back into SwiftUI (e.g. inspector showing live position) and to let small self-contained views depend on a single entity rather than the whole scene.
keyAPIs.gotchas: The flip side of the loop warning: reading entity.position inside the update/body closure creates a dependency. Keep observation in small leaf views and out of the reconcile path.

keyAPIs.name: Entity.animate(to:relativeTo:animation:speed:) and content.animate(animation:) { }
keyAPIs.purpose: Animate Transform/audio/model/light component changes with SwiftUI-style animations (e.g. .bouncy) — set the target, RealityKit interpolates
keyAPIs.whenToUse: This is the built-in interpolation layer: animate layout-engine placements, return-to-tray, and pop-out instead of hard-setting positions each pass.
keyAPIs.gotchas: move(to:) / Entity.animate is preferred over animating inside the update closure; animating in update can still interact with the body-evaluation/observation rules.

keyAPIs.name: HoverEffectComponent(.highlight / .spotlight / .shader)
keyAPIs.purpose: RealityKit-native gaze/hover feedback applied across an entity hierarchy (highlight tint, spotlight, or shader-graph-driven intensity)
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Drive the x-badge and node highlight here (plus a 'jitter' via Entity.animate on hover) so the effect is RealityKit-visible, not a SwiftUI scaleEffect buried in an attachment that doesn't render.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Effect targets the entity whose collider the gaze ray hits FIRST — an enclosing parent collider steals it. spotlight/shader styles are richer than the default .highlight. Custom hover-state shader needs a shader-graph hover node.

keyAPIs.name: ViewAttachmentComponent(rootView:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Render a SwiftUI view as an entity in 3D (the locked 'SwiftUI renders content' paradigm)
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Keep using it for cards/panels/notes/controls. In visionOS 26 you can create attachments imperatively as components rather than only via the attachments builder, giving finer control over when/where they appear.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Was the source of the 'setup race' (attachments not ready in make). The component form (visionOS 2.6+) avoids relying on RealityViewAttachments timing.

keyAPIs.name: PresentationComponent(isPresented:configuration:content:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Present SwiftUI popovers/sheets anchored to a RealityKit entity from within the scene
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Consider for in-scene node editing / context menus instead of routing everything through top-level .sheet bindings keyed off selectedEntityID.
keyAPIs.gotchas: New API; validate against your volumetric window before committing UX to it.

keyAPIs.name: DragGesture caching pattern (start transform on first onChanged, apply translation as delta, clear on onEnded)
keyAPIs.purpose: The pre-26 canonical drag: store entity start position once, add value.convert(translation) delta, never re-read live position mid-drag
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Fallback if you cannot adopt ManipulationComponent. Combine with a per-entity 'gesture owns transform' flag so the reconciler skips that entity.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Caching the START is essential — reading live position each frame compounds error. The flag MUST live where the update closure can read it reliably (entity component or @Observable model field), NOT view @State, which is not reliably visible to the update closure between a gesture onChanged and the next reconcile (our failed fix).

antiPatterns.pattern: A single reconcile(content:attachments:) that re-asserts EVERY property of EVERY entity on every update pass
antiPatterns.why: Update fires on any observed @Observable change, so this is effectively a full redraw loop. It makes the reconciler the de-facto owner of transforms it doesn't own during gestures/animations (root cause of drag snap-back), and on visionOS 26 reading/writing Observable entities here risks infinite body re-evaluation. Apple explicitly says not to modify observed entity state in update.

antiPatterns.pattern: Re-setting card.position from the data model's STORED position while a drag is in progress
antiPatterns.why: The stored model position is stale until drag-end, so each pass yanks the card back to its tray/wall slot and it jerks back on release. Transform ownership must transfer to the gesture for the duration of the drag; the reconciler must defer.

antiPatterns.pattern: Using a view @State flag (draggingID) inside the update closure to skip the dragged entity
antiPatterns.why: @State mutated inside a RealityKit gesture .onChanged is not reliably visible to the update closure before the next reconcile pass — the dirty/ownership state must live in the entity (a component) or the @Observable model, not transient view state.

antiPatterns.pattern: Deleting and re-adding ALL edges and ports from scratch every pass
antiPatterns.why: O(E) teardown/rebuild per observed change causes churn, flicker, and lost per-edge state, and defeats any change-detection. Edges should be diffed (add/remove/move only what changed) and their endpoints driven by live entity transforms, ideally rebuilt only when an edges-dirty flag is set.

antiPatterns.pattern: A parent card CollisionComponent (depth max(z,0.03)) that encloses its child x-badge sitting only +0.006 m in front
antiPatterns.why: The gaze ray / pinch hits the enclosing parent collider first, so the badge never receives hover or tap; its SwiftUI .hoverEffect scaleEffect never fires. Hierarchical collision/input precedence must be designed so controls have their own clearly-separated, front-facing colliders.

antiPatterns.pattern: Driving highlight only via SwiftUI .hoverEffect { scaleEffect } inside a ViewAttachmentComponent rootView
antiPatterns.why: That SwiftUI hover state does not reliably translate to a RealityKit-visible transform on the placed entity. Use a RealityKit HoverEffectComponent (highlight/spotlight) and/or an Entity.animate jitter so the effect is visible in 3D.

antiPatterns.pattern: One global gesture with targetedToAnyEntity() dispatched by parsing entity names ('ctl::role::owner', 'edge::')
antiPatterns.why: String-prefix routing is brittle and centralizes all interaction logic. visionOS 26 GestureComponent lets each entity carry its own scoped gesture, removing the parser and the name-as-protocol coupling.

antiPatterns.pattern: Hard-setting transforms to the layout-engine output with no interpolation layer
antiPatterns.why: Snaps and fights live manipulation. Separate target (model/layout) from presented (scene) and interpolate via Entity.animate or a damped follow so layout, return-to-tray, and pop-out are smooth and never overshoot.

recommendationsForOurApp: ADOPT visionOS 26 ManipulationComponent for all movable nodes/volumes/notes. Use ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(entity, hoverEffect:..., collisionShapes:...) and set releaseBehavior = .stay. Persist the new transform in ManipulationEvents.WillEnd (call viewModel.endDrag), and clamp to VolumeMetrics bounds in DidUpdateTransform by constraining the value, not replacing it. This deletes the hand-rolled DragGesture, the start-position caching, and the draggingID problem outright — grab-and-drag becomes near-trivial as the developer expects.

INVERT the reconciler from 'write everything every pass' to 'diff and write only what changed'. Maintain explicit dirty sets in the @Observable model: addedIds, removedIds, transformDirtyIds (model-driven moves only), and an edgesDirty flag. In update: add new attachments, remove gone ones, and for transformDirtyIds animate the entity toward the target via Entity.animate(...). Never touch an entity currently owned by ManipulationComponent (check for an in-progress manipulation / a per-entity 'manipulating' component flag).

MOVE transform-ownership state out of view @State and into the entity/model. The single source of truth for 'who owns this transform right now' should be a RealityKit component on the entity (e.g. a small TransformOwner component: .model / .gesture / .animating) or an @Observable model field — never SwiftUI @State, which the failed draggingID fix proved is not reliably visible to update mid-gesture.

SEPARATE target transform from presented transform. The SpatialLayoutEngine produces TARGETS; the scene interpolates to them via Entity.animate (built-in) or a frame-rate-independent critically-damped follow. This makes auto-layout, return-to-tray, and pop-out animate smoothly and structurally removes any fight between layout output and live manipulation.

FIX the x-control with proper hierarchical collision + per-entity input. Give the badge its own collider clearly in FRONT of the card (enough z-separation that the card's collider does not enclose it, or shrink the card collider so it excludes the control region), attach a RealityKit HoverEffectComponent (.highlight or .spotlight) for visible hover, add an Entity.animate scale 'jitter' on WillBegin/hover for the playful feel, and route its tap via its own GestureComponent instead of name-parsing.

REPLACE name-prefix gesture dispatch with GestureComponent per entity. Cards get a tap-to-edit gesture; the x-badge gets a tap-to-dismiss; ports/edges get none (or their own). This removes the 'ctl::'/'edge::' string protocol and the central tapGesture/dragGesture switch, scaling cleanly as new entity kinds and controls are added.

REBUILD edges incrementally and as a managed sub-graph. Key each edge by a stable composite id (sourceId+targetId+kind), diff against existing edge entities (add/remove/reposition only deltas), and update endpoints from live entity transforms each frame only while an endpoint is moving — gate full rebuild behind edgesDirty. Avoid full teardown/re-add.

KEEP the locked paradigm and the pure layout engine; this is purely a client interaction-architecture change. Continue SwiftUI-renders-content via ViewAttachmentComponent (consider the imperative component form to kill the setup-race), keep the nonisolated unit-tested SpatialLayoutEngine producing targets, and verify with xcodebuild -scheme TaskPlannerVision -destination 'generic/platform=visionOS Simulator' (trust xcodebuild over SourceKit). Add Swift unit tests for the new dirty-diff reducer (pure function: previous scene state + new model -> mutation list) so the reconciler logic is testable without the simulator.

CONSIDER a small ECS-style System for continuous work (edge endpoint following, hover jitter, damped transform follow) instead of doing it in the update closure — Apple and the visionOS 26 AGENTS guidance both push continuous behavior into custom Systems, keeping update for discrete structural changes only.

research.topic: visionOS/RealityKit gestures and direct object manipulation (grab-and-drag, ManipulationComponent, hover/highlight, coordinate spaces)
research.summary: visionOS offers two paths for moving entities: hand-rolled SwiftUI gestures targeted to RealityKit entities (visionOS 1.0+), and the new visionOS 26 ManipulationComponent / .manipulable that bundle move/rotate/scale/handoff. Both require entities to carry InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent. The single biggest lesson for our app is that drag must be done by converting the gesture TRANSLATION DELTA (value.translation3D) added to a transform captured on the first onChanged event, never by teleporting to value.location3D (which is the pinch point on the surface, not the origin, and is the documented cause of grab-time snapping). Our drag snap-back (bug #1) is fundamentally a transform-OWNERSHIP problem: the reconcile pass re-asserts the stale stored position every RealityView update, which Apple explicitly warns against — observed entity state must not be mutated in the update closure, and an entity under active gesture or animation must be exempt from reconcile via an explicit ownership flag/component, not a view @State the update closure cannot observe in time. Our x-control hover failure (bug #2) is because a SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside an attachment does not drive RealityKit-visible highlight and the parent card's collision box encloses the child control; the fix is a real RealityKit HoverEffectComponent on the control with its own visionOS 26 GroupID (to decouple from parent hover propagation) and its own non-occluded collider. visionOS 26 also adds GestureComponent (per-entity gestures, eliminating our name-prefix string dispatch), the CoordinateSpace3D protocol for unified SwiftUI/RealityKit conversion, and Entity.animate/content.animate spring animations that let transitions and the desired playful "jitter" own the transform cleanly. Indirect gaze+pinch is the comfortable primary input model and pairs naturally with translation-delta drag.

corePrinciples.principle: Transform ownership must be explicit: at any instant exactly one authority (data model, live gesture, or animation) owns an entity's transform
corePrinciples.detail: Our reconcile re-asserts the data-model position every RealityView update pass, fighting the live gesture (whose new position isn't written until release). Apple explicitly warns: do NOT modify observed entity state inside the update closure (it causes a feedback loop). The fix is an ownership handshake: when a drag/animation begins, mark the entity as owned (a custom component or a Set on the view-model that reconcile reads); reconcile then SKIPS owned entities entirely; on release the gesture writes the final transform to the model and relinquishes ownership. A view @State set inside a RealityKit gesture .onChanged is NOT reliably visible to the next update pass — ownership must live in observed model state or an entity component, never view @State.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 Session 274 'Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit' (Avoiding Infinite Loops / safe places to modify observed state)

corePrinciples.principle: Drag by converted TRANSLATION DELTA from a stored start transform, never teleport to location3D
corePrinciples.detail: value.location3D is the gesture point ON the entity (where the pinch landed), not the entity origin — assigning it directly makes the entity jump/snap on grab. Robust recipe: on first onChanged capture sourceTransform = entity.transform; then entity.position = sourceTransform.translation + value.convert(value.translation3D, from: .local, to: entity.parent!); clear sourceTransform on .onEnded (and there persist to SpatialEntity). This verbatim pattern (from simonbs/ImmersiveMoveAndRotate) is exactly what our code is missing.
corePrinciples.source: simonbs/ImmersiveMoveAndRotate EntityMovementViewModifier.swift; Step Into Vision 'Drag Gesture' (documents the location3D snap caveat)

corePrinciples.principle: Hover/highlight must be a RealityKit HoverEffectComponent, and hierarchy is controlled by GroupID
corePrinciples.detail: A SwiftUI .hoverEffect{} buried in an attachment's rootView does not reliably produce RealityKit-visible scaling. Use a real HoverEffectComponent on the entity. visionOS 26's GroupID controls propagation: by default child entities inherit the parent's hover; assigning distinct GroupIDs to the card vs the 'x' control decouples them so the control highlights independently. Combined with separating the control's collider from the card's box (which currently encloses the +0.006z child and swallows its input), this fixes bug #2.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 Session 287 'What's new in RealityKit' (HoverEffectComponent GroupID)

corePrinciples.principle: Animations are a transform-ownership state, not a fire-and-forget side effect
corePrinciples.detail: visionOS 26 drives RealityKit transforms with SwiftUI Animation curves via entity.animate(with:) / content.animate(with:). Transitions (snap-to-layout, return-to-tray, the playful 'jitter' on highlight) should run as animations that temporarily OWN the transform, with reconcile suppressed for that entity until the animation completes — otherwise model and animation fight. Jitter = a short spring on position/scale; Transform is implicitly animatable.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 Session 274 (SwiftUI-Driven Animation: Entity.animate / content.animate spring)

corePrinciples.principle: Gesture-targetable entities need BOTH InputTargetComponent and CollisionComponent; coordinate spaces differ across frameworks
corePrinciples.detail: Every targetable entity needs InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent (ManipulationComponent.configureEntity adds these + HoverEffect automatically). RealityKit is meters, y-up, +z toward the user; SwiftUI gesture values are points with inverted y, so convert() is mandatory. visionOS 26 unifies this via the CoordinateSpace3D protocol (Entity and Scene conform). InputTargetComponent.allowedInputTypes selects direct (reach-out) vs indirect (gaze+pinch).
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 Session 274; Apple 'Transforming RealityKit entities using gestures'; WWDC24 Session 10094

corePrinciples.principle: Indirect (gaze+pinch) is the primary, comfortable input model — design for amplified small movements
corePrinciples.detail: Indirect input (look + pinch at a distance) is the default and most comfortable visionOS modality because small hand motions are amplified into large actions; direct input (reach out and touch) suits nearby objects. Translation-delta drag naturally supports indirect input; teleport-to-location does not. Apps cannot read gaze directly (privacy), so the hover highlight is the only cue of what will receive the pinch — making a working HoverEffectComponent functionally important, not cosmetic.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC24 Session 10094 'Explore game input in visionOS'; Unity PolySpatial visionOS Hover Effects docs (gaze-privacy rationale)

keyAPIs.name: DragGesture().targetedToAnyEntity() + EntityTargetValue
keyAPIs.purpose: Hand-rolled, fully-controllable drag. EntityTargetValue gives value.entity (hit entity), value.translation3D, value.location3D, and value.convert(_:from:to:) for coordinate conversion.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Use as our PRIMARY drag mechanism for cards. Gives total control over clamping to VolumeMetrics bounds, layout-engine snap, and persist-on-release to SpatialEntity — control ManipulationComponent's opinionated behavior does not expose.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Requires InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent. Use value.translation3D (delta) + stored start transform, NOT value.location3D (teleport = snap). .targetedToAnyEntity() serves all entities via one gesture; disambiguate by value.entity identity (entity reference / component lookup), far more robust than name-prefix string parsing.

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationComponent + ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(_:collisionShapes:hoverEffect:allowedInputTypes:)
keyAPIs.purpose: One component providing move (one hand), rotate (one/two hands), scale (two-hand pinch-drag), and hand-to-hand passing, with built-in hover and audio. configureEntity also adds InputTarget, Collision, and HoverEffect components.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Strong candidate for workflowVolume and note entities where free 6DoF move/rotate/scale is desirable. Less ideal for taskNode/stepNode cards that must clamp to bounds and snap to type-cluster slots, since its translation/clamping is internal.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Default releaseBehavior = .reset animates back to origin (a built-in snap-back). Set releaseBehavior = .stay to persist, then write the final transform to SpatialEntity in a ManipulationEvents.WillEnd handler. Set audioConfiguration = .none to suppress default sounds.

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationEvents (WillBegin, DidUpdateTransform, WillRelease, WillEnd, DidHandOff)
keyAPIs.purpose: Lifecycle hooks for ManipulationComponent; subscribe via content.subscribe(to: ManipulationEvents.WillBegin.self){...}. WillBegin=picked up, WillEnd=at rest after release, DidUpdateTransform=per-frame, DidHandOff=passed between hands.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Use WillBegin to claim transform ownership (suppress reconcile for that entity) and WillEnd to release ownership and persist the final transform via tRPC — the ManipulationComponent equivalent of our onChanged/onEnded ownership handshake.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Canonical physics handoff: WillBegin sets PhysicsBodyComponent.mode = .kinematic, WillEnd sets .dynamic — only relevant if we add gravity/drop physics (likely unnecessary for clamped cards).

keyAPIs.name: GestureComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: Attaches a SwiftUI gesture directly to a RealityKit entity (entity.components.set(GestureComponent(TapGesture().onEnded{...}))) instead of a top-level .gesture. Gesture values are reported in the entity's coordinate space by default.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Cleanest way to give each entity (card, x-control, volume) its OWN gestures, replacing our single top-level gesture + name-prefix dispatch. Per-entity gestures give per-entity hit-testing and remove the disambiguation hack.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Still requires InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent (Apple's explicit pro tip). Reports in entity-local space, so convert when writing scene/parent positions.

keyAPIs.name: .manipulable(transform:coordinateSpace:operations:inertia:isEnabled:) SwiftUI modifier
keyAPIs.purpose: SwiftUI-side manipulation for views presenting 3D content (notably Model3D). With a transform: binding it persists ('stays where the user leaves it'); operations restricts to [.translation,.primaryRotation,.secondaryRotation,.scale]; inertia adds weight; event closure exposes .active/.ended phases.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Limited use for us: docs note it targets Model3D and that other views get clipped by window/volume bounds. Our cards are SwiftUI rendered as RealityKit attachments, so RealityKit-side ManipulationComponent or hand-rolled gestures fit better.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Default (no transform binding) auto-resets on release — same reset-vs-persist trap. Clipping makes it unsuitable for free-roaming cards in a volume.

keyAPIs.name: HoverEffectComponent (with GroupID) + InputTargetComponent.allowedInputTypes
keyAPIs.purpose: HoverEffectComponent gives system-rendered highlight (e.g. .spotlight) on gaze/hover. visionOS 26 GroupID controls hierarchy propagation (default: children inherit parent; distinct GroupIDs decouple). allowedInputTypes selects direct/indirect/all.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Use a real HoverEffectComponent on cards and controls instead of SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside attachments. Give the x-control a distinct GroupID from its parent card so it highlights independently — direct fix for bug #2. Pair with a jitter spring via Entity.animate.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Works only if the entity has its own non-occluded CollisionComponent. The parent card's collision box (depth max(z,0.03)) encloses the child x at +0.006z and swallows input — offset the control outward, shrink the parent box, or use input-target precedence so the child is independently hittable.

antiPatterns.pattern: Re-asserting the data-model transform every RealityView update pass (our reconcile re-sets card.position each pass)
antiPatterns.why: Apple explicitly warns against modifying observed entity state inside the update closure — it creates a feedback loop and fights any concurrent gesture/animation. While a gesture writes the transform, reconcile overwrites it with the stale stored position, producing the jerky snap-back. Reconcile must skip entities whose transform is currently owned by a gesture/animation (via an explicit ownership flag/component in observed state), not via a view @State the update closure cannot see in time.

antiPatterns.pattern: Setting entity.position = value.convert(value.location3D, ...) (teleport-to-location drag)
antiPatterns.why: location3D is the pinch point on the entity surface, not its origin, so the entity jumps to the cursor on grab. Documented snap cause. Use translation3D delta + stored start transform instead.

antiPatterns.pattern: Disambiguating gesture targets by parsing entity names ('ctl::role::ownerId', 'edge::')
antiPatterns.why: Stringly-typed dispatch is brittle and will not scale. Replace with per-entity GestureComponent (visionOS 26) or component-based identity (a custom EntityRole/EntityRef component read via value.entity.components[...]), giving type-safe routing and per-entity hit-testing.

antiPatterns.pattern: SwiftUI .hoverEffect{ scaleEffect } inside a ViewAttachmentComponent rootView to highlight a 3D control
antiPatterns.why: The attachment's SwiftUI hover state does not drive RealityKit-visible transform changes reliably, and the parent card's collision swallows the child's hover. Use a RealityKit HoverEffectComponent on the control entity with its own GroupID and its own non-occluded collider.

antiPatterns.pattern: Rebuilding ALL edges/ports and the x-control from scratch every reconcile pass
antiPatterns.why: Delete-and-re-add every frame thrashes the entity graph, invalidates collision/hover state mid-interaction, and is O(n) churn that won't scale. Edges/controls should be diffed and updated in place (move endpoints, toggle visibility) by a custom RealityKit System that updates edge endpoints from live entity positions each frame, not by recreating entities.

antiPatterns.pattern: Leaving ManipulationComponent / .manipulable at default releaseBehavior (.reset)
antiPatterns.why: Default behavior animates the object back to its origin on release — a built-in snap-back. If we adopt ManipulationComponent we MUST set releaseBehavior = .stay (or bind a transform) and persist via WillEnd, or we reproduce our current bug through the framework.

recommendationsForOurApp: Adopt an explicit transform-ownership model. Add an ownership authority per entity (e.g. an InteractionState component or an observed Set<EntityID> on the view-model: .model / .gesture(id) / .animating). reconcile() reads it and SKIPS any entity not owned by .model. Drag onChanged sets ownership to .gesture; onEnded writes the final transform to SpatialEntity via tRPC and returns ownership to .model. This is the root fix for bug #1 and replaces the non-working view @State draggingID guard.

Rewrite card drag with the canonical delta recipe: capture sourceTransform on first onChanged, set position = sourceTransform.translation + value.convert(value.translation3D, from: .local, to: entity.parent!), clamp to VolumeMetrics, persist on onEnded. Never use value.location3D. This makes grab-and-drag the near-trivial operation the developer expects.

Fix the x-control hover (bug #2) with a real RealityKit HoverEffectComponent on the control entity, give it a distinct visionOS 26 HoverEffectComponent GroupID so it does not inherit/share the card's hover, and stop relying on SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside the attachment. Ensure the control has its own collider that is not enclosed by the parent card's collision box (offset it on +z beyond the card depth, or shrink the card collider, or set input-target precedence).

Migrate from one top-level gesture + entity-name string parsing to per-entity GestureComponent (visionOS 26) and component-based identity (a typed EntityRole component). This removes the brittle 'ctl::'/'edge::' parsing and gives type-safe, per-entity hit testing that scales as kinds grow.

Make all motion animation-owned: use entity.animate(with: .spring(...)) for layout snaps, return-to-tray, and the playful highlight 'jitter', and suppress reconcile for the entity for the animation's duration. This delivers the smooth-animation and jitter requirements without model/animation conflict.

Stop delete-and-re-adding edges/ports and controls every reconcile pass. Create them once and update them in place via a small custom RealityKit System that recomputes edge endpoints from live entity positions each frame; reconcile should diff (add/remove/show-hide) rather than rebuild. This is required for the system to scale.

Evaluate ManipulationComponent specifically for workflowVolume and note entities (free 6DoF feel, hand-to-hand passing) while keeping the hand-rolled translation-delta drag for taskNode/stepNode cards that must clamp and snap to layout slots. If ManipulationComponent is used anywhere, set releaseBehavior = .stay and persist in WillEnd. Both approaches must share the same ownership model so reconcile treats them uniformly.

Default interactive entities to indirect input (gaze+pinch) via InputTargetComponent.allowedInputTypes since it is the comfortable primary visionOS modality, and treat the working hover highlight as functionally required (the only legal gaze-target feedback), not decorative — important for the neurodivergent/ADHD audience that benefits from clear affordances.
