research.topic: Hover / Highlight / Focus / Selection feedback in visionOS & RealityKit (with direct fixes for the "x doesn't highlight" bug and drag snap-back)
research.summary: visionOS draws a hard line between TWO completely different feedback systems that our code currently conflates. (1) System gaze hover: when an entity has InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent + HoverEffectComponent, the OS highlights it on gaze entirely OUTSIDE the app process for privacy — the app is never told what is being looked at, so app code can never read a live "is hovered" boolean and use it to drive a transform. (2) App selection state: app-driven highlight (e.g. our isPicked merge/link selection) which the app fully controls and can animate. Our "x doesn't highlight" bug has two root causes confirmed by primary sources: first, the parent card's CollisionComponent (depth max(z,0.03)) encloses the ×-badge child which sits only +0.006 m forward, and per a RealityKit engineer "HoverEffectComponent only affects its Entity and won't affect its children" — so hover does not naturally cascade and the parent's larger collision wins the gaze/hit-test, swallowing the child; second, the SwiftUI .hoverEffect{ scaleEffect } inside the ViewAttachmentComponent rootView is applied by the system to the SwiftUI layer and (in our manual-component setup) is not reliably activated because the badge entity's own hover/collision is dominated by the parent. visionOS 2 fixed the cascade ambiguity with HoverEffectComponent.GroupID (shared/independent activation regardless of hierarchy), and visionOS 26 introduced ManipulationComponent — which is the real foundation fix for BOTH the snap-back and the hover bugs: configureEntity adds collision+input-target+hover for you, releaseBehavior=.stay keeps the entity where the user dropped it (no snap-back), and the system OWNS the transform during manipulation so reconcile can never fight a live drag. Custom playful effects (glow/jitter/scale) that respond to gaze must be done via a ShaderGraphMaterial HoverState node (.shader style), because that is the only path where gaze drives a RealityKit-visible material/geometry change without the app learning hover. WWDC25's GestureComponent + ViewAttachmentComponent + observable transforms let us replace the entire name-parsing reconcile loop with per-entity gesture ownership.

corePrinciples.principle: Gaze hover is applied by the system OUTSIDE the app process — the app is NEVER told what is hovered
corePrinciples.detail: Custom hover effects 'were designed from the ground up to preserve privacy, as they're applied by the system outside the app process without requiring extra entitlements.' Consequence for us: you cannot read a live hover boolean in Swift and use it to move a RealityKit entity. Any gaze-reactive visual must be declared up front (HoverEffectComponent style, or a SwiftUI .hoverEffect closure, or a shader HoverState node) and the SYSTEM evaluates/animates it. This is exactly why a hand-rolled 'on hover, set entity.scale' design is impossible and why our intuition keeps failing.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC24 'Create custom hover effects in visionOS' (Session 10152); WWDC24 'Discover RealityKit APIs' (Session 10103)

corePrinciples.principle: HoverEffectComponent requires the trio AND only affects its own entity, not children
corePrinciples.detail: An Apple Graphics/Games engineer: 'HoverEffectComponent ... only affect[s] its Entity and won't affect its children.' Hover detection also requires InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent on the SAME entity (confirmed twice on the dev forums). So a child × badge needs its OWN hover+input+collision, AND must not be eclipsed by the parent's collision volume.
corePrinciples.source: Apple Developer Forums thread 732863 and thread 761151

corePrinciples.principle: HoverEffectComponent.GroupID (visionOS 2) controls cascade — without it hover propagates ambiguously
corePrinciples.detail: 'GroupIDs are a way to create associations between hover effects. Any hover effects that share a GroupID will also share activations.' And: 'Normally hover effects are applied hierarchically where child entities inherit the effects of their parent; if an entity has a GroupID, then they will not propagate their effects to their children.' This is the precedence/isolation knob to make the parent card and the × badge each light up independently.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC24 'Discover RealityKit APIs' (Session 10103); Unity PolySpatial GroupID discussion

corePrinciples.principle: Three built-in HoverEffectComponent styles: spotlight, highlight, shader — only shader can do custom glow/jitter
corePrinciples.detail: spotlight (default): subtle gaze-following spotlight, tint+strength configurable. highlight: uniform tint over the whole mesh (HighlightHoverEffectStyle(color:strength:)). shader: drives a ShaderGraphMaterial HoverState node whose 'intensity value is animated from 0 to 1 when the player looks at the entity' — the ONLY built-in path to a gaze-reactive glow/scale/jitter because a GeometryModifier or surface shader reads that intensity. Spotlight/highlight cannot scale or jitter geometry.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC24 Session 10103; HoverState in RealityView forum 761151; Reality Composer Pro materials WWDC23 Session 10202

corePrinciples.principle: ManipulationComponent (visionOS 26) is the transform-ownership foundation: the SYSTEM owns the transform during a drag
corePrinciples.detail: ManipulationComponent.configureEntity() 'will set up the subject with collision, assign the Input Target Component and the default Hover Effect.' During manipulation the system drives the entity transform; releaseBehavior=.stay leaves it at the dropped transform (default .reset snaps it back); persist the final transform in the ManipulationEvents.WillEnd handler. This structurally eliminates the 'reconcile re-asserts stored position mid-drag' snap-back because the app does not own the transform while the user is holding the entity, and the persisted value is written exactly when the gesture ends.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 'What's new in RealityKit' (Session 287); 'Deep Dive into Manipulation on visionOS' (Step Into Vision)

corePrinciples.principle: SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside an attachment affects the SwiftUI layer, not a RealityKit entity transform
corePrinciples.detail: The closure form `.hoverEffect { effect, isActive, proxy in effect.scaleEffect(isActive ? 1.3 : 1.0) }` is a SwiftUI-layer effect the system applies to the rendered view; it CAN scale the attachment's rendered content, but it is gated by that view actually receiving system hover. In our setup the badge entity's gaze/hit is swallowed by the parent card's oversized collision, so isActive effectively never flips. The effect itself is correct SwiftUI; the problem is upstream input/collision precedence, not the modifier.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC24 Session 10152; createwithswift 'Creating advanced hover effects in visionOS'

corePrinciples.principle: App selection state is separate from system hover and is fully app-owned
corePrinciples.detail: Our merge/link 'isPicked' highlight (NodeCardView isHighlighted) is correct as an APP-state visual — it is not hover, it is selection, and the app legitimately controls it and can animate it with SwiftUI .animation. Keep these two concepts in separate code paths: HoverEffectComponent/.hoverEffect for gaze feedback; explicit @Observable selection flags for selection feedback. Never try to read system hover to compute selection.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC24 Session 10152 (hover is system/privacy-applied); general SwiftUI state model

keyAPIs.name: HoverEffectComponent(.spotlight/.highlight/.shader)
keyAPIs.purpose: Declarative gaze-hover highlight on an entity; system-applied, privacy-preserving
keyAPIs.whenToUse: On every interactive entity (card, badge, port) that should light up on gaze. Use .highlight(HighlightHoverEffectStyle(color:strength:)) for a tinted card glow; .shader(.default) when a ShaderGraphMaterial HoverState node drives a custom glow/jitter.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Requires InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent on the same entity. Only affects its own entity, not children. Default plain HoverEffectComponent() = spotlight. Cannot scale/move geometry except via the shader style.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 1.0 (component + spotlight/highlight); .shader style and GroupID added visionOS 2.0

keyAPIs.name: HoverEffectComponent.GroupID
keyAPIs.purpose: Associate or isolate hover activations across entities regardless of parent/child hierarchy
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Give the parent card and the × badge DIFFERENT GroupIDs so each activates independently (fixes the × not highlighting because the parent swallows it); or the SAME GroupID to make a card + its ports glow together.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Must be set on all participating entities. Without it, hierarchical propagation is ambiguous and effects can leak globally.

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationComponent + ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(_:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Built-in pick-up/move/rotate/scale of an entity; the system owns the transform during the gesture
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Replace our hand-rolled translation DragGesture for moving cards. configureEntity auto-adds CollisionComponent + InputTargetComponent + default HoverEffectComponent. This is THE fix for drag snap-back.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Set releaseBehavior=.stay (default .reset snaps the entity back to its origin on release — likely a second contributor to our snap-back). Tune dynamics (translation/rotation/scaling/inertia). Persist the final transform in the WillEnd event, not per-frame.

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationEvents (WillBegin / DidUpdateTransform / WillRelease / WillEnd / DidHandOff)
keyAPIs.purpose: Lifecycle hooks around a manipulation to constrain transforms and persist on release
keyAPIs.whenToUse: DidUpdateTransform: clamp to VolumeMetrics bounds per frame (read/modify event.entity.transform). WillEnd: write the final x/y/z + quaternion to the view model / tRPC (replaces our endDrag-on-DragGesture.onEnded).
keyAPIs.gotchas: Constrain in DidUpdateTransform; do NOT persist every frame. WillBegin/WillRelease are the right place to mute/restore a hover effect if needed.

keyAPIs.name: GestureComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: Attach a specific SwiftUI gesture (TapGesture/DragGesture) directly to a single entity
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Replace the two top-level .targetedToAnyEntity() gestures + the 'ctl::/edge::' name-parsing disambiguation. Put a TapGesture on each card (open edit), a separate gesture on the × badge (dismiss), etc. — ownership becomes explicit per entity.
keyAPIs.gotchas: visionOS 26 only. Removes the fragile string-prefix routing; each entity declares its own behavior. Combine with ManipulationComponent for move + tap.

keyAPIs.name: SwiftUI .hoverEffect(_:in:isEnabled:) closure form
keyAPIs.purpose: Custom system-applied hover effect on a SwiftUI view (incl. RealityView attachments & ornaments)
keyAPIs.whenToUse: For attachment-rendered controls (the × badge's SwiftUI content) to scale/clip/opacity on gaze: `.hoverEffect { effect, isActive, proxy in effect.scaleEffect(isActive ? 1.2 : 1.0) }`. Use .hoverEffectGroup to group/isolate within the view tree.
keyAPIs.gotchas: isActive is evaluated by the SYSTEM, not delivered to your logic; you only declare the mapping. The effect only fires if that view actually receives system hover — which fails when a parent RealityKit collision eclipses the attachment entity. Affects the SwiftUI layer, not the RealityKit entity transform.

keyAPIs.name: ShaderGraphMaterial + HoverState node (Reality Composer Pro)
keyAPIs.purpose: Drive a gaze-reactive glow/scale/jitter on geometry via the shader, paired with HoverEffectComponent(.shader)
keyAPIs.whenToUse: For the developer's wanted 'playful jitter / glow on highlight' on the 3D card mesh itself (not the SwiftUI content). HoverState.intensity (0→1 on gaze) feeds a Mix into emissive (glow) or a GeometryModifier (scale/jitter).
keyAPIs.gotchas: Requires building the material in Reality Composer Pro; surface shader = PBR/emissive changes, GeometryModifier = vertex/scale changes. Still needs InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent. Our cards are SwiftUI attachments (flat quads), so shader-jitter applies to a mesh card, not the attachment's SwiftUI text.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 1.0 (ShaderGraphMaterial); HoverState node usable with .shader hover style visionOS 2.0

keyAPIs.name: InputTargetComponent (.isEnabled) + CollisionComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: Make an entity hit-testable for gaze/pinch and define the precise input region
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Required prerequisites for ANY hover/tap/drag. Set isEnabled=false to temporarily make an entity transparent to input. Size the collision to the actual quad — and INSET the parent card's collision (or reduce its z-depth) so it does not enclose the forward-offset × badge.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Our parent collision uses depth max(z,0.03) and the badge sits only +0.006 m forward — the parent box swallows the badge. Shrink parent z-depth, push the badge further forward, or give them distinct GroupIDs / separate input ownership.

antiPatterns.pattern: Reading or inferring a live 'is hovered' boolean in app code to drive an entity transform
antiPatterns.why: Gaze hover is applied by the system outside the app process for privacy; the app is never told what is hovered. Any design that branches on hover in Swift to move/scale an entity cannot work. Declare the effect (HoverEffectComponent style / .hoverEffect closure / shader HoverState) and let the system animate it.

antiPatterns.pattern: A single reconcile(content:) re-asserting card.position = storedPosition on EVERY update pass, including mid-drag
antiPatterns.why: This is the literal root cause of snap-back: the stored VM position is stale until release, so every observed change yanks the card back. The @State draggingID skip is unreliable because @State mutated in a RealityKit gesture .onChanged isn't visible to the update closure in time. Fix by giving the transform a single owner: ManipulationComponent (system owns it during drag; .stay keeps it; persist in WillEnd).

antiPatterns.pattern: Wrapping a child × badge in the parent card's collision volume (parent depth max(z,0.03), child at +0.006 z)
antiPatterns.why: Hover/hit-testing of the badge is eclipsed by the larger parent collision, and HoverEffectComponent does not cascade to children — so the badge never gets its own gaze highlight. Inset/shrink the parent collision, push the badge forward, and/or assign distinct HoverEffectComponent.GroupIDs.

antiPatterns.pattern: Routing all gestures through two top-level .targetedToAnyEntity() handlers + parsing entity names ('ctl::role::owner', 'edge::')
antiPatterns.why: Fragile, unscalable disambiguation that hard-codes roles into strings and forces every new interactive element to extend a central parser. visionOS 26 GestureComponent attaches the right gesture to the right entity directly, making ownership explicit and grab-and-drag near-trivial.

antiPatterns.pattern: Deleting and re-adding ALL edges + ports from scratch on every reconcile pass
antiPatterns.why: Wasteful churn that also re-triggers @Observable invalidation and contributes to the feedback loops; edges should be persistent entities whose endpoints are updated (or driven by an ECS System) rather than destroyed/rebuilt each frame.

antiPatterns.pattern: Using .spotlight or .highlight hover style to try to achieve scale/jitter/glow
antiPatterns.why: Those styles only tint/spotlight the existing mesh; they cannot scale or deform geometry. Playful jitter/glow on gaze requires the .shader style with a ShaderGraphMaterial HoverState node (GeometryModifier for scale, emissive Mix for glow).

recommendationsForOurApp: Adopt ManipulationComponent (visionOS 26) for moving cards instead of the hand-rolled translation DragGesture. Call ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(card) once when the card is created (it adds collision + input-target + default hover), set releaseBehavior=.stay, and persist the final transform in the ManipulationEvents.WillEnd subscription (calling viewModel.endDrag). This makes the system own the transform during a drag, so the reconcile loop can no longer fight it — eliminating snap-back at the root rather than via the unreliable @State draggingID skip.

Stop re-asserting card.position every reconcile pass. Establish single transform ownership: layout engine sets position only when an entity is first placed or when auto-flow explicitly re-lays-out (hand-moved entities already excluded); after that, the entity transform is owned by ManipulationComponent during drag and by the persisted value otherwise. Reconcile should add/remove entities and update content, not re-stamp positions of already-placed, user-movable entities every frame.

Fix the × badge hover with three combined changes: (a) shrink the parent card's CollisionComponent z-depth and inset it so it does not enclose the badge; (b) push the badge further forward than +0.006 m; (c) give the parent card and the badge DIFFERENT HoverEffectComponent.GroupIDs so the badge activates independently (hover does not cascade to children, so the badge must have its own InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent + HoverEffectComponent — which it already does — plus an isolating GroupID).

Replace the name-parsing gesture router with per-entity GestureComponent (visionOS 26): a TapGesture on the card (open edit / toggle volume), a separate TapGesture on the × badge (dismiss), ManipulationComponent for move. This removes the 'ctl::'/'edge::' string parsing and makes each new interactive element self-contained — directly serving the developer's 'should scale as the UI grows' goal.

For gaze feedback on cards, prefer the built-in HoverEffectComponent(.highlight(HighlightHoverEffectStyle(color: typeTint, strength:))) for an elegant tinted glow that matches the type color, since it is battle-tested and free. Reserve the .shader style + ShaderGraphMaterial HoverState node for the playful jitter/glow the developer wants — but note our cards are SwiftUI attachments (flat quads), so shader jitter must target a backing mesh, while SwiftUI-layer scale can be done with the .hoverEffect closure inside the attachment rootView (once the badge/card actually receives system hover).

Keep system hover and app selection strictly separate. The merge/link 'isPicked' highlight (NodeCardView.isHighlighted) is app selection state — correct as-is, and it can be animated with SwiftUI .animation. Do not attempt to derive selection from hover. Document this two-system split in .claude/rules/ so future sessions don't re-conflate them.

Validate the .shader/HoverState path early: per forum 761151, HoverEffectComponent(.shader(.default)) shows nothing unless InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent are also present. Add a small spike before committing to shader-based effects, since it requires authoring a ShaderGraphMaterial in Reality Composer Pro (heavier than the declarative .highlight style).

research.topic: Collision and hit-testing hierarchy in RealityKit on visionOS (CollisionComponent, InputTargetComponent, CollisionGroup/Filter, raycast, hover) — concrete recipe for an independently hit-testable card + child "x"
research.summary: In RealityKit on visionOS, an entity is hit-testable for gestures only if it has BOTH a CollisionComponent (defines the hit region) and an InputTargetComponent (marks it input-capable); HoverEffectComponent adds gaze feedback. Hit-test resolution among nested/overlapping entities is decided by collision geometry and depth: when a parent's collision shape fully encloses a child's shape and the parent also has an InputTargetComponent, the parent is hit FIRST and the child becomes unreachable. This is the exact, Apple-DTS-confirmed root cause of bug #2: the card's collision box (depth max(z,0.03), ~3cm) swallows the "x" child placed only +0.006m (6mm) in front of it, so the child never receives hover or tap — and HoverEffectComponent does not propagate to children, while the SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside the attachment is correct but never fires because the system never hit-tests the child. The fix is hierarchical: make the parent NOT input-targetable in the region where the child lives (use a precisely-sized/inset collision shape, push the child out in front by a real gap of >=2-3cm so it sits clear of the parent box, or disable the parent's InputTargetComponent and decompose the card's own hit area into child sub-targets), plus use CollisionGroup/CollisionFilter masks to route gestures only to intended categories. Bug #1 (drag snap-back) is a transform-ownership problem rooted in the reconcile-in-update pattern: writing entity.position every update pass while only persisting on release means the stored value is stale during the drag, and RealityView's update closure re-asserts it. visionOS 26 provides first-class solutions: ManipulationComponent (handles grab/drag/persist natively with releaseBehavior=.stay), GestureComponent (per-entity gestures with correct local targeting), Observable entities + content.animate()/Entity.animate() for smooth SwiftUI-driven transforms, and ViewAttachmentComponent for component-based attachments — all of which let the developer stop hand-rolling the reconcile/transform-ownership engine. scene.raycast(origin:direction:length:query:mask:) with CollisionGroup masks gives precise, filtered hit queries when manual hit-testing is needed; many colliders are fine if shapes are simple boxes/spheres and you avoid per-frame shape regeneration (the recursive visualBounds feedback-loop trap).

corePrinciples.principle: Two components are mandatory and complementary: CollisionComponent + InputTargetComponent
corePrinciples.detail: An entity receives gestures ONLY if it has a CollisionComponent (its shapes define the hit region) AND an InputTargetComponent (marks it as input-capable). Missing either means no hits. HoverEffectComponent is separate and adds gaze-driven visual feedback. This is confirmed across Apple docs, the Lickability collision guide, and the stepinto SwiftUI-gestures guide.
corePrinciples.source: Apple RealityKit docs; Better together SwiftUI and RealityKit (WWDC25-274); stepinto.vision SwiftUI gestures guide. visionOS 1.0+.

corePrinciples.principle: Hit-test precedence: an enclosing parent collision shape with InputTargetComponent ALWAYS wins over its children
corePrinciples.detail: Per Apple DTS: 'If your surface has a collision shape that fully encompasses the children's collision shapes, and it also has an InputTargetComponent, it is expected that you receive hits on the surface and not its children, because the children cannot be hit, the parent would always be hit first.' Resolution is closest/frontmost by collision geometry, NOT by entity z-position alone — a parent box that spatially overlaps the child region intercepts the hit even if the child is nominally in front by a few mm.
corePrinciples.source: Apple Developer Forums thread 747256 (DTS Engineer); echoed in forum 732863. visionOS 1.0+.

corePrinciples.principle: HoverEffectComponent affects only its own entity, not children
corePrinciples.detail: Adding HoverEffectComponent to a parent does NOT highlight children. For a child control you must add HoverEffectComponent to the child entity itself AND ensure the system actually hit-tests that child (i.e. the parent must not swallow the hover). For SwiftUI content rendered via ViewAttachmentComponent, the in-view .hoverEffect{ effect, isActive, _ in effect.scaleEffect(...) } is the correct mechanism, but it only fires when the child entity receives the hover.
corePrinciples.source: Apple Developer Forums thread 732863; Create custom hover effects in visionOS (WWDC24-10152). visionOS 1.0 (component), 2.0 (highlight/shader styles).

corePrinciples.principle: Do not mutate observed entity transforms inside RealityView's update closure — this is the root of drag snap-back
corePrinciples.detail: Writing entity.position/transform in update creates a feedback/ownership conflict: any observed change re-fires update, which re-asserts a (stale-during-drag) stored position. Apple explicitly warns this risks infinite loops and says the safe places to modify observed entities are gesture closures, custom Systems, or the make closure — never blindly in update. Guard writes (only write if value actually changed) if you must.
corePrinciples.source: Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit (WWDC25-274). visionOS 26 for Observable entities; the anti-pattern applies on all versions.

corePrinciples.principle: CollisionGroup + CollisionFilter route which entities a query/gesture can hit
corePrinciples.detail: CollisionGroup is an OptionSet (bitwise, e.g. 1<<0). CollisionFilter(group:mask:) declares an entity's category and which categories it interacts with. Used with scene.raycast(..., mask:) or hitTest(..., mask:) to restrict hits to a category (e.g. only 'control' entities, or exclude edges). This is the clean way to disambiguate instead of parsing entity names.
corePrinciples.source: codingxr.markhorgan.com Interactivity in RealityKit; Apple CollisionFilter docs; Discover RealityKit APIs (WWDC24-10103). visionOS 1.0+.

corePrinciples.principle: Collision shape sizing/offset is the lever for nested hit-testability
corePrinciples.detail: ShapeResource.generateBox(size:).offsetBy(translation:) lets you size and position a hit region independent of the visual mesh. To keep a child reachable: size the parent's collision box to NOT cover the child's screen region (inset it, or cut the depth so it does not extend toward the viewer past the child), and/or move the child entity a real gap (>= 2-3cm, not 6mm) in front so its own box clears the parent box. visualBounds(recursive:false) measures only the entity's own quad — correct — but regenerating shapes every frame from visualBounds risks drift/feedback; cache the shape and only rebuild on size change.
corePrinciples.source: Apple ShapeResource docs; Lickability collision guide; current SpatialSceneView.swift analysis (depth max(z,0.03) vs child +0.006). visionOS 1.0+.

corePrinciples.principle: visionOS 26 makes grab-and-drag near-trivial via ManipulationComponent and per-entity GestureComponent
corePrinciples.detail: ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(entity, hoverEffect:, allowedInputTypes:, collisionShapes:) wires InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent + HoverEffectComponent and handles translate/rotate/scale natively, with releaseBehavior=.stay to persist position (vs .reset). GestureComponent attaches a SwiftUI gesture to ONE entity so values arrive in that entity's local space — eliminating .targetedToAnyEntity name-parsing disambiguation. Both remove the need to hand-own transforms in reconcile.
corePrinciples.source: Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit (WWDC25-274); What's new in RealityKit (WWDC25-287); stepinto.vision deep dive into manipulation. visionOS 26+.

keyAPIs.name: CollisionComponent(shapes:mode:filter:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Defines the hit-test/physics region of an entity via ShapeResource shapes. mode .default (collides) vs .trigger (events only). filter (CollisionFilter) controls group/mask routing.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: On every entity that must be hit by a gesture or raycast. Size the shapes to the intended hit region, not necessarily the visual mesh.
keyAPIs.gotchas: A parent shape that encloses a child shape intercepts the child's hits. Regenerating shapes from visualBounds every frame causes drift/feedback — cache and rebuild only on actual size change.

keyAPIs.name: InputTargetComponent (isEnabled, allowedInputTypes / InputType: .direct, .indirect, .all)
keyAPIs.purpose: Marks an entity as eligible to receive input gestures. isEnabled=false makes it pass-through (does NOT consume hits). allowedInputTypes restricts to direct (hand-touch) vs indirect (gaze+pinch) input.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Pair with CollisionComponent on anything interactive. Set isEnabled=false on a container parent so its children become the hit targets (key fix for the card/x problem).
keyAPIs.gotchas: Without it, an entity with a collision shape still gets no gestures. Disabling it on the parent is the cleanest way to let a child control win the hit test.

keyAPIs.name: HoverEffectComponent(.spotlight / .highlight / .shader)
keyAPIs.purpose: System-rendered gaze feedback. spotlight (default subtle), highlight (uniform tint over whole mesh, tint+strength configurable), shader (drives a ShaderGraphMaterial HoverState node).
keyAPIs.whenToUse: On RealityKit mesh entities for built-in highlight. For SwiftUI-attachment content (your cards/x), prefer the in-view SwiftUI .hoverEffect modifier instead, which can scale/clip/fade the rendered view.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Affects only its own entity, never children. Hover is resolved by the system out-of-process (privacy) and only fires when that entity is actually hit-tested — if the parent box swallows the hover, neither the component nor the attachment's .hoverEffect will fire.

keyAPIs.name: SwiftUI .hoverEffect { effect, isActive, proxy in ... } (CustomHoverEffect, HoverEffectGroup)
keyAPIs.purpose: Drives view-level visual changes (scaleEffect/opacity/clipShape) on gaze. Supports grouping (HoverEffectGroup) so a hover on one element animates several, and delayed/animated reveals.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Inside the SwiftUI rootView of a ViewAttachmentComponent to make cards/controls visibly react — this is the correct, locked SwiftUI-renders-content pattern. Use a HoverEffectGroup to do the playful 'jitter on highlight' across the card and its controls together.
keyAPIs.gotchas: It is purely visual and only activates when the underlying entity receives the hover hit. The current DismissBadge .hoverEffect is correct code — it fails only because the parent collision box prevents the badge entity from being hovered.

keyAPIs.name: ShapeResource.generateBox(size:)/generateSphere(radius:)/generateConvex(from:), .offsetBy(translation:rotation:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Builds collision/hit shapes decoupled from the visual mesh; offsetBy repositions/reorients the shape within the entity's local space.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: To inset or shrink a parent's hit region so it doesn't cover child controls, and to place a child control's hit box precisely. Box/sphere are cheapest; convex is heavier.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Async variants exist (generateConvex async). Offsetting must account for the entity's own pivot; verify with Reality Composer Pro 'Check Collision Shapes' or a debug wireframe.

keyAPIs.name: scene.raycast(origin:direction:length:query:mask:relativeTo:) and raycast(from:to:query:mask:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Manual ray hit-test against collision geometry. query .nearest (frontmost) or .all; mask (CollisionGroup) filters which categories are eligible. Returns CollisionCastHit with entity, position, distance, normal.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: When you need explicit, filtered hit resolution (e.g. find the frontmost 'node' under a point ignoring edges/ports) instead of relying on gesture targeting.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Only hits entities with collision shapes. Use a mask to avoid hitting decorative entities (edges/ports) — cleaner than name-prefix parsing.

keyAPIs.name: CollisionGroup (OptionSet) + CollisionFilter(group:mask:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Categorize entities and declare which categories interact. Bidirectional: both sides' masks must agree for a collision; for hit-testing a mask selects eligible groups.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Define groups like .node, .control, .edge. Put cards in .node, the x in .control, edges in .edge with empty input mask. Route gestures/raycasts by mask to disambiguate cleanly.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Easy to get the bitwise math wrong; wrap in named constants. Filter affects physics collisions too if mode=.default — use .trigger if you only want events.

keyAPIs.name: GestureComponent(_:) (per-entity SwiftUI gesture)
keyAPIs.purpose: Attaches a SwiftUI gesture directly to a single entity; values are reported in that entity's local coordinate space, removing the need for .targetedToAnyEntity + manual entity disambiguation.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Give each card its own drag/tap, and give the x its own tap. Eliminates the ctl::/edge:: name-parsing dispatch in the current code.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Still requires InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent on the entity. New API — battle-testing limited; keep RealityView-level gestures as a fallback path.

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationComponent + ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(_:hoverEffect:allowedInputTypes:collisionShapes:) and .manipulable() / ManipulationEvents
keyAPIs.purpose: Native object grab/move/rotate/scale with audio + hover, releaseBehavior=.stay to persist position. configureEntity wires Input/Collision/Hover in one call. Events: WillBegin/DidUpdateTransform/WillRelease/WillEnd/DidHandOff.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: To replace the hand-rolled DragGesture + reconcile transform ownership for moving cards. DidUpdateTransform lets you clamp to VolumeMetrics bounds; WillEnd is where you persist to the backend.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Default releaseBehavior is .reset (snaps back!) — must set .stay. A known beta bug can stop translation app-wide until device restart. Clamp inside DidUpdateTransform, not by re-asserting position in update.

keyAPIs.name: ViewAttachmentComponent(rootView:) and PresentationComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: ViewAttachmentComponent puts a SwiftUI view on an entity from anywhere (replaces the make-closure attachments builder, fixing the setup race). PresentationComponent shows SwiftUI popovers/sheets/menus anchored to an entity.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Render every card/note/control as a ViewAttachmentComponent entity. Use PresentationComponent for the edit form/menus instead of the top-level .sheet, keeping UI spatially anchored.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 26 (component-based attachments); visionOS 1.0 had builder-based attachments
keyAPIs.gotchas: Component-based attachments still need Input/Collision to be interactive. The current code adds attachments in update due to a setup race — the component form removes that race.

keyAPIs.name: content.animate{ } / Entity.animate(animation:) (SwiftUI-driven implicit animation of components)
keyAPIs.purpose: Animate Transform/Audio/Light/Model component changes using SwiftUI animation curves (.bouncy etc.) without manual timers — the proper home for smooth moves and the 'jitter' highlight.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: For return-to-tray, layout re-flow, and playful highlight jitter. Set the target transform inside the animate block; the framework interpolates.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Do not combine with re-asserting transforms in update (conflicts). On <26, fall back to FromToByAnimation / Transform-based animation controllers.

antiPatterns.pattern: Re-asserting card.position from the stored (data-model) value on EVERY RealityView update pass
antiPatterns.why: This is the direct cause of drag snap-back (bug #1). During a drag the stored value is stale (written only on release), and update re-fires on any observed change, fighting the live gesture. Transform ownership must shift to the gesture/manipulation while active. Apple explicitly warns against mutating observed entities in update (infinite-loop / ownership conflict).

antiPatterns.pattern: Trying to skip the dragged card via a @State draggingID checked inside reconcile
antiPatterns.why: @State mutated inside a RealityKit gesture .onChanged is not reliably visible to the update closure before the next reconcile pass, so the skip misses and the card still snaps. The fix is architectural (own the transform in the gesture/ManipulationComponent), not a flag check.

antiPatterns.pattern: Giving a parent card a collision box that fully encloses a child control placed only 6mm in front
antiPatterns.why: Apple DTS-confirmed: an enclosing parent with InputTargetComponent is hit first, so the child x never receives hover or tap (bug #2). max(z,0.03) depth (3cm) over a +0.006 (6mm) child guarantees the parent swallows it. Inset/shrink the parent shape, push the child out by a real gap, or disable the parent's InputTargetComponent.

antiPatterns.pattern: Adding HoverEffectComponent to the parent and expecting the child x to highlight
antiPatterns.why: HoverEffectComponent only affects its own entity, never children. The child needs its own hover mechanism (its attachment's SwiftUI .hoverEffect) AND must actually be hit-tested — which it currently never is because the parent box intercepts the hover.

antiPatterns.pattern: Disambiguating gestures by parsing entity names ('ctl::', 'edge::') with .targetedToAnyEntity()
antiPatterns.why: Fragile and unscalable. Routing should use CollisionGroup/CollisionFilter masks (and per-entity GestureComponent on visionOS 26), so edges/ports simply aren't input targets and controls are a distinct group — no string parsing, no accidental matches.

antiPatterns.pattern: Deleting and re-adding ALL edges + ports and rebuilding the x control from scratch every update pass
antiPatterns.why: Per-frame teardown/rebuild thrashes the scene, regenerates shapes from visualBounds (feedback/drift trap), and breaks any in-flight hover/animation state. Reconcile should diff and update in place; transforms should be SwiftUI-animated, not recreated.

antiPatterns.pattern: Regenerating CollisionComponent shapes from visualBounds(recursive:true) each frame
antiPatterns.why: Recursive bounds include children (controls/ports), inflating the box every pass → outward drift and a self-reinforcing feedback loop. Use recursive:false, cache the shape, and rebuild only when the card's intrinsic size actually changes.

recommendationsForOurApp: Fix bug #2 with hierarchical collision, not patches: either (a) set the card parent's InputTargetComponent.isEnabled=false and add a dedicated child 'card body' entity with its own correctly-sized collision box as the drag target, so the x child (its own Input+Collision+HoverEffect) is independently hit-tested; or (b) inset/shrink the parent's collision box (offsetBy + smaller extents) so it does not cover the x's screen region, and push the x out by a real >=0.02-0.03m gap, not 0.006m.

Route gestures by CollisionGroup/CollisionFilter instead of name parsing: define groups .node, .control, .edge. Cards in .node, x/ports controls in .control, edge lines in .edge with no InputTargetComponent. Then a TapGesture masked to .control hits only controls; drag masked to .node hits only cards. Delete the ctl::/edge:: string-parsing dispatch.

Fix bug #1 by moving transform ownership off the data model during interaction. Two viable paths: (Preferred, visionOS 26) replace the hand-rolled DragGesture with ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(card, collisionShapes:[...]) and releaseBehavior=.stay; clamp to VolumeMetrics in a DidUpdateTransform subscription; persist in WillEnd. (Or) keep a custom gesture but STOP writing position in reconcile — let the gesture own the live transform and only reconcile entities that are not the active drag target, tracked via an @Observable viewModel.activeDragID (observable, not @State) so update sees it.

Make reconcile diff-and-update-in-place, not teardown-and-rebuild. Keep a stable id->entity map; on update only add new entities, remove gone ones, and write transforms only for entities whose model position changed AND that are not being dragged/animated. Never rebuild edges/ports/x wholesale each pass — update their endpoints in place.

Stop regenerating collision shapes every frame. Compute the card's collision box once from visualBounds(recursive:false) when its size is first known (or changes), cache it on the entity, and reuse — eliminating the visualBounds feedback/drift loop the memory notes flag as an open bug.

Use the SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside attachment rootViews as the single highlight mechanism (the DismissBadge code is already correct). For the playful 'jitter on highlight', wrap card + its controls in a HoverEffectGroup so they animate together, and use Entity.animate(animation: .bouncy) for spatial jitter on the entity transform. This honors the locked 'SwiftUI renders content' rule.

Adopt the visionOS 26 component-based stack to delete the foundation cruft: ViewAttachmentComponent (removes the make/update setup-race for attachments), GestureComponent (per-entity gestures in local space, removes .targetedToAnyEntity disambiguation), and content.animate/Entity.animate (removes manual transform stepping). Target is visionOS 26.2 so all are in scope.

Make activeDragID / animation state @Observable on the view model (not SwiftUI @State) so the RealityView update closure reliably observes it — this is the specific reason the prior draggingID skip failed.

Establish a small component vocabulary as the design system: an InteractiveCard assembler that, given a SwiftUI view, produces an entity with ViewAttachmentComponent + a cached right-sized CollisionComponent (group .node) + InputTargetComponent + HoverEffectComponent; and a Control assembler (group .control, parent isEnabled handling) for x/ports. Build all entities through these two factories so new entity kinds scale without re-deriving collision/hover wiring.
Keep edges/ports purely decorative: no InputTargetComponent, no collision (or .trigger-only), and in group .edge excluded from all input masks — they should never participate in hit-testing.

research.topic: RealityKit/visionOS animation for UI feedback (transitions + jitter)
research.summary: visionOS 26 adds a SwiftUI-to-RealityKit animation bridge; one transform authority at a time avoids our snap-back and dead-hover bugs. Entity.animate/content.animate drive transitions, a deltaTime System drives jitter, ManipulationComponent(.stay) drives drag.

corePrinciples.principle: Exclusive transform ownership per instant
corePrinciples.detail: Reconcile re-asserting stored positions while a gesture writes = snap-back. Gesture owns during drag, data model after release. Never write observed entity state in RealityView.update (infinite loop, WWDC25 274); write in gesture/event closures, Systems, or make.

corePrinciples.principle: Bridge for transitions; deltaTime System for jitter
corePrinciples.detail: Entity.animate(.spring/.easeInOut){...} (gesture/event closures) and content.animate (inherits the SwiftUI transaction) drive layout moves and hover pop. Idle/selected jitter+breathing lives in a custom System.update integrating context.deltaTime, offsetting a clean base transform; gate behind accessibilityReduceMotion (ADHD users).

corePrinciples.principle: Prefer ManipulationComponent over hand-rolled DragGesture
corePrinciples.detail: visionOS 2.6+; releaseBehavior=.stay persists (no snap-back), ManipulationEvents handle handoff, DidUpdateTransform clamps to bounds. RealityKit owns the transform during manipulation, fixing the @State-not-visible-in-update class of bug.

keyAPIs.name: Entity.animate / RealityViewContent.animate
keyAPIs.purpose: Apply a SwiftUI Animation implicitly to entity Transform/color/audio.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Entity.animate in gesture/event closures; content.animate inherits the transaction; read+write observed in update = loop. Pre-26 fallback: entity.move(to:relativeTo:duration:timingFunction:) (AnimationTimingFunction, no spring).

keyAPIs.name: System + EntityQuery + SceneUpdateContext.deltaTime
keyAPIs.purpose: Per-frame procedural jitter/breathing/wiggle over matching entities.
keyAPIs.gotchas: deltaTime is seconds (never assume 90fps); offset a clean base; no allocations; registerSystem(). Authored-loop alt: AnimationResource/FromToByAnimation/OrbitAnimation + BindTarget(.transform/.parameter).

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationComponent / HoverEffectComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: Built-in drag/rotate/scale; gaze/hover feedback.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Default releaseBehavior .reset snaps back—set .stay; clamp in DidUpdateTransform. SwiftUI hoverEffect inside an attachment does NOT scale the entity (bug #2); a parent collision enclosing the child swallows hover/taps.

antiPatterns.pattern: Re-asserting stored transforms / writing observed props in update
antiPatterns.why: Snap-back (update overwrites the gesture's live position with stale stored) and infinite loop (read+write @Observable in update, per Apple). The @State draggingID skip is unreliable (not visible to update in time). Diff and only move changed dataOwned entities; write in gestures/Systems.

antiPatterns.pattern: SwiftUI hoverEffect/scaleEffect in the attachment + child inside the parent collision
antiPatterns.why: The effect scales flat 2D content not the entity (bug #2 no-highlight); the parent collision (depth max(z,0.03)) encloses the child x (+0.006 z) and swallows hover/taps. Pop the entity via Entity.animate; give the child a tight non-overlapping collision or HitTarget.

antiPatterns.pattern: Rebuilding entities/edges each frame; baking jitter into the persisted position
antiPatterns.why: Recreation kills in-flight animations/hover/System motion and churns the graph; jitter in the stored transform drifts saved layout. Diff in place (move edge endpoints); keep a clean base + ephemeral deltaTime offset.

recommendationsForOurApp: Per-entity ownership state machine (dataOwned/dragging/animating); reconcile becomes a diff that writes transforms only for dataOwned entities and only when the data-model position changed. Root-fixes snap-back instead of the @State skip.

Replace DragGesture with ManipulationComponent(releaseBehavior=.stay); clamp in DidUpdateTransform, persist via tRPC on WillEnd/WillRelease. Animate moves Entity.animate(.easeOut(0.35)){move(to:target)} and hover pop Entity.animate(.spring(0.25,0.6)){scale=1.08}; keep HoverEffectComponent; gate decorative motion behind reduceMotion.

JitterSystem+JitterComponent(base,amplitude,frequency,seed): phase+=context.deltaTime, transform=base+sin offset+tiny rotation (clean base=no drift). Fix the x control with its own tight non-overlapping collision (further +z / shrink card depth) or HitTarget; highlight via Entity.animate. Add a FeedbackStyle layer; glass glow via ShaderGraphMaterial BindTarget(.parameter); allocation-free System, profile RealityKit Trace at 90fps.

citations.title: WWDC25 274 Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit
Step Into Vision Manipulation deep-dive
Apple Docs SceneUpdateContext.deltaTime

research.topic: Materials, Glass, and Semi-Transparency for elegant visionOS UI (visionOS 26.2, SwiftUI-renders-content + RealityKit-places-it)
research.summary: For our locked "SwiftUI renders content, RealityKit places it" paradigm, the correct material engine is SwiftUI itself, not RealityKit materials. Every card/panel/note is a SwiftUI view inside a ViewAttachmentComponent, so it should style with SwiftUI glass/materials (.glassBackgroundEffect on visionOS, the new .glassEffect/Glass/GlassEffectContainer from the unified visionOS 26 Liquid Glass design, or system materials .ultraThin/.thin/.regular/.thick with vibrancy). RealityKit materials (PhysicallyBasedMaterial, UnlitMaterial, ShaderGraphMaterial, OcclusionMaterial) are only relevant for non-attachment geometry we actually draw with meshes: our edge lines, ports, grounding/occlusion planes, and any volume "shell." Mixing the two is where translucency bugs come from. There is a documented, Apple-engineer-confirmed limitation that .glassBackgroundEffect can render opaque over a skybox/custom immersive background — but in a standard volumetric WindowGroup over passthrough (our case) glass works as intended, so we should prefer it. Overlapping translucent RealityKit meshes (our edges/ports crossing cards) suffer order-dependent sorting artifacts that are controlled with ModelSortGroup/ModelSortGroupComponent and opacityThreshold, not by hoping the renderer sorts correctly. The biggest architectural lever is that visionOS 26 makes Entities Observable and lets SwiftUI animations drive RealityKit Transforms (entity.animate), and provides ManipulationComponent for drag — which is the real fix for our drag snap-back and the foundation for a token-driven, cohesive glass design system. Text legibility on glass comes for free from system text styles + vibrancy (primary/secondary/tertiary); colored text on glass is an explicit anti-pattern.

corePrinciples.principle: For attachment content, style with SwiftUI materials/glass — never substitute RealityKit materials
corePrinciples.detail: Our cards/panels/notes are SwiftUI views surfaced via ViewAttachmentComponent. They must be styled with SwiftUI: .glassBackgroundEffect() (visionOS-native frosted glass), the visionOS 26 .glassEffect(_:in:)/Glass/GlassEffectContainer Liquid Glass API, or .background(.thinMaterial/.regularMaterial, in: shape) with system vibrancy. RealityKit PhysicallyBasedMaterial/ShaderGraphMaterial are for meshes we draw ourselves (edges, ports, planes), not for the look of SwiftUI cards. Our NodeCardView already does this correctly (.background(.regularMaterial)); the architecture should make that the only sanctioned path.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 Session 274 'Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit'; createwithswift 'Using Attachments in a RealityView'; HIG Materials

corePrinciples.principle: Glass over passthrough works; glass over a custom skybox/immersive background can render opaque (known limitation)
corePrinciples.detail: An Apple Vision Pro engineer confirmed on the dev forums that .glassBackgroundEffect does not let a skybox texture show through — it renders opaque/frosted with no environment behind it. This is a limitation of immersive-skybox setups, NOT of our volumetric WindowGroup over real passthrough. Since our volume is gravity-aligned over passthrough, glass behaves correctly, so we should adopt it confidently. If we ever add a skybox/full-immersion mode, fall back to .background(Color.white.opacity(0.5)) or a mesh plane.
corePrinciples.source: Apple Developer Forums thread 755910 (glassMaterialEffect not working with skybox)

corePrinciples.principle: Material level encodes elevation/hierarchy; this is the basis for design tokens
corePrinciples.detail: Apple's HIG defines a layering rule: lighter/thicker glass = foreground/closer, darker/thinner = background; .thin highlights interactive elements (buttons, selected items), .regular separates sections (panels/sidebars), .thick makes things recede (text fields). 'Use darker material over lighter to create depth; lighter over darker to highlight interactive areas.' This maps cleanly to a token system: each entity kind gets a material level + tint + corner radius + elevation, instead of ad-hoc per-card .background calls.
corePrinciples.source: Apple HIG — Materials foundation; createwithswift 'Ensuring interface legibility and contrast in visionOS'

corePrinciples.principle: Text legibility on glass is handled by vibrancy + system text styles, not custom colors
corePrinciples.detail: visionOS provides primary/secondary/tertiary vibrancy. System text styles include appropriate vibrancy automatically and remain legible over arbitrary passthrough. Use .foregroundStyle(.primary/.secondary) for hierarchy; reserve tertiary for low-importance text. Explicit DON'Ts: colored text on glass, stacking light materials without a darker separator, fully opaque windows. Our cards use .foregroundStyle(.secondary) correctly; the system should forbid hardcoded text colors in the token layer.
corePrinciples.source: Apple HIG — Materials; createwithswift legibility article

corePrinciples.principle: Liquid Glass (visionOS 26) is the unified forward direction; GlassEffectContainer enables cohesive multi-element glass
corePrinciples.detail: .glassEffect(_:in:isEnabled:) with Glass (.regular/.clear/.identity, .tint(_:), .interactive()) is the cross-platform Liquid Glass API (visionOS 26.0+). Critical rule: 'glass cannot sample other glass' — wrap related glass elements in a GlassEffectContainer so they share a sampling region and can morph (glassEffectID + Namespace, glassEffectUnion, glassEffectTransition). .regular is universal; .clear needs bold content over media. Accessibility (Reduce Transparency, Increase Contrast, Reduce Motion) is handled automatically — do not override. For visionOS specifically, .glassBackgroundEffect remains the established window/attachment glass; treat .glassEffect as the newer in-content variant and validate per-surface with xcodebuild.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 'Liquid Glass' direction; LiquidGlassReference (conorluddy); Apple newsroom 2025-06

corePrinciples.principle: Overlapping translucent RealityKit meshes are order-dependent — control draw order explicitly
corePrinciples.detail: RealityKit uses rasterization with depth-based sorting that fails for intersecting/overlapping transparent surfaces (flicker, vanishing outlines). For our derived edges/ports that cross translucent cards, control order with ModelSortGroup/ModelSortGroupComponent (render innermost/smallest first, outermost/largest last) and choose a depth-pass strategy. Also set opacityThreshold on transparent PhysicallyBasedMaterial (even 0.0) to avoid the FB12605220 erroneous-culling bug when blending with faceCulling=.none.
corePrinciples.source: xreality.zone 'Solving Nested Transparent Objects in RealityKit with Rendering Ordering'; varrall 'Skyboxes and transparency in visionOS' (FB12605220)

corePrinciples.principle: Depth/elevation for floating cards comes from grounding shadows + subtle specular, not fake drop shadows
corePrinciples.detail: Without shadows it is hard to read relative depth of floating entities. RealityKit grounding shadows (GroundingShadowComponent(castsShadow: true)) give real spatial shadows; an OcclusionMaterial plane beneath content plus a DirectionalLight shadow is the manual route. HIG: interactive elements get subtle specular highlights that reinforce dimensionality. For UI color fidelity on any mesh we draw, use UnlitMaterial with applyPostProcessToneMap:false so colors match SwiftUI exactly (tone mapping is on by default and shifts UI colors).
corePrinciples.source: WWDC23 Session 10095 'Explore rendering for spatial computing'; OcclusionMaterial docs; HIG Materials

keyAPIs.name: .glassBackgroundEffect(displayMode:)
keyAPIs.purpose: visionOS-native frosted/glass background for SwiftUI views, including RealityView attachment root views
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Default glass for our card/panel/note attachment root views over passthrough. The established, battle-tested visionOS window/attachment glass.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Renders opaque over a custom skybox/immersive background (confirmed Apple limitation, FB-worthy). Fine over real passthrough in a volumetric WindowGroup (our case).

keyAPIs.name: .glassEffect(_:in:isEnabled:) / Glass / GlassEffectContainer / glassEffectID
keyAPIs.purpose: Unified Liquid Glass material for in-content glass shapes, with morphing/union across grouped elements
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Newer cohesive glass language (buttons, our 'x' dismiss control, grouped toolbars). Wrap related glass in GlassEffectContainer so they sample shared backdrop and can morph.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Glass cannot sample other glass — must share a GlassEffectContainer. .clear needs bold content. Battle-tested on iOS; on visionOS validate each surface with xcodebuild since SourceKit lies.

keyAPIs.name: System materials: .ultraThinMaterial/.thinMaterial/.regularMaterial/.thickMaterial + vibrancy
keyAPIs.purpose: Layered translucent backgrounds that encode elevation; primary/secondary/tertiary vibrancy for legible text
keyAPIs.whenToUse: The token vocabulary for our design system: map entity kind -> material level. .thin for interactive, .regular for section/panel, .thick for recessed.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 1.0+ (materials); vibrancy via system text styles
keyAPIs.gotchas: Don't stack two light materials without a darker separator; don't use colored text on glass. Let system vibrancy handle contrast.

keyAPIs.name: ViewAttachmentComponent(rootView:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Attach any SwiftUI view to a RealityKit entity from anywhere in code (visionOS 26 attachment model)
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Replace the old builder-based RealityView attachments closure. Each SpatialEntity card becomes Entity(components: ViewAttachmentComponent(rootView: ...)). This is the seam where SwiftUI material styling lives.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Pair with InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent on the gesture target. Coordinate space: 1 point = 1 meter, y-up.

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationComponent + ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(_:hoverEffect:allowedInputTypes:collisionShapes:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Built-in grab/move/rotate/scale with automatic hover effect, collision, and input-target wiring; release behavior hooks
keyAPIs.whenToUse: The ROOT FIX for our drag system. Replaces hand-rolled DragGesture + per-pass position re-assertion. configureEntity auto-adds Manipulation/Collision/InputTarget/HoverEffect components and owns the transform during drag.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Use releaseBehavior:.stay to keep dragged position; subscribe to ManipulationEvents.WillRelease to persist/animate. hoverEffect:.spotlight/.highlight gives the highlight our 'x' control lacks.

keyAPIs.name: Entity Observable + entity.animate(using:) / content.animate { }
keyAPIs.purpose: Entities are Observable; SwiftUI animations can implicitly drive Transform/color/light/audio component changes
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Smooth animations (snap-back-on-release, jitter on highlight, layout transitions) and to drive SwiftUI state from entity transforms. Core of the animation architecture.
keyAPIs.gotchas: DO NOT write observed entity state inside RealityView update closure (infinite loop — exactly our reconcile-per-pass bug pattern). Guard writes: if entity.position != new { ... }. Mutate in systems, gesture closures, or make closure.

keyAPIs.name: HoverEffectComponent / .hoverEffect(_:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Eye-tracking highlight feedback on entities (RealityKit) or attachment views (SwiftUI)
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Every interactive entity AND the 'x' dismiss control. SwiftUI .hoverEffect(.highlight) inside the attachment, or HoverEffectComponent on the entity. Custom 'jitter' can be driven by reacting to hover via a custom component/system + entity.animate.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 1.0+ (component); .hoverEffect SwiftUI variants 1.0+, richer styles in 26
keyAPIs.gotchas: Our 'x' fails to highlight because the parent card CollisionComponent (depth max(z,0.03)) swallows it. Fix with proper child collision shape + InputTargetComponent precedence, or make 'x' a separate sibling entity, not nested inside the card's collision volume.

keyAPIs.name: CoordinateSpace3D / GeometryProxy3D.coordinateSpace3D() / convert(_:from:to:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Unified points<->meters and axis conversion between SwiftUI and RealityKit
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Converting drag/gesture values and layout-engine meter positions into entity space cleanly instead of value.convert(... .local,.scene) ad hoc math.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Entity and Scene conform to CoordinateSpace3D; use it instead of manual unit assumptions.

keyAPIs.name: PhysicallyBasedMaterial.blending = .transparent(opacity:) + opacityThreshold + faceCulling
keyAPIs.purpose: Transparency on RealityKit meshes we draw (edges, ports, volume shells)
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Only for actual mesh geometry, not SwiftUI cards. Set blending for translucency; set opacityThreshold (even 0.0) to dodge FB12605220 culling; faceCulling .none for double-sided.
keyAPIs.gotchas: No 'opacity' property in code (only in Reality Composer Pro) — set .blending. Overlapping transparent meshes sort incorrectly without ModelSortGroup.

keyAPIs.name: ModelSortGroup / ModelSortGroupComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: Explicit draw-order control for overlapping translucent meshes (depth-pass: pre/post/none)
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Our derived edges/ports crossing translucent cards. Render smallest/innermost first, largest/outermost last to stop flicker and vanishing lines.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Each depth-pass strategy trades color correctness vs outline visibility; no perfect option for dynamic intersecting transparency — keep edges thin and minimize overlap.

keyAPIs.name: OcclusionMaterial / GroundingShadowComponent + UnlitMaterial(applyPostProcessToneMap:false)
keyAPIs.purpose: Depth cues: real grounding shadows and invisible occluders; tone-map-free color for UI-accurate mesh color
keyAPIs.whenToUse: GroundingShadowComponent on cards/volumes for floating-depth readability. OcclusionMaterial plane for shadow catch or hiding. UnlitMaterial(...,applyPostProcessToneMap:false) so any colored mesh (ports/edges) matches SwiftUI palette.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Tone mapping is ON by default and shifts UI colors — disable for menus/HUD/UI-colored meshes per WWDC23 guidance.

antiPatterns.pattern: Re-asserting card.position to the stored model value on EVERY RealityView update pass
antiPatterns.why: This is the root of the drag snap-back. WWDC25 explicitly warns: do not modify observed entity state in the update closure (creates a fight/loop). The renderer's update pass must not own transforms during a gesture. Use ManipulationComponent (owns transform during drag) + persist on ManipulationEvents.WillRelease; if you must write in update, guard with if entity.position != new.

antiPatterns.pattern: Building UI look from RealityKit primitive meshes / 3D text / SimpleMaterial planes behind attachments
antiPatterns.why: Already rejected in our project and contradicts the locked paradigm. SwiftUI must render content; RealityKit only places it. Mesh-backed 'glass' (SimpleMaterial(color:.blue.withAlphaComponent(0.5))) is the forum workaround for the skybox bug — not needed over passthrough and visually inferior to real glass + vibrancy.

antiPatterns.pattern: Nesting the 'x' dismiss control inside the parent card's collision volume
antiPatterns.why: The parent CollisionComponent (depth max(z,0.03)) encloses the child 'x' (+0.006 z) and swallows hover/taps, so the SwiftUI .hoverEffect scale never fires. There is no hierarchical collision precedence by default. Fix: make controls siblings with their own tight collision + InputTargetComponent, or use one ViewAttachmentComponent whose SwiftUI root contains the button (SwiftUI handles intra-view hit-testing), not a separate RealityKit child poking through the card's collider.

antiPatterns.pattern: Relying on a SwiftUI .hoverEffect{ scaleEffect } inside an attachment to scale a RealityKit child entity
antiPatterns.why: SwiftUI hover state inside an attachment drives the SwiftUI view's own rendering, not a sibling/child RealityKit entity's transform. For entity-level highlight/jitter, drive a RealityKit HoverEffectComponent or react to hover in a system and animate via entity.animate.

antiPatterns.pattern: Ad-hoc per-card .background(.regularMaterial) / .opacity / stroke calls scattered across views
antiPatterns.why: Produces an inconsistent visual language and makes global changes (accessibility, theming, elevation) impossible. Replace with design tokens: a Material/Tint/CornerRadius/Elevation enum keyed by SpatialEntityKind, applied via one shared ViewModifier. HIG's material-level=elevation mapping makes this principled.

antiPatterns.pattern: Colored text on glass and stacking two light materials without a darker separator
antiPatterns.why: HIG explicitly calls these out as legibility failures. Use system text styles + vibrancy (primary/secondary/tertiary) and alternate material darkness to preserve contrast over arbitrary passthrough.

antiPatterns.pattern: Drawing many overlapping translucent edge/port meshes and trusting default depth sorting
antiPatterns.why: RealityKit transparency is order-dependent; overlapping translucent surfaces flicker and lose outlines. Requires ModelSortGroup + opacityThreshold. Keep edges thin/opaque-ish, minimize overlap, or render edges with UnlitMaterial and a sort group.

antiPatterns.pattern: Leaving RealityKit tone mapping enabled on UI-colored meshes
antiPatterns.why: Tone mapping (on by default) shifts colors so port/edge colors won't match the SwiftUI type palette. Use UnlitMaterial(applyPostProcessToneMap:false) for color-accurate UI geometry.

recommendationsForOurApp: Adopt a token-driven material system: define a SurfaceStyle token (material level, tint color, corner radius, elevation, stroke) keyed by SpatialEntityKind (taskNode/stepNode/typePanel/workflowVolume/note). Apply via ONE shared SwiftUI ViewModifier so all cards share a cohesive translucent language instead of per-view .background calls. This directly answers the 'scalable component/design system' goal.

Use .glassBackgroundEffect() as the default card surface over passthrough (it works in our volumetric WindowGroup; the opaque-over-skybox bug does not apply). Layer with HIG semantics: typePanel = .regular (section), nodes = .thin (interactive), recessed/secondary content = .thick. Reserve the visionOS 26 .glassEffect/GlassEffectContainer for grouped controls (toolbar, the 'x' + future per-card buttons) so they morph cohesively.

Replace the hand-rolled DragGesture + per-pass position re-assertion with ManipulationComponent via ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(card, hoverEffect: .spotlight/.highlight, allowedInputTypes:.all, collisionShapes:[...]). Set releaseBehavior:.stay and subscribe to ManipulationEvents.WillRelease to persist position to the SpatialEntity. This makes grab-and-drag near-trivial and eliminates snap-back at the root because RealityKit owns the transform during the gesture — not reconcile.

Establish explicit transform ownership: the layout engine/model owns position ONLY when an entity is in auto-flow and not being manipulated; the gesture/ManipulationComponent owns it during drag; entity.animate owns it during transitions. Stop writing transforms in the RealityView update closure; move reconcile logic to a custom RealityKit System and guard all writes with if entity.position != target. This is the architectural fix WWDC25 prescribes for the infinite-loop/fight pattern.

Fix the 'x' control by putting it INSIDE the card's SwiftUI attachment root (so SwiftUI does the hit-testing and .hoverEffect works) rather than as a separate RealityKit child entity poking through the parent's collider. If a control must be a separate entity, give it its own tight CollisionComponent + InputTargetComponent positioned clearly outside the parent's collision box, and drive highlight via HoverEffectComponent.

Add depth cues for floating cards: GroundingShadowComponent(castsShadow:true) on nodes/volumes and subtle specular via the glass material. This restores the spatial-depth readability that flat glass cards lack.

Implement playful 'jitter on highlight' via entity.animate(using:) reacting to hover (HoverEffectComponent state read in a System, or onHover in the SwiftUI attachment toggling an entity transform with a spring/easeInOut), NOT via SwiftUI scaleEffect inside the attachment which cannot move the entity.

For derived edges/ports (real RealityKit meshes): keep them thin and use UnlitMaterial(...,applyPostProcessToneMap:false) for color-accurate type tints; if they visibly cross translucent cards, assign a ModelSortGroupComponent and set opacityThreshold to avoid sorting flicker and the FB12605220 culling bug. Rebuild edges incrementally (diff) inside a System rather than delete-and-re-add-all every pass.

Migrate attachments to ViewAttachmentComponent and gestures toward GestureComponent/targetedToEntity with CoordinateSpace3D conversion — this is the visionOS 26 idiom, removes our name-parsing gesture disambiguation, and gives clean points<->meters conversion for the layout engine.

Lean on automatic accessibility: do NOT override Reduce Transparency / Increase Contrast / Reduce Motion; let system materials and Liquid Glass adapt. Keep text on system styles + vibrancy (primary/secondary/tertiary); ban hardcoded text colors in the token layer.

Validate every material/glass choice with xcodebuild -scheme TaskPlannerVision -destination 'generic/platform=visionOS Simulator' (SourceKit reports false errors) and visually in Simulator, since glass behavior in RealityView attachments is the kind of thing the compiler accepts but renders wrong.
