research.topic: Apple Human Interface Guidelines & ergonomics for visionOS spatial design — translated to rules for a draggable-card task-planner volume
research.summary: For visionOS 26.2, the right container for a workspace of movable cards/panels/notes is a Volume launched in the Shared Space (not an immersive space); volumes are for self-contained 3D experiences where movable 3D content is the primary point. The single biggest finding is that both known bugs are symptoms of fighting the platform instead of using its modern interaction stack. Bug #1 (drag snap-back) is a transform-ownership problem that visionOS 26's ManipulationComponent solves outright: it owns the entity transform during a pinch-drag (with z-telescoping, rotate, two-handed scale, audio), and releaseBehavior=.stay persists the moved position — making grab-and-drag genuinely trivial. The deeper root cause is mutating @Observable entity transforms inside RealityView's update closure, which WWDC25 #274 explicitly warns against; combined with GestureComponent (per-entity gestures in entity coordinate space) and the Unified Coordinate Conversion API, this lets the architecture drop the reconcile-reasserts-position pattern, the name-prefix gesture parsing, and the manual point-to-meter math. Bug #2 (the × not highlighting) is a target-size + hierarchical-collision failure: the × is far under the 60 pt (≈2.5°, ≈4.4 cm @ 1 m) minimum and its collision is enclosed by the parent card's 30 mm-deep collision box that swallows hover/taps; a SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside a ViewAttachmentComponent also won't visibly scale a placed entity. Ergonomically, primary content belongs centered and slightly below eye level beyond arm's length at consistent depth (the current upper-back panel row strains neck/eyes), legibility comes from glass + vibrancy rather than solid/colored fills, and any playful "jitter" must be low-amplitude, gated behind Reduce Motion with a crossfade fallback, plus dynamic world scaling and full accessibility (AccessibilityComponent, VoiceOver, Dwell). These are all battle-tested, shipping visionOS 26 APIs.

corePrinciples.principle: Prefer the platform's combined manipulation gesture over a hand-rolled DragGesture
corePrinciples.detail: ManipulationComponent (visionOS 26) is the answer to 'grab-and-drag should be near-trivial.' It owns the transform during interaction, telescopes z so far cards can be drawn close, supports rotate and two-handed scale, plays spatial audio, and offers releaseBehavior=.stay to keep the moved position. A plain DragGesture has a known confusion (movement stays relative to original facing direction if the user turns) that Manipulation solves for free. Replaces the custom translation-grab dragGesture and removes any need to suppress reconcile during drag.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 #274 'Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit'; 'Deep Dive into Manipulation on visionOS' (stepinto.vision)

corePrinciples.principle: Never mutate observed entity transforms inside RealityView's update closure
corePrinciples.detail: This is the literal root cause of bug #1. WWDC25 #274 states entities are Observable in visionOS 26 and warns that modifying observed entities in update creates infinite loops / fights live interaction. Safe mutation zones are gesture closures, custom RealityKit systems, and the make closure. The current reconcile() re-asserts every card's stored position every update pass, and the stored position is stale mid-drag — so the card snaps to the data-model value. Stop driving transforms from update; drive them from the gesture/manipulation and persist on release.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 #274 'Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit'

corePrinciples.principle: 60 pt (≈2.5°, ≈4.4 cm @ 1 m) minimum target, ≥4 pt spacing, and each target needs its own non-enclosed collision
corePrinciples.detail: Gaze targeting requires every interactive element occupy ≥60 pt of space (visually smaller is OK if total allocated space is 60 pt) with ≥4 pt spacing to survive eye-tracking drift. Bug #2's × fails twice: it is tiny AND its collision is enclosed by the parent card's collision box (parent depth max(z,0.03)=30 mm vs child only +6 mm in front), so the parent swallows hover/tap. Targets need their own collision not contained by a parent/sibling volume; rounded shapes (circle/pill) are easier to look at than sharp corners (the eye fixates on sharp edges).
corePrinciples.source: WWDC23 #10073 'Design for spatial input'; WWDC25 #303 'Design hover interactions for visionOS'; HIG Eyes

corePrinciples.principle: Highlight every custom interactive element on gaze; never reveal a control that pulls the eye away
corePrinciples.detail: System controls highlight automatically; custom ones (cards, ×, ports) must add a highlight whose shape matches the content, and it must be RealityKit-visible (a SwiftUI .hoverEffect{scaleEffect} inside a ViewAttachmentComponent rootView does not reliably scale the placed entity — use HoverEffectComponent with .highlight/.spotlight, or a custom hover effect). Hover runs outside the app's process for privacy, so highlight is the only feedback channel pre-gesture. WWDC25 #303 warns specifically against hidden close buttons that appear on hover: the eye jumps to the new control and triggers accidental dismissal — exactly the × redesign risk. Use the instant/delayed/ramp animation categories deliberately, keep an anchoring element static, keep effects small.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 #303 'Design hover interactions for visionOS'; WWDC23 #10073

corePrinciples.principle: Place primary content centered and slightly below eye level, beyond arm's length, at consistent interaction depth
corePrinciples.detail: Eyes turn down and laterally comfortably; upward/diagonal rotation fatigues. Put frequently-used content centered and slightly below the sight line; reserve periphery and high positions for secondary/brief content. Extended-reading content goes beyond arm's length; the closest zone is for momentary effort or direct touch only. Keep interactive elements at consistent depth to avoid constant refocusing. The current type-cluster layout (panels in a row across the upper-back, tasks stacked beneath) places primary content high and far — a neck/eye-strain pattern the layout engine should correct.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC23 #10078 'Design considerations for vision and motion'; HIG Designing for visionOS

corePrinciples.principle: Adopt glass + vibrancy for legibility; never opaque windows or colored text
corePrinciples.detail: Glass is an adaptive material that modulates background colors for optimal contrast and grounds content in the room. Use vibrancy tiers (.primary for standard text, .secondary for footnotes/subtitles, .tertiary for inactive/low-legibility) rather than solid colors, which have fixed contrast and can't adapt to passthrough. Prefer white/light text, avoid colored text, and stack a darker material beneath lighter ones behind text. Avoid opaque windows — they kill spatial awareness. This is the platform-correct way to get the desired elegant semi-transparent glass.
corePrinciples.source: 'Ensuring interface legibility and contrast in visionOS' (createwithswift); WWDC23 #10110 'Elevate your windowed app for spatial computing'

corePrinciples.principle: Avoid head-anchored content and vection-inducing motion; provide Reduce Motion crossfade fallbacks
corePrinciples.detail: Prefer world-locked placement (this app's persisted positions are already correct). Make any moving virtual object semitransparent so passthrough stays visible and the brain doesn't read object motion as self-motion. Avoid abrupt movement, oscillation near 0.2 Hz (one cycle / 5 s), and fast/large objects passing close. For the desired playful 'jitter on highlight': keep amplitude low, gate it behind accessibilityReduceMotion, substitute a crossfade/static state when Reduce Motion is on. Head-anchored content also breaks Zoom for low-vision users — honor accessibilityPrefersHeadAnchorAlternative if any head-locked UI is ever added.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC23 #10078 'Design considerations for vision and motion'; WWDC23 #10034 'Create accessible spatial experiences'

corePrinciples.principle: Use a Volume in the Shared Space; enable dynamic world scaling and handle Window Zoom/resize
corePrinciples.detail: Volumes are for self-contained 3D experiences where movable 3D content is the primary point — exactly this app. Apps should launch in the Shared Space (window/volume), not jump straight to an immersive space. Use .defaultWorldScalingBehavior(.dynamic) so UI grows as it recedes, preserving angular target area (the 60 pt rule) and FOV coverage; fixed scale shrinks targets to unusable sizes. Also scale the RealityView root by rendered-volume-size / defaultSize (via GeometryReader3D / coordinate conversion), or content clips at non-default Window Zoom and user resize.
corePrinciples.source: HIG Designing for visionOS; WWDC23 #10073; 'If you've created a visionOS app with a volume, you probably did it wrong' (lunarskydiving)

corePrinciples.principle: Disambiguate interaction by typed component / per-entity identity, not by parsing entity.name strings
corePrinciples.detail: visionOS 26's GestureComponent attaches gestures to specific entities and reports values in that entity's coordinate space, and the Unified Coordinate Conversion API (CoordinateSpace3D on Entity/Scene; GeometryProxy3D.coordinateSpace3D()) replaces manual point-to-meter math. This obsoletes the current 'ctl::/edge:: name-prefix parsing' (which also overloads entity.name as the id). Express role/identity via real components/custom component types per entity.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 #274 'Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit'

corePrinciples.principle: Make the spatial scene accessible from day one
corePrinciples.detail: Add AccessibilityComponent to each interactive entity (isAccessibilityElement, label, value, traits like .button, systemActions=[.activate]) so VoiceOver users can navigate and activate cards. Support VoiceOver Direct Gesture mode (post AccessibilityNotification.Announcement for state changes), Dwell Control (all actions reachable via tap/scroll/long-press/drag), Reduce Motion (crossfade), and Reduce Transparency. Spatial experiences can be fully usable without precise vision or movement — design for that, not as an afterthought.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC23 #10034 'Create accessible spatial experiences'; 'Improving accessibility support in your visionOS app' (Apple docs)

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: Combined pinch-and-hold gesture to move/rotate/scale any entity with z-telescoping, two-handed scale, hand-off, and spatial audio — the production fix for drag snap-back.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: On every movable card/note/panel/workflow-volume entity, replacing the hand-rolled DragGesture. Set releaseBehavior=.stay to persist; subscribe to ManipulationEvents.WillEnd to save the transform via tRPC. Configure via ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(entity, hoverEffect:, allowedInputTypes:, collisionShapes:); tune .dynamics (translationBehavior, primary/secondaryRotationBehavior, scalingBehavior, inertia). Requires CollisionComponent + InputTargetComponent (+ HoverEffectComponent).
keyAPIs.gotchas: Default releaseBehavior=.reset returns the entity home on release (looks like snap-back) — must set .stay or persist in WillEnd. ManipulationEvents are WillBegin / DidUpdateTransform (per-frame, for clamping to VolumeMetrics bounds) / WillRelease / WillEnd / DidHandOff. Set audioConfiguration=.none for custom audio. Known system-wide bug where translation can stop until device restart.

keyAPIs.name: GestureComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: Attach SwiftUI gestures (Tap, etc.) directly to a specific entity; values reported in that entity's coordinate space.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: For tap-to-edit, connect/link mode taps, and the × dismiss action — per entity, instead of two top-level .targetedToAnyEntity() gestures disambiguated by name parsing.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Still requires InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent on the targeted entity. Lets you retire the ctl::/edge:: name-prefix scheme.

keyAPIs.name: HoverEffectComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: RealityKit-visible gaze highlight on a placed entity (system .highlight, .spotlight, or a custom hover effect).
keyAPIs.whenToUse: On every interactive entity (cards, ×, ports). This is what bug #2 actually needs — a SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside the attachment's rootView does not reliably scale the entity.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 1 (custom hover effects expanded in visionOS 2/26)
keyAPIs.gotchas: The effect's shape should match content; for the × use a rounded shape ≥60 pt. Custom effects animate via instant/delayed/ramp categories (WWDC25 #303).

keyAPIs.name: Entity.animate(with:) / RealityViewContent.animate(with:)
keyAPIs.purpose: SwiftUI-style implicit animation of RealityKit Transform/Model/Light/Audio components (e.g., .bouncy, .spring).
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Smooth card movement on auto-layout reflow, settle-on-release, and the low-amplitude 'jitter' on highlight (gated behind Reduce Motion).
keyAPIs.gotchas: Do not drive these from the update closure on observed entities; run from gesture closures or systems. Honor accessibilityReduceMotion.

keyAPIs.name: defaultWorldScalingBehavior(.dynamic)
keyAPIs.purpose: Scene modifier making window/volume UI scale up as it moves away so angular target size (the 60 pt rule) and FOV coverage stay constant.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: On the volumetric WindowGroup so cards remain readable/targetable at distance.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 2 (Apple's recommended default behavior for spatial input)
keyAPIs.gotchas: Distinct from RealityKit content scaling for Window Zoom/user-resize — still scale the RealityView root by rendered-size/defaultSize via GeometryReader3D / coordinate conversion or content clips.

keyAPIs.name: Unified Coordinate Conversion API (CoordinateSpace3D)
keyAPIs.purpose: Convert points between SwiftUI and RealityKit scene space; Entity and Scene conform to CoordinateSpace3D, plus GeometryProxy3D.coordinateSpace3D().
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Anywhere the code currently does value.convert(translation3D, from:.local, to:.scene) or hand-rolled point-to-meter math (edges, spawn points, drag deltas).
keyAPIs.gotchas: Use gesture.values(in: entity.coordinateSpace3D) to get values already in the right space.

keyAPIs.name: ViewAttachmentComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: Declare a SwiftUI view inline on a RealityKit entity (the locked 'SwiftUI renders content, RealityKit places it' pattern).
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Keep using it for all card/panel/note visuals; in visionOS 26 it can be created inline rather than via the RealityView attachments builder, which also resolves the make-time attachment race the current code works around.
keyAPIs.gotchas: A control rendered as a child attachment still needs its own InputTargetComponent + non-enclosed CollisionComponent + HoverEffectComponent to receive gaze/taps.

keyAPIs.name: AccessibilityComponent + AccessibilityNotification.Announcement
keyAPIs.purpose: Make RealityKit entities navigable by VoiceOver (isAccessibilityElement, label, value, traits, systemActions) and announce state changes.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: On every interactive entity; announce merges/links/dismissals and Direct Gesture mode events.
keyAPIs.gotchas: In VoiceOver Direct Gesture mode, activate actions are unavailable — must post announcements for meaningful events.

keyAPIs.name: accessibilityReduceMotion / accessibilityReduceTransparency / accessibilityPrefersHeadAnchorAlternative
keyAPIs.purpose: SwiftUI environment values to honor accessibility settings.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Gate jitter/animation behind Reduce Motion (crossfade fallback); reduce glass translucency for Reduce Transparency; avoid/relocate head-anchored UI when the head-anchor-alternative is preferred.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Reduce Motion fallback should be a crossfade, not simply removing feedback.

keyAPIs.name: PresentationComponent / SpatialContainer + depthAlignment
keyAPIs.purpose: PresentationComponent presents SwiftUI popovers/sheets from an entity; SpatialContainer + depthAlignment lay out views in 3D (overlapping bounding box, depth alignment).
keyAPIs.whenToUse: PresentationComponent for the edit form anchored to a tapped card (instead of a flat .sheet); SpatialContainer/depthAlignment/spatialOverlay for meaningful subtle depth in cards and clusters.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Keep interaction depth consistent; use depth for hierarchy, not gratuitous parallax that forces refocusing.

antiPatterns.pattern: Re-asserting each entity's stored transform from RealityView's update/reconcile closure every pass
antiPatterns.why: Direct cause of bug #1 drag snap-back: the data-model position is stale mid-drag, and mutating observed entities in update fights live interaction and risks infinite loops (WWDC25 #274). Transforms must be owned by the gesture/ManipulationComponent during interaction and persisted on release.

antiPatterns.pattern: A @State draggingID flag set inside a RealityKit gesture .onChanged to skip the dragged card in update
antiPatterns.why: The developer already found this doesn't work: @State mutated in a gesture closure isn't reliably visible to the update closure before the next pass. It's patching the wrong layer — the fix is to not drive transforms from update at all.

antiPatterns.pattern: Hover-revealed or undersized child controls (the ×) nested inside a parent's collision volume
antiPatterns.why: Bug #2. A child 6 mm in front of a 30 mm-deep parent collision box is swallowed by the parent; a SwiftUI hoverEffect inside a ViewAttachmentComponent doesn't visibly scale the placed entity. Apple also warns hover-revealed controls yank the eye and cause accidental dismissal. The × must be ≥60 pt, rounded, persistent (or deliberately ramp-revealed), with its own non-overlapping collision and a HoverEffectComponent highlight.

antiPatterns.pattern: Placing primary content high and far in the upper-back of the volume
antiPatterns.why: Upward/diagonal eye rotation is the most fatiguing; primary content belongs centered and slightly below eye level beyond arm's length. The type-cluster panel row forces neck/eye strain for the most-used elements.

antiPatterns.pattern: Solid/colored fills and text on cards, or opaque card backgrounds
antiPatterns.why: Solid colors have fixed contrast and can't adapt to arbitrary passthrough, so legibility breaks against bright/busy rooms; opaque windows kill spatial awareness. Use glass + vibrancy tiers and white/light text instead.

antiPatterns.pattern: Custom gestures and unconstrained playful motion without accessibility fallbacks
antiPatterns.why: Custom gestures should be rare with UI fallbacks; uncontrolled jitter/oscillation (esp. ~0.2 Hz) induces discomfort/vection and excludes Reduce Motion users. Motion must be low-amplitude, semitransparent if moving, and crossfade when Reduce Motion is on.

antiPatterns.pattern: Encoding gesture roles by parsing entity.name string prefixes (ctl::, edge::)
antiPatterns.why: Brittle and overloads name (also used as the entity id). visionOS 26 GestureComponent + per-entity custom components express role/identity natively; the Unified Coordinate Conversion API replaces manual point-to-meter math.

antiPatterns.pattern: Deleting and re-adding ALL edges and ports from scratch on every reconcile pass
antiPatterns.why: Wasteful churn that also recreates ModelEntity meshes/materials each frame; combined with re-asserting transforms it amplifies the update-loop instability. Edges should be persistent entities whose endpoints update (ideally driven by a RealityKit system observing entity positions), not rebuilt each pass.

recommendationsForOurApp: Replace the hand-rolled DragGesture with ManipulationComponent on every movable entity: configure via ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(card, hoverEffect: .highlight, allowedInputTypes: .indirect, collisionShapes:[box]), set releaseBehavior=.stay, and subscribe to ManipulationEvents.WillEnd to call viewModel.endDrag (persist via tRPC). Use ManipulationEvents.DidUpdateTransform to clamp into VolumeMetrics bounds. This makes grab-and-drag trivial and deletes the draggingID workaround entirely.

Stop driving transforms from reconcile/update. In update only add/remove entities and wire components; let the gesture/ManipulationComponent own the live transform and write position to the entity only when the data model genuinely changes (e.g., auto-layout reflow of non-hand-moved entities), animated with entity.animate(with:.spring). This fixes bug #1 at the root and aligns with WWDC25 #274's 'don't mutate observed entities in update' rule.

Rebuild the × as a real ≥60 pt rounded target with its own collision NOT enclosed by the card: either move it far enough off the card face (or out to the side) that the parent's 30 mm-deep box doesn't contain it, or give the card a precise (non-inflated-depth) collision and the × a sibling collision. Add HoverEffectComponent (.highlight) so it visibly responds to gaze, and avoid hover-to-reveal (persist it, or ramp-reveal slowly per WWDC25 #303) to prevent accidental dismissal. This fixes bug #2.

Adopt GestureComponent + the Unified Coordinate Conversion API to attach tap/dismiss/connect-link handlers per entity, and retire the ctl::/edge:: name-prefix parsing and the value.convert point-to-meter math. Stop overloading entity.name as both id and role carrier — store role in a typed custom component.

Revise the SpatialLayoutEngine so the most-used clusters sit centered and slightly below eye level within a comfortable FOV cone, not across the upper-back; keep interactive content at consistent depth and reserve high/peripheral positions for secondary content. Add .defaultWorldScalingBehavior(.dynamic) to the volumetric WindowGroup and scale the RealityView root by rendered-volume-size/defaultSize so cards stay readable at distance and don't clip at non-default Window Zoom.

Build the card/panel/note design system on glass + vibrancy: glassBackgroundEffect, foregroundStyle(.primary/.secondary/.tertiary) for text hierarchy, white/light text, no colored text, darker material stacked behind text; honor accessibilityReduceTransparency. This delivers the elegant semi-transparent glass aesthetic the platform way.

Implement the playful highlight 'jitter' as a low-amplitude entity.animate spring triggered by the hover state, gated behind accessibilityReduceMotion with a crossfade fallback; never approach ~0.2 Hz oscillation, and make any moving entity semitransparent during motion to avoid vection.

Make edges persistent entities updated in place by a small RealityKit System that reads live entity positions (entities are Observable in visionOS 26), instead of deleting/recreating all edge and port ModelEntities every reconcile pass — eliminating per-frame mesh/material churn.

Add accessibility from the start: AccessibilityComponent (label/value/traits/.activate systemAction) on every interactive entity, VoiceOver Direct Gesture support with AccessibilityNotification.Announcement for merges/links/dismissals, and verify all actions are reachable via Dwell Control. Keep the volume in the Shared Space (no immersive space) so the planner coexists with other apps.

Adopt the architectural separation the platform now affords: SwiftUI renders content via ViewAttachmentComponent (inline in visionOS 26, which also removes the make-time attachment race); RealityKit Systems + Components own behavior (ManipulationComponent for transform, GestureComponent for taps, HoverEffectComponent for highlight, a custom EdgeSystem for derived edges, a custom LayoutSystem for auto-flow); the @Observable view model stays the single source of data truth and is written only on gesture-end, never read-back-asserted every frame.

research.topic: Cross-platform XR/VR UI architecture patterns for complex, scalable spatial UIs (mapped to a SwiftUI+RealityKit visionOS task planner)
research.summary: Every mature XR UI stack converges on the same shape: a layered pipeline (data/state -> interaction -> layout -> scene-graph placement -> visual render) with ONE explicit transform owner at each instant, and strict separation of interaction logic from visual presentation from data. MRTK3 is the clearest statement: interactables are "pure state and interaction" that render nothing; a separate "visual driver" (StateVisualizer) observes that state to produce feedback; and the interactor's attachTransform "IS the interaction logic," so the manipulated object never cares which input produced the pose. Unity XRI formalizes a Manager-as-intermediary running a deterministic per-frame loop (validate -> exit events -> enter events, interactor-before-interactable) over the four states Hover/Select/Focus/Activate. Meta's Interaction SDK splits the same way (Interactor+Interactable pair; Grabbable/Pointable carry transform logic). ECS-for-UI experience (Bevy/Leafwing, Flecs, Unity DOTS) shows retained-mode entities + change detection + a clean layout/render split scale well, but one-off UI callbacks, hierarchy traversal, and multi-entity widgets need first-class abstractions or they become boilerplate. The decisive finding: RealityKit is itself an ECS, and visionOS 26 (the deploy target) ships exactly the primitives that dissolve both known bugs — ManipulationComponent (visionOS 2.6+) owns the transform during a drag so the data model stops fighting it; Observable entities plus the rule "never mutate observed state inside RealityView.update" remove the reconcile feedback loop; and the hierarchical InputTargetComponent/CollisionComponent model plus per-entity HoverEffectComponent fix the swallowed-hover "x" control. The synthesis: stop driving transforms imperatively from a monolithic reconcile pass; move continuous concerns into RealityKit Systems keyed by custom Components, let ManipulationComponent own live drag, let SwiftUI attachments render visuals as state observers, and derive edges in a System instead of rebuilding them every update.

corePrinciples.principle: Separate interaction logic from visual presentation from data — the most repeated lesson across MRTK3, XRI, and Meta ISDK
corePrinciples.detail: MRTK3 states it bluntly: interactables are pure state and interaction; the interactable doesn't render any visual changes based on its internal state. Visual feedback is produced by a SEPARATE visual driver (StateVisualizer) that observes the interactable's state (IsGazeHovered, IsGrabSelected, Selectedness). The same PressableButton logic can drive a squishy ball, a flat trackpad, or a network event with zero changes. For our app: the card visuals (glass, jitter-on-hover, highlight) must be a function of an observed interaction-state value, NOT logic baked into reconcile. Today reconcile both decides state and mutates visuals — the exact MRTK2 mistake MRTK3 retired.
corePrinciples.source: MRTK3 Interactables architecture + State Visualizer (Microsoft Learn)

corePrinciples.principle: Exactly one authority owns an entity's transform at any instant; ownership transfers explicitly between data-model, live-gesture, and animation
corePrinciples.detail: This is the root of bug #1. In XRI/MRTK the interactor's attachTransform owns the pose during manipulation; the data model only reads it. In visionOS 26, ManipulationComponent makes this native: during manipulation the component owns transform updates. Constraints are applied by subscribing to ManipulationEvents.DidUpdateTransform and writing the clamped value back inside that callback; the data model never re-asserts position mid-drag. General rule (WWDC25 274 boundary guidance): a transform is owned by a System/ManipulationComponent during interaction, by SwiftUI-driven animation during a transition, or by the data projection at rest — and writes from one owner must be suppressed while another owns it. The current code has no ownership concept: reconcile re-asserts the stored position on EVERY pass, fighting the live gesture.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 Session 274; Step Into Vision Manipulation deep dive; MRTK3 Interactor architecture (attachTransform)

corePrinciples.principle: Never mutate observed state inside the reactive update closure — the canonical feedback loop, and precisely our drag snap-back
corePrinciples.detail: Apple (WWDC25 274): to avoid an infinite loop, don't modify observed state within your update closure; you may modify entities you're not observing. Safe write sites: custom RealityKit Systems, gesture closures (run on input, not body evaluation), and the make/setup closure. RealityView.update is NOT a game tick — it runs only when referenced state changes, so writing observed state there schedules another update. Our reconcile() does exactly the forbidden thing: it runs on every Observable change AND re-sets card.position from the stale view-model each pass. The @State draggingID guard fails because gesture-set @State isn't reliably visible to the next update closure. The fix is structural: move transform authority out of update entirely.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 Session 274; Step Into Vision update-closure; Apple Developer Forums 733020

corePrinciples.principle: The layered pipeline state -> interaction -> layout -> scene-graph -> render, with an explicit handoff at each boundary
corePrinciples.detail: Bevy's ECS GUI (Leafwing) decouples layout (taffy/flexbox produces size/position) from a separate render pass from a separate picking system that maps pointer events to targets — enabling swapping layout strategies without touching rendering. This validates our existing pure SpatialLayoutEngine and argues for keeping it strictly nonisolated, downstream of state and upstream of placement. Boundary discipline: the layout engine outputs target poses for AUTO-FLOW entities only; hand-moved/manipulated entities are excluded (owned by the gesture/manipulation layer); placement writes layout output to an entity only when no higher-priority owner holds it.
corePrinciples.source: Leafwing ECS-backed GUI framework; MRTK3; Unity XRI architecture

corePrinciples.principle: A Manager/loop as deterministic intermediary between input sources and targets, with ordered phases and a four-state model (Hover, Select, Focus, Activate)
corePrinciples.detail: Unity XRI's Interaction Manager acts as an intermediary between Interactors and Interactables, running a deterministic per-frame loop: validate existing states -> dispatch exit events -> dispatch enter events, with interactors always notified before interactables. Targets are prioritized (closest first) via pluggable Target filters, and Interaction Groups allow only one member to interact at once with continuity bias. The four states are a proven vocabulary. Our app currently disambiguates interaction by PARSING entity names (ctl::role::ownerId, edge::) inside gesture handlers — fragile and unscalable. The mature pattern is typed components/roles + a small mediator, not string parsing.
corePrinciples.source: Unity XR Interaction Toolkit Architecture @3.3; Meta Interaction SDK lifecycle

corePrinciples.principle: Retained-mode scene entities + change detection (dirty flags/observers) beat immediate-mode rebuild-everything for complex persistent spatial scenes
corePrinciples.detail: Retained mode lets the library transfer to the renderer only data that changed and perform occlusion culling. RealityKit is retained-mode ECS: a System calls a single update method per frame over entities matched by EntityQuery, reading per-entity Component state. The anti-pattern our code commits is immediate-mode-in-a-retained-world: it deletes and re-adds ALL edges + ports from scratch every reconcile pass and rebuilds the x control each pass. Retained approach: create entities once, mutate only changed components, query-and-update via Systems, and let RealityKit diff. visionOS 26 Observable entities make the reverse direction (entity -> SwiftUI) reactive without polling.
corePrinciples.source: Microsoft Learn Retained vs Immediate Mode; RealityKit ECS; MRTK3 StateVisualizer (polling noted as a perf compromise)

corePrinciples.principle: Make input/collision hierarchy explicit; hover/highlight/focus must drive something the renderer actually shows
corePrinciples.detail: Root of bug #2. InputTargetComponent and CollisionComponent are HIERARCHICAL: descendants with collision components can be targeted for gestures — but a parent collision box that encloses a child (our x is only +0.006 z inside a depth max(z,0.03) parent box) means the parent's hit region overlaps the child, so input/hover resolves ambiguously and the parent swallows it. Fix: give the child its own non-overlapping collision shape that protrudes beyond the parent (or carve the parent's collision), and give each interactive sub-entity its own InputTargetComponent + HoverEffectComponent. A SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside an attachment rootView highlights only if that attachment entity is itself the eye-tracking target; the reliable native highlight is HoverEffectComponent on the entity. For playful jitter, drive a transform/shader effect from an observed hover state in a System or via Entity.animate, not a SwiftUI scaleEffect the parent swallows.
corePrinciples.source: visionOS component docs (AppleInsider, Step Into Vision); WWDC24 Explore game input in visionOS (10094); AGENTS.md visionOS 26

corePrinciples.principle: attachTransform / inversion of control: write new INPUT sources, not new per-object interaction code
corePrinciples.detail: MRTK3: philosophically the attachTransform IS the interaction logic; always prefer writing a new interactor with new attachTransform logic over rewriting interactables. Result: every interactable automatically supports every input modality. For us: a card should not contain modality-specific drag math; it should expose a single grabbed-pose input that a drag gesture, a future hand-tracking grab, or a programmatic move all feed. ManipulationComponent already does this on visionOS 26, so adopting it is the highest-leverage move.
corePrinciples.source: MRTK3 Interactor architecture (AttachTransform and Inversion of Control)

corePrinciples.principle: Unidirectional data flow (MVU/MVVM) for the SCENE, with a single source of truth and messages as the only mutation path
corePrinciples.detail: MVU/Elm: Model -> View renders -> user input -> Update produces new Model — unidirectional flow makes state predictable and reduces bugs. For a 3D scene: SpatialEntity rows (data) -> layout/derive -> entities (view) -> gestures emit intents -> view-model reduces intents to data writes -> persistence. The key is that gestures emit INTENTS (.moved(id,pose), .dismissed(id), .connected(a,b)) rather than directly mutating both entity and stored position in two places. Our Observable view-model is the right vehicle; it needs a disciplined intent/reduce boundary so transform writes have one path and one owner.
corePrinciples.source: Model-View-Update / Elm Architecture writeups; Andre Staltz Unidirectional UI Architectures

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationComponent / ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(_:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Native drag/translate/single- and two-hand rotate/scale with built-in coordinate handling, inertia, hand-off, audio. During manipulation the component OWNS the entity transform, eliminating manual delta math and the data-model fight.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Primary fix for bug #1 (drag snap-back). Replace the top-level translation DragGesture for moving cards. Clamp to VolumeMetrics bounds by subscribing to ManipulationEvents.DidUpdateTransform and writing the clamped pose back there; persist on WillEnd/WillRelease. Set releaseBehavior=.stay so cards remain where dropped.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Requires CollisionComponent + InputTargetComponent (configureEntity adds them). Disable unwanted rotation/scale via dynamics.primaryRotationBehavior=.none etc. Known intermittent bug where translation stops app-wide until device restart — keep a manual DragGesture fallback behind a flag. CRITICAL: do not also re-assert position from reconcile/update while a manipulation owns the entity.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 2.6 (configureEntity + events); fully featured in visionOS 26

keyAPIs.name: Observable Entity / Component (RealityKit) + RealityView update-closure rule
keyAPIs.purpose: Entities and components are Observable starting visionOS 26, so SwiftUI reactively tracks transform/component changes — and you must NOT write observed state in update or you create a feedback loop.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Use entity observation to drive SwiftUI HUD/minimap/derived labels from live poses. Use the rule to delete the snap-back: stop writing card.position in the update/reconcile pass; write transforms only in gesture closures, ManipulationComponent callbacks, or custom Systems.
keyAPIs.gotchas: update is not a per-frame tick; it fires on observed-state change. Split large views into smaller self-contained ones so unrelated entity changes don't re-evaluate the whole body. Accessing observable props in make/setup does NOT create a dependency (safe); accessing them in update DOES.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 26 (entity/component observation); update-closure semantics since visionOS 1

keyAPIs.name: RealityKit System + EntityQuery / QueryPredicate + custom Component
keyAPIs.purpose: The retained-mode replacement for the monolithic reconcile pass. A System's update(context:) runs once per frame over entities matched by an EntityQuery on a marker/data Component; it reads/writes component state. Correct home for continuous behavior (edge redraw, hover jitter, layout settling).
keyAPIs.whenToUse: EdgeSystem querying entities tagged with an EdgeEndpointComponent updates only edge geometry whose endpoints moved (instead of deleting+re-adding ALL edges every pass). HighlightSystem applies jitter/scale from an observed HoverStateComponent. LayoutApplySystem writes auto-flow target poses ONLY to entities not currently owned by manipulation. Register components once with Component.registerComponent() and systems with System.registerSystem().
keyAPIs.gotchas: Keep component logic to validation/initialization only (Apple guidance); behavior lives in Systems. Systems run on MainActor by default under Swift 6.2 default isolation; keep the pure layout engine nonisolated and call it FROM the system. Query with updatingSystemWhen:.rendering for per-frame.

keyAPIs.name: InputTargetComponent (hierarchical) + CollisionComponent + HoverEffectComponent
keyAPIs.purpose: InputTargetComponent marks an entity (and hierarchically its descendants) as gesture-eligible; CollisionComponent shapes define the actual hit-test regions; HoverEffectComponent gives native eye-tracking highlight.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Fix bug #2: give the x control its own CollisionComponent shaped to protrude clearly beyond the parent card's collision (or carve the parent), its own InputTargetComponent, and a native HoverEffectComponent rather than a SwiftUI-internal hover effect the parent swallows.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Parent collision enclosing a child causes ambiguous/swallowed hits — the +0.006 z offset is far too small vs the depth max(z,0.03) parent box. There is no automatic input-target precedence beyond the hierarchy; you must shape collisions to disambiguate. HoverEffectComponent supports .spotlight/.highlight styles in visionOS 2+; pair with InputTargetComponent or it does nothing.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 1 (all three); HoverEffectComponent style options expanded in visionOS 2

keyAPIs.name: GestureComponent + ViewAttachmentComponent + PresentationComponent (entity-attached SwiftUI)
keyAPIs.purpose: visionOS 26 lets you attach SwiftUI gestures directly to an entity (GestureComponent), attach a SwiftUI rootView as the entity's visual (ViewAttachmentComponent), and present popovers/sheets from an entity (PresentationComponent) — keeping SwiftUI-renders-content / RealityKit-places-it while removing name-parsing dispatch.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Replace the two global .targetedToAnyEntity gestures + string-prefix disambiguation with per-entity GestureComponents (tap on card opens edit, tap on x dismisses) so entity identity is intrinsic, not parsed. Keep ViewAttachmentComponent for all card/panel/note visuals. Use PresentationComponent for the edit sheet where appropriate.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Entities targeted by GestureComponent still need InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent. Gesture values are in the entity's coordinate space by default. ViewAttachmentComponent replaces the visionOS 1 builder-based attachments API.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 26 (GestureComponent, ViewAttachmentComponent as a component, PresentationComponent)

keyAPIs.name: Unified coordinate conversion (CoordinateSpace3D) + Entity.animate / content.animate
keyAPIs.purpose: CoordinateSpace3D converts poses between SwiftUI and RealityKit (points<->meters, axis normalization). Entity.animate / content.animate drive RealityKit Transform/color changes with SwiftUI animation curves.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Use CoordinateSpace3D instead of manual value.convert(translation3D,.local,.scene) bookkeeping. Use Entity.animate for the smooth-animation requirement: animate a card to its layout slot, animate dismiss/return-to-tray, animate the jitter — owned by the animation authority, distinct from gesture/data authority.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Animating a transform is itself a transform-ownership claim — suppress data/layout writes for that entity until the animation completes. Use interactiveSpring for grab-handoff feel.

keyAPIs.name: MRTK3 StateVisualizer pattern (concept to reimplement in Swift) + StatefulInteractable.Selectedness
keyAPIs.purpose: A reusable visual driver mapping interaction state (hover/select/focus + analog Selectedness) to visual feedback, decoupled from interaction logic. Selectedness models analog/variable selection (pinchedness, press depth) rather than binary.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Borrow the concept, not the code: build a Swift visual-driver layer where each card's glass opacity, highlight, and jitter are pure functions of an observed InteractionState component. This is the scalable design-system seam — new entity kinds get visuals by declaring state->style mappings, not by editing reconcile.
keyAPIs.gotchas: MRTK3 currently POLLS state (acknowledged perf compromise) and plans to move to event listeners; prefer observation/events from the start (visionOS 26 Observable entities make this free). Don't put the state->visual mapping in the interactable/data layer.
keyAPIs.minOS: Engine-agnostic concept; implement on visionOS 26 with Observable components

antiPatterns.pattern: Re-asserting every entity's stored transform on every reactive update/reconcile pass (immediate-mode in a retained-mode engine)
antiPatterns.why: Direct cause of drag snap-back. Violates transform-ownership (data model overwrites the live gesture) and the don't-mutate-observed-state-in-update rule (feedback loop). Retained-mode engines expect you to mutate only what changed; re-asserting everything each pass also destroys frame-coherent state (selection, in-flight animation) and scales O(n) per change. Replace with: create once, mutate changed components, and let ManipulationComponent/Systems own transforms.

antiPatterns.pattern: Disambiguating interaction by parsing entity names (ctl::role::ownerId, edge::) inside global .targetedToAnyEntity gestures
antiPatterns.why: String-typed dispatch is unscalable and error-prone; every new control kind grows a parser branch with no compiler help. Mature stacks use typed Interactor/Interactable roles or per-entity GestureComponents (visionOS 26) so identity and behavior are intrinsic. It also couples all interaction into two megafunctions, the opposite of the single-responsibility seams MRTK3/XRI rely on.

antiPatterns.pattern: Deleting and re-adding ALL edges + ports from scratch every update pass
antiPatterns.why: Pure immediate-mode rebuild — discards retained-mode benefits (diffing, culling, stable identity), thrashes the entity graph, and scales badly as the graph grows. Edges are derived data with stable endpoints; model them as entities owned by an EdgeSystem that updates only geometry whose endpoint poses changed (EntityQuery on EdgeEndpointComponent).

antiPatterns.pattern: Baking visual feedback (highlight, jitter, color, press) into the same code that computes interaction state
antiPatterns.why: The MRTK2 mistake MRTK3 explicitly retired (interactables are pure state and interaction, render no visuals). Coupling makes visuals un-reskinnable, un-testable, and forces interaction edits whenever design changes. Split into a state layer (observed) and a visual-driver layer (functions of state). Directly relevant to the requested scalable design/component system.

antiPatterns.pattern: Relying on a SwiftUI .hoverEffect{ scaleEffect } inside a ViewAttachmentComponent rootView for a child control while a parent CollisionComponent encloses the child
antiPatterns.why: Bug #2's root: the parent's hit region overlaps the child (only +0.006 z inside a depth max(z,0.03) box), so eye-tracking/hover resolves to the parent and the child's SwiftUI hover never fires. Native HoverEffectComponent on the child entity (with its own non-overlapping CollisionComponent + InputTargetComponent) is the reliable mechanism; SwiftUI-internal hover effects don't substitute for RealityKit input targeting.

antiPatterns.pattern: Using a view @State flag (draggingID) checked inside the RealityView update closure to skip the dragged entity
antiPatterns.why: State mutated inside a RealityKit gesture .onChanged is not reliably visible to the update closure before the next reconcile pass, so the guard races and fails (already observed). The problem is architectural, not a missing flag: remove transform-writing from update entirely and let the gesture/ManipulationComponent own the transform, so there is nothing to guard.

antiPatterns.pattern: Putting continuous/per-frame behavior in the RealityView update closure (treating it like a game tick)
antiPatterns.why: update fires on observed-state change, not per frame, so continuous logic there is both incorrect and a feedback-loop hazard. Guidance: for continuous behaviors implement a custom System, not the update closure. Systems run once per frame over EntityQuery matches with proper isolation.

recommendationsForOurApp: Adopt a 5-layer pipeline with explicit ownership at each seam: (1) Data: SpatialEntity rows from tRPC; (2) Derive/Layout: the existing pure nonisolated SpatialLayoutEngine produces target poses for AUTO-FLOW entities only; (3) Interaction: per-entity roles + ManipulationComponent/GestureComponent emit INTENTS; (4) Placement/Scene: a small set of RealityKit Systems write transforms, but a transform is written by exactly one owner at a time (manipulation > animation > layout > data); (5) Render: SwiftUI attachments as pure observers of interaction state. This is the MRTK3/XRI/Bevy consensus applied to RealityKit.

Fix bug #1 at the root by deleting position-writing from reconcile/update and adopting ManipulationComponent (visionOS 2.6+) for card movement. Clamp to VolumeMetrics in a ManipulationEvents.DidUpdateTransform subscription; persist on WillEnd. Keep releaseBehavior=.stay. This makes grab-and-drag the near-trivial thing the developer expects.

Fix bug #2 at the root via hierarchical collision shaping: give the x control its own CollisionComponent that protrudes clearly beyond the card's collision box (or carve the parent box), its own InputTargetComponent, and a native HoverEffectComponent (not a SwiftUI-internal hover effect). Make jitter-on-highlight a transform animation driven from an observed HoverStateComponent via Entity.animate or a HighlightSystem.

Replace the monolithic reconcile(content:attachments:) with: a one-time entity factory (create card/control/edge entities once), an EntitySyncSystem that adds/removes entities when SpatialEntity rows change (diff, don't rebuild), an EdgeSystem that updates only moved edges (EntityQuery on EdgeEndpointComponent), and a LayoutApplySystem that writes auto-flow poses only to entities no other authority owns. Register custom Components/Systems once at setup.

Introduce a typed interaction/visual seam instead of name-parsing: define entity roles as components (TaskNodeRole, DismissControlRole, EdgeRole, etc.), attach per-entity GestureComponents (visionOS 26) so taps route by identity, and build an MRTK3-style visual driver where each card's glass/opacity/highlight is a pure function of an observed InteractionState. New entity kinds declare state->style and never touch dispatch code.

Enforce unidirectional flow in the @Observable view-model: gestures emit intents (.moved, .dismissed, .connected, .linked, .toggledVolume); a single reduce path turns intents into data writes and persistence. No code path may write both an entity transform and the stored position in two places — that duplication is what made the snap-back possible.

Keep the layout engine pure and nonisolated (Foundation/simd only) and call it FROM a MainActor System; do not let Systems' default MainActor isolation leak into the engine. This preserves the unit-tested deterministic layout while satisfying SWIFT_DEFAULT_ACTOR_ISOLATION=MainActor.

Use Entity.animate/content.animate with interactiveSpring/easeInOut for all transitions (snap-to-slot, dismiss-to-tray, volume expand/collapse), and treat each animation as a temporary transform-ownership claim that suppresses layout/data writes for that entity until it completes — preventing a new class of snap-back during animations.

Adopt the four-state vocabulary (Hover, Select, Focus, Activate) for entity interaction even though RealityKit has no Manager — a tiny mediator/reducer suffices. This gives a stable, testable model for highlight, selection, connect/link modes, and future near/far hand-tracking interactors without per-modality branching, mirroring XRI's attachTransform inversion-of-control.

Validate everything with xcodebuild -scheme TaskPlannerVision -destination 'generic/platform=visionOS Simulator' (trust it over SourceKit). Gate ManipulationComponent behind a fallback path due to the known intermittent translation-stops-working bug, and unit-test the layout engine and the intent->reduce logic with Swift tests where the VM logic is nonisolated/extractable.

citations.title: Interactor architecture — MRTK3 (Microsoft Learn): attachTransform IS the interaction logic; inversion of control; good citizens of XRI
Interactables — MRTK3 (Microsoft Learn): interactables are pure state and interaction; strict separation of state and visuals; visual drivers; variable Selectedness
State Visualizer — MRTK3 (Microsoft Learn): visual driver that observes interactable state; perf/animation notes; planned move to event listeners
Architecture — Unity XR Interaction Toolkit @3.3: Interaction Manager as intermediary; Hover/Select/Focus/Activate; ordered update phases; target prioritization
Interactor/Interactable Lifecycle — Meta Interaction SDK (Meta for Developers): Interactor+Interactable pairing, states; Grabbable/Pointable carry transform logic

Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit — WWDC25 Session 274 (Apple): Observable entities; GestureComponent; ManipulationComponent; CoordinateSpace3D; Entity.animate; don't modify observed state in the update closure
Deep Dive into Manipulation on visionOS (Step Into Vision): ManipulationComponent owns the transform; configureEntity; ManipulationEvents; releaseBehavior; dynamics; min OS visionOS 2.6
RealityKit Basics: update closure (Step Into Vision): update is not a per-frame tick; it fires on referenced-state change
So you want to build an ECS-backed GUI framework (Leafwing Studios): ECS-for-UI pain points; layout (taffy) vs render vs picking separation; retained vs immediate
Retained Mode Versus Immediate Mode — Win32 (Microsoft Learn): retained mode sends only changed data; enables culling/diffing
Understanding the modular architecture of RealityKit (Apple Developer Documentation): Entity/Component/System roles; limit component logic; systems process per frame via EntityQuery
Implementing systems for entities in a scene (Apple Developer Documentation): System.update(context:) once per frame; EntityQuery/QueryPredicate; registering systems/components
Unidirectional User Interface Architectures (Andre Staltz): MVU/Elm/Flux/Redux unidirectional flow taxonomy and tradeoffs

AGENTS.md for visionOS 26 & Swift 6.2 (tomkrikorian gist): use custom Systems not the update-closure for continuous behavior; draggable entities need CollisionComponent+InputTargetComponent; always add hoverEffect; MainActor default isolation
Explore game input in visionOS — WWDC24 Session 10094 (Apple): InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent hierarchy and hit-testing for gesture targeting

research.topic: Scalable Spatial Design System and Reusable Component Library for visionOS 3D UI (Task-Planner Cards, Panels, Controls, Edges)
research.summary: The current scene's two root problems are architecture gaps, not gesture bugs. (1) It re-asserts every entity transform inside the RealityView update closure each pass, fighting live drags and SwiftUI Observation. WWDC25 is explicit that the update closure is an extension of the view body and must not own continuous transforms; transforms belong to gestures, a custom RealityKit System, or Entity.animate(), and on visionOS 26 entities are Observable so SwiftUI can read state without re-pushing it. (2) The custom x control does not highlight because its scale comes from a SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside a ViewAttachment whose parent card has an enclosing CollisionComponent only +0.006z away; hierarchical hover on a RealityKit entity is the job of HoverEffectComponent (spotlight/highlight/shader, visionOS 2+) plus a non-overlapping child collision shape, not SwiftUI content-hover inside an occluded attachment. The single biggest lever is visionOS 26 ManipulationComponent (and SwiftUI .manipulable), giving system-owned grab/translate/rotate/scale with WillBegin/DidUpdateTransform/WillEnd events for persistence, making grab-and-drag near-trivial. For the design system, Apple HIG plus a Unity MRTK3 style token/theme model maps cleanly to Swift: one nonisolated token enum (meters spacing/elevation, corner radius, glass material levels, tint, motion durations/easings, hover styles) feeding composable SwiftUI card primitives, with a per-entity state machine (rest/hover/selected/dragging/disabled) expressed as a RealityKit Component driven by a System rather than parsed from entity names. Edges/ports become a derived, diffed System, not a per-pass teardown. Tokens + component state machine + ManipulationComponent + HoverEffectComponent + Systems-driven reconcile + Observation is the production-grade foundation that scales.

corePrinciples.principle: SwiftUI renders content; RealityKit owns transforms; the RealityView update closure owns neither continuously
corePrinciples.detail: WWDC25 session 274 states the update closure is treated as an extension of the view body, so mutating observed state or re-asserting transforms there causes loops and fights gestures. Safe places to mutate an entity transform: the gesture closure (live drag), a custom System.update(context:), the one-shot make closure, or Entity.animate(). Root fix for drag snap-back: stop re-writing card.position from the stale view-model value every pass.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 Better together: SwiftUI and RealityKit (session 274)

corePrinciples.principle: On visionOS 26 entities are Observable; read entity state into SwiftUI, do not push SwiftUI state onto entities every frame
corePrinciples.detail: entity.observable exposes position/scale/rotation/children/components to Swift Observation, so SwiftUI re-renders when an entity changes. The data flow inverts: the gesture/System mutates the entity transform (single source of truth for placement), persistence reads it on release, SwiftUI chrome observes it. Removes the stale-position-during-drag bug class.
corePrinciples.source: Apple docs Entity.Observable; WWDC25 session 274

corePrinciples.principle: Continuous/derived scene behavior belongs in a RealityKit System, not the update closure
corePrinciples.detail: Primary and community sources converge: do not use the RealityView update closure for continuous behavior; use a custom System conforming to RealityKit.System, running one update(context: SceneUpdateContext) per frame over EntityQuery+QueryPredicate matches, reading/writing components directly. Correct home for edge/port redraw (diff vs teardown-rebuild) and the per-entity visual state machine.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC23 session 10080; swiftorbit.io ECS articles; tomkrikorian visionOS 26 AGENTS.md

corePrinciples.principle: visionOS 26 ManipulationComponent makes grab/drag/rotate/scale a system-owned, single-line capability
corePrinciples.detail: ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(entity, hoverEffect:allowedInputTypes:collisionShapes:) adds Manipulation + Collision + InputTarget + HoverEffect. During manipulation the component owns the transform (no ownership conflict); persist via ManipulationEvents.WillEnd/WillRelease. releaseBehavior=.stay keeps the final pose vs .reset. It auto-handles user re-orientation mid-drag, the exact coordinate-space complexity the manual DragGesture+convert code wrestles with. SwiftUI equivalent: .manipulable(operations:inertia:).
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 session 274; stepinto.vision Deep Dive into Manipulation on visionOS

corePrinciples.principle: Hover/highlight/focus is a RealityKit component concern; parent collision must not occlude child controls
corePrinciples.detail: HoverEffectComponent (visionOS 1+) supports .spotlight(SpotlightHoverEffectStyle(color:strength:)), .highlight(HighlightHoverEffectStyle(color:strength:)) uniform mesh tint (good for playful jitter/glow on select), and .shader(...) driven by a ShaderGraph HoverState node whose intensity (0 to 1 on gaze) animates emissive/scale (highlight and shader: visionOS 2+). The x control fails because its parent card CollisionComponent (depth max(z,0.03)) encloses the child only +0.006z away, so gaze/taps resolve to the parent. Fix: own forward-offset non-overlapping collision + own HoverEffectComponent.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC24 sessions 10103 + 10152; HoverEffectComponent docs

corePrinciples.principle: Centralize design decisions as tokens (MRTK3/Material-3 pattern, adapted to meters)
corePrinciples.detail: MRTK3 data-binding/theming themes whole hierarchies from one profile; Material 3 formalizes tokens as single source of truth. Apple HIG seeds spatial token values: ~60pt min interactive target, 44pt standard / 28pt mini buttons, 16pt min button spacing, 4pt padding to avoid hover-effect overlap, ornament 20pt window overlap, concentric corner radius = innerRadius + padding, glass primary material with vibrancy tiers and white text by default. In Swift: one nonisolated enum SpatialToken (Foundation/simd only), testable and shared with the layout engine.
corePrinciples.source: Apple HIG / WWDC23 session 10076; MRTK3 Data Binding and Theming docs; Material Design 3 tokens

corePrinciples.principle: Composition over inheritance; every interactive entity carries Collision + InputTarget + Hover; theme by variant not name-parsing
corePrinciples.detail: visionOS 26 AGENTS.md: prefer composition over inheritance, create custom components implementing Component and Codable; all draggable entities must have CollisionComponent and InputTargetComponent; always add a hover effect to custom interactive elements. Gesture disambiguation should read typed components (a SpatialRoleComponent with kind and ownerId), not parse ctl::role::id strings; this scales and is testable.
corePrinciples.source: tomkrikorian visionOS 26 and Swift 6.2 AGENTS.md; WWDC25 session 274 GestureComponent

corePrinciples.principle: Animate transforms declaratively with Entity.animate()/move(to:); do not snap
corePrinciples.detail: visionOS 26 Entity.animate(.bouncy) { entity.position = ... } implicitly animates Transform/Audio volume/Model and Light color via a SwiftUI animation curve; pre-26 fallback is entity.move(to:relativeTo:duration:timingFunction:) or FromToByAnimation + AnimationResource.generate + playAnimation. Use for return-to-tray, expand/collapse pop-out, and selection jitter, with durations/easings tokenized.
corePrinciples.source: WWDC25 session 274; createwithswift.com Play an animation in RealityKit

keyAPIs.name: ManipulationComponent / ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(_:hoverEffect:allowedInputTypes:collisionShapes:)
keyAPIs.purpose: System-owned grab/translate/rotate/scale with handoff, inertia, audio; replaces manual DragGesture + value.convert coordinate math.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Make cards, notes, workflow volumes draggable. Set releaseBehavior=.stay; persist transform in ManipulationEvents.WillEnd. Disable scaling/rotation per-kind via dynamics for translate-only cards.
keyAPIs.gotchas: During manipulation the COMPONENT owns the transform; do not also write position from the view model (this is the snap-back cause). Subscribe to DidUpdateTransform only to clamp to VolumeMetrics. Reported sim quirk: translation can stop app-wide until device restart.

keyAPIs.name: .manipulable(operations:inertia:) (SwiftUI)
keyAPIs.purpose: SwiftUI-level manipulation for Model3D/attachment-backed views, mirroring ManipulationComponent for the content-renders-in-SwiftUI paradigm.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: When a card is presented primarily as a SwiftUI view and you want manipulation without entity-level component wiring.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Operations: .translation, .primaryRotation, .secondaryRotation, .scaling. Prefer entity-level ManipulationComponent when you need WillEnd events for tRPC persistence and clamping.

keyAPIs.name: HoverEffectComponent(.spotlight/.highlight/.shader)
keyAPIs.purpose: Gaze-driven visual feedback owned by RealityKit per entity: spotlight (default), highlight (uniform mesh tint, color/strength), shader (ShaderGraph HoverState intensity).
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Every interactive entity: cards (subtle spotlight), x and port controls (highlight/scale), selected state (highlight tint via tokens). Drives playful jitter via shader intensity or animate-on-hover.
keyAPIs.gotchas: SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside a ViewAttachment will not visibly scale when the parent CollisionComponent occludes the child; that is bug #2. Use entity-level HoverEffectComponent and give the child its own non-overlapping collision shape offset enough in +z to clear the parent box.

keyAPIs.name: RealityKit System + EntityQuery (System protocol, SceneUpdateContext)
keyAPIs.purpose: Per-frame query-driven processing for all entities of interest in one update call; correct home for derived edges/ports and the visual state machine.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Replace drawEdges() teardown-rebuild with an EdgeSystem that diffs/repositions existing edge entities against live positions; add a NodeStateSystem mapping a StateComponent to hover/selection visuals.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Apple guidance: do not do continuous work in the RealityView update closure; Systems run off the render loop and do not entangle with SwiftUI body evaluation.

keyAPIs.name: Entity.observable (Entity.Observable)
keyAPIs.purpose: Observation-framework view of an entity transform/components so SwiftUI re-renders on change without pushing state back into the entity.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Any SwiftUI chrome that must reflect live entity position (minimap, selection HUD, persistence trigger) reads entity.observable instead of a mirrored @Observable position field.
keyAPIs.gotchas: Lets you delete position-mirroring in the view model that causes stale-position fights. Pre-26 you must mirror manually but still must not re-assert during drag.

keyAPIs.name: Entity.animate(_:body:) / content.animate { } / Entity.move(to:relativeTo:duration:timingFunction:)
keyAPIs.purpose: Declarative implicit animation of entity Transform (and audio/model/light color) using SwiftUI curves; move(to:) is the pre-26 fallback with explicit easing.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Return-to-tray, expand/collapse pop-out, selection jitter, snap-to-clamp. Centralize curve/duration in the token file.
keyAPIs.gotchas: move(to:) timing functions: .linear/.easeIn/.easeOut/.easeInOut (plus cubicBezier). Returns AnimationPlaybackController. Do not animate inside the update closure unless wrapped in content.animate within a state-gated branch.

keyAPIs.name: ViewAttachmentComponent / PresentationComponent / GestureComponent (visionOS 26)
keyAPIs.purpose: ViewAttachmentComponent embeds a SwiftUI view on an entity (no builder); PresentationComponent presents popovers/sheets from an entity; GestureComponent attaches a SwiftUI gesture to a specific entity.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Cards/notes/panels as attachments; per-entity tap (edit) and dismiss as GestureComponent, removing the global tap gesture + name-parsing. Edit sheets via PresentationComponent anchored to the card.
keyAPIs.minOS: visionOS 26 (component-form attachments, PresentationComponent, GestureComponent); ViewAttachment builder: visionOS 1
keyAPIs.gotchas: GestureComponent still requires InputTargetComponent + CollisionComponent. Per-entity gestures eliminate the brittle prefix-parsing dispatcher.

keyAPIs.name: Glass materials: .glassBackgroundEffect() and Material levels (.ultraThin/.thin/.regular/.thick)
keyAPIs.purpose: Adaptive semi-transparent glass surfaces with vibrancy for elegant cards/panels; material level = an elevation token.
keyAPIs.whenToUse: Token material level per tier: panels = regular, cards = regular/thin, transient controls = thin/ultraThin. Use .glassBackgroundEffect() for spatially-aware glass on attachments.
keyAPIs.gotchas: HIG: avoid stacking lighter materials on lighter; prefer white text/symbols and vibrancy tiers (primary/secondary/tertiary) over custom colors for legibility on glass.

antiPatterns.pattern: Re-asserting card.position from the view-model in the RealityView update closure every pass
antiPatterns.why: Root cause of drag snap-back. The update closure is an extension of the view body; the stored VM position is stale until release, so it yanks the card back. Transform ownership must be the gesture/ManipulationComponent during interaction; persistence reads the entity on release.

antiPatterns.pattern: Using @State draggingID checked inside reconcile to skip the dragged card
antiPatterns.why: @State mutated in a RealityKit gesture .onChanged is not reliably visible to the update closure before the next pass (already observed). The fix is architectural: do not have an update-pass that owns transforms at all.

antiPatterns.pattern: SwiftUI .hoverEffect { effect.scaleEffect } inside a ViewAttachment for a control under a larger parent collision box
antiPatterns.why: Root cause of the x not highlighting. The parent card CollisionComponent (depth max(z,0.03)) encloses the child only +0.006z away, so gaze/hover resolve to the parent and the child SwiftUI hover never activates. Use entity-level HoverEffectComponent + a non-overlapping child collision shape.

antiPatterns.pattern: Tearing down and re-adding ALL edges + ports from scratch every reconcile pass
antiPatterns.why: Wasteful and causes flicker/GC churn as the graph grows; prevents animating edges. Replace with an EdgeSystem that diffs the derived edge set and only creates/removes/repositions changed line/port entities against live positions.

antiPatterns.pattern: Encoding control/edge identity in entity names (ctl::role::owner, edge::) and parsing them in the gesture handler
antiPatterns.why: Stringly-typed dispatch does not scale and is not testable. Replace with typed components (SpatialRoleComponent/StateComponent) and per-entity GestureComponent so role/owner are first-class data.

antiPatterns.pattern: Scattering meters/colors/durations/corner radii as magic numbers across views and the reconcile (0.006, 0.03, 0.07, 16, opacity 0.9)
antiPatterns.why: No single source of truth means inconsistency as components multiply. MRTK3/Material-3 practice and the existing VolumeMetrics precedent argue for one nonisolated token file feeding every component.

antiPatterns.pattern: Generating CollisionComponent from card.visualBounds(recursive:) every pass
antiPatterns.why: Documented in project memory as a recursive visualBounds feedback loop (bounds grow as children are added, inflating the box each pass). Compute collision from token-defined card dimensions, not measured recursive bounds.

antiPatterns.pattern: Hand-building primitive-mesh / 3D-text UI for cards and controls
antiPatterns.why: Already tried and rejected as unusable; violates the locked SwiftUI-renders-content paradigm. Keep all content as SwiftUI attachments; RealityKit only places, animates, and routes gestures.

recommendationsForOurApp: Create one token source of truth: a nonisolated enum SpatialTokens (Foundation/simd only, same isolation rule as SpatialLayoutEngine) holding meters spacing, z-elevation layers (card, control, edge, selection-lift), corner radii, material levels per tier, tint roles, motion durations/easings, and hover styles. Every card view, the collision sizing, and the layout engine read from it. Directly testable and ends magic numbers.

Adopt visionOS 26 ManipulationComponent for all movable kinds via ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(card, hoverEffect:.spotlight(...), collisionShapes:[tokenSizedBox]). Persist in ManipulationEvents.WillEnd by reading the entity transform and calling viewModel.endDrag; clamp to VolumeMetrics in DidUpdateTransform. Delete the manual DragGesture + value.convert + dragStart/draggingID logic. This single change fixes bug #1 at the root and makes grab-and-drag trivial.

Stop owning transforms in the update closure. Move scene reconcile to: (a) a one-shot make closure for initial add, (b) an entity-diffing add/remove step gated on the entity SET actually changing (not every observed change), and (c) RealityKit Systems for continuous/derived work. Read live positions via entity.observable instead of mirroring position in the view model; the VM keeps canonical data + persistence, not per-frame placement.

Build a reusable SpatialCard component abstraction with an explicit state machine: a NodeStateComponent with state rest/hover/selected/dragging/disabled plus a NodeStateSystem that maps state to visuals (HoverEffectComponent highlight tint from tokens, a small Entity.animate jitter/scale on hover, selection lift in +z). Cards, panels, controls, and workflow volumes are variants of the same primitive differing only by tokens/content, mirroring MRTK3 theming-by-profile.

Fix the x control as its own first-class entity: give it a token-sized collision box offset far enough in +z to clear the parent card box (and/or shrink the parent box depth), attach HoverEffectComponent(.highlight) + GestureComponent(TapGesture) so it gets its own gaze/tap, and render its visual as a SwiftUI attachment. This fixes bug #2 and removes the need for hierarchical collision filtering hacks. Apply the 4pt-padding HIG rule (in meters) so control and card hover regions do not overlap.

Replace name-parsing gesture dispatch with typed components: SpatialRoleComponent with kind and ownerId on controls/edges and per-entity GestureComponent for tap/edit/dismiss. The global TapGesture + controlRole(name:) string parsing goes away, making the interaction layer testable and scalable to new control types.

Convert edges/ports to an EdgeSystem that diffs the derived edge set (from TaskStep.dependsOn + links) against existing edge entities and only mutates deltas, repositioning via entity.observable positions. Token edge thickness/port radius/colors. Removes per-pass teardown and lets edges animate as nodes move.

Centralize glass/material as elevation tokens: panels = .regular glass, cards = .regular/.thin, transient controls = .thin/.ultraThin, with vibrancy tiers and white symbols per HIG. Animate state transitions (return-to-tray, expand/collapse pop-out, selection) with Entity.animate using token durations/easings (pre-26 fallback: move(to:timingFunction:)). Verify only via xcodebuild -scheme TaskPlannerVision (SourceKit false errors), and gate visionOS-26-only APIs since the target is 26.2.

gaps.title: reconcile re-asserts every card's stored position every update pass - root cause of drag snap-back
gaps.description: The update closure is the de-facto transform owner: each pass it overwrites every non-dragged card with the stale stored model position (only written on onEnded). Any observed change firing the closure mid-drag yanks the card back; a raced reconcile on release leaves the snap-back. WWDC25 274: never mutate observed entity state in the update closure.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneView.swift:246-250: if entity.id != draggingID then card.position = clamp(stored positionX/Y/Z); called from the update closure (25-26) which reads viewModel.renderedEntities (231).
gaps.bestPractice: Encode ownership (a TransformAuthorityComponent (model,gesture,animating) or visionOS 26 ManipulationComponent, which owns the transform during manipulation). Reconcile owns IDENTITY only, writing a transform once on first add or explicit relayout.
gaps.recommendedFix: Delete the re-assert at 246-250. Use ManipulationComponent.configureEntity + releaseBehavior stay, persist in WillEnd, clamp in DidUpdateTransform. If keeping the gesture, write the transform only in the gesture closure; reconcile places only when card.parent==nil.

gaps.title: State draggingID guard cannot reliably suppress the re-assert mid-drag
gaps.description: The only guard is a SwiftUI State var draggingID set in the gesture onChanged. State mutated in a gesture closure is not reliably visible to the update closure before the next reconcile (the developer's failed fix; update is body evaluation, not gesture timing), so the guard races and the card is re-stamped to its stale position.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneView.swift:20 @State var draggingID; set at 108 (onChanged); read at 246 (reconcile); cleared at 114 (onEnded).
gaps.bestPractice: Encode ownership where the update closure can reliably read it: a Component on the entity or an Observable view model field. With ManipulationComponent the component owns the transform, so there is nothing to guard.
gaps.recommendedFix: Delete draggingID (20, 108, 114, 246). With ManipulationComponent transfer is automatic; otherwise use an entity-local marker component or @Observable activeDragID. Removing the re-assert (gap 1) makes the guard moot.

gaps.title: Drag-end persistence writes the observed entities array, re-firing update on release
gaps.description: endDrag calls updateLocalPosition, mutating the Observable entities array and re-firing the update closure. draggingID is cleared to nil (114) BEFORE the async endDrag Task runs (118), so the next reconcile re-asserts card.position from the just-written model value; any clamp/float/relayout difference makes the card jump on release.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneView.swift:112-118: onEnded sets draggingID=nil (114) then Task endDrag (118). SpatialSceneViewModel.swift:353-357 endDrag to updateLocalPosition (344-349) writes entities[idx].positionX/Y/Z.
gaps.bestPractice: On release the entity transform is already authoritative; persist without re-pushing a model value onto the entity the same frame.
gaps.recommendedFix: Persist from ManipulationEvents WillEnd; stop reconcile re-asserting transforms (gap 1).

gaps.title: Edges and ports are deleted and fully rebuilt from scratch every reconcile pass
gaps.description: drawEdges removes every edge/port entity and re-creates new ModelEntity meshes plus materials for every edge and link each pass - immediate-mode in a retained-mode engine: O(edges) churn, flicker, poor scaling. Reconcile runs on every model mutation.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneView.swift:311-314 removes all edge-prefixed entities; 316-339 rebuilds posByEntity and re-adds every edge via addEdge (342-374): new ModelEntity (355-358) plus two sphere ports per edge (365-373). Called unconditionally at 270.
gaps.bestPractice: Make edges persistent stably-keyed entities updated in place from live positions in an EdgeSystem. Gate full rebuild behind edgesDirty.
gaps.recommendedFix: Create edge/port entities once keyed by composite id; add only new and remove only gone. Reposition endpoints in an EdgeSystem update from live positions. Stop the teardown at 311-314.

gaps.title: CollisionComponent regenerated from visualBounds every pass instead of once
gaps.description: Each pass, for every entity, the code recomputes visualBounds and resets InputTargetComponent, HoverEffectComponent, and a fresh CollisionComponent box - needless work, and recursive-visualBounds is a documented feedback/drift trap.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneView.swift:253-264: per pass sets InputTargetComponent, HoverEffectComponent, computes card.visualBounds(recursive false), then sets CollisionComponent; inside the loop at 239.
gaps.bestPractice: Set Input/Collision/Hover once at creation; recompute collision only on real size change.
gaps.recommendedFix: Wire components in a one-time factory step gated on card.parent==nil; cache the box size.

gaps.title: reconcile is one monolithic pass with a single broad observation dependency
gaps.description: reconcile does five jobs and reads renderedEntities plus links and step, so the whole pass re-runs on ANY observed mutation. No dirty-set - one stale write triggers a full-scene rebuild that fights live interaction.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneView.swift:230-271: removal (235-236), add/position (239-250), collision (253-264), controls (267), edges (270). Reads renderedEntities (231); drawEdges reads step (327), links (335).
gaps.bestPractice: Split by lifetime: reconcile handles identity only; continuous behavior lives in Systems via marker components + EntityQuery.
gaps.recommendedFix: Make reconcile identity-only; move edges and transform glide to Systems.

gaps.title: Two unsynchronized write paths for the same transform; no single source of truth
gaps.description: Position is written authoritatively in two unsynchronized places: the live entity transform (gesture, 110) and stored positionX/Y/Z. Reconcile copies model to entity while the gesture copies entity to model on release. relayout persists only for entities at the origin, so the origin doubles as both sentinel and legal coordinate.
gaps.evidence: Live: SpatialSceneView.swift:110 setPosition. Model: VM 139-141, 344-349, 354, 523. Reconcile model-to-entity at 246-250. Sentinel: VM:137 isPlaceholder tests abs(axis)less than 1e-4.
gaps.bestPractice: One source of truth, unidirectional. Do not overload the origin as a sentinel.
gaps.recommendedFix: Make the entity transform the runtime source of truth; replace the origin sentinel (VM 137-138) with an explicit placement flag.

gaps.currentApproach: All input is routed through two top-level SwiftUI gestures attached to the RealityView in `SpatialSceneView.body` — `.gesture(dragGesture)` and `.gesture(tapGesture)` (SpatialSceneView.swift:32-33). Both use `DragGesture()/TapGesture().targetedToAnyEntity()` (lines 103, 126), so a single handler receives hits for EVERY entity in the scene (cards, the × badge, edge lines, port spheres) and must disambiguate after the fact. Disambiguation is done by parsing the hit entity's `name` string: a `ctl::<role>::<ownerId>` prefix marks control children and `edge::` marks edges (controlPrefix line 83, controlRole parser lines 86-91, isControlOrEdge lines 93-95, edgePrefix line 376).
