Replace the white/black literals with control tokens so all future controls (ports, menus) share one visual language.

gaps.currentApproach: The entire spatial workspace is implemented in exactly two oversized files plus a small set of leaf views, with no ECS layer and no reusable spatial-component abstraction. SpatialSceneView.swift (543 lines) is a single SwiftUI View that simultaneously owns: gesture definitions (dragGesture/tapGesture, lines 101-148), gesture dispatch via string-prefix name parsing (controlRole/isControlOrEdge, lines 83-95), the entire scene reconcile (reconcile, lines 230-271), control-entity construction (updateDismissControl/makeDismissControl, lines 280-306), edge/port mesh generation (drawEdges/addEdge, lines 311-374), card view selection (card(for:), lines 391-427), spawn-point math (nextSpawn, lines 447-452), and four embedded sheet/prompt subviews (lines 457-543). SpatialSceneViewModel.swift (605 lines, @Observable) owns data loading, task indexing, the layout orchestration (relayout/buildLayoutInput, lines 122-240), entity materialization for three kinds (ensurePanels/ensureSprintTaskNodes/ensureWorkflowVolumes, lines 158-204), hand-rolled step-row layout that bypasses the engine (stepRowPositions, lines 489-500), all CRUD/persist actions, connect/link/collapse orchestration, content lookups, and dead scale-property logic. The ONLY decomposed, testable units are SpatialLayoutEngine.swift (103 lines, nonisolated, pure) and VolumeMetrics.swift (54 lines, nonisolated, pure). There are zero RealityKit Systems, zero custom Components, zero EntityQuery usage, and zero visionOS 26 APIs (ManipulationComponent/GestureComponent/entity.animate) in the entire TaskPlannerVision target (grep confirms NO MATCHES). The only test in the target is an unrelated AuthManager test (TaskPlannerMobileTests.swift, lines 4-11).

gaps.title: No ECS System/Component layer — all continuous and per-entity behavior is crammed into one View + one ViewModel
gaps.description: RealityKit is an ECS, but the codebase uses none of it: no type conforms to RealityKit.System, no custom struct conforms to Component, and there is no EntityQuery anywhere. Every behavior that research says belongs in a System (transform glide-to-layout, edge re-derivation, hover jitter, drag-ownership arbitration) is instead expressed imperatively inside the SwiftUI RealityView.update closure (reconcile). This is the structural root of both known bugs and of the file bloat: there is no place for behavior to live except the reconcile pass.
gaps.evidence: grep for 'System\b|registerSystem|EntityQuery|: Component|ManipulationComponent|GestureComponent|entity.animate' across TaskPlannerVision returns NO MATCHES. All behavior is in SpatialSceneView.swift reconcile (lines 230-271) and SpatialSceneViewModel.swift. The reconcile pass even rebuilds edges/ports from scratch every call (drawEdges, lines 311-373: `for edge in content.entities where ... { content.remove(edge) }` then re-adds all).
gaps.bestPractice: Continuous/derived scene behavior belongs in custom RealityKit Systems keyed by marker Components, queried via EntityQuery and ticked in System.update(context:) (WWDC23 10080; Apple 'Implementing systems for entities'). The RealityView update closure is not a tick and must not own continuous behavior (WWDC25 274).
gaps.recommendedFix: Introduce a Spatial/Components/ and Spatial/Systems/ layer. Define marker/data components (e.g. SpatialRoleComponent{kind,ownerId}, TransformAuthorityComponent{model|gesture|animating}, LayoutTargetComponent, EdgeEndpointComponent, HoverStateComponent) and register them once. Move edge re-derivation into an EdgeSystem that diffs/repositions stably-keyed edge entities instead of teardown-rebuild; move layout glide into a LayoutSystem; move jitter into a JitterSystem. Shrink reconcile to identity-only (add/remove/attach).

gaps.title: No reusable spatial-component (entity-factory) abstraction — entity assembly is duplicated and ad hoc per kind
gaps.description: There is no single factory that, given a SwiftUI view + kind, produces a correctly-wired entity (attachment + collision + input-target + hover + role). Instead, the card entity wiring is inlined in reconcile for every rendered entity on every pass (re-setting InputTargetComponent, HoverEffectComponent, recomputing CollisionComponent from visualBounds), and the dismiss control has its own separate bespoke assembler (makeDismissControl). Edges/ports are assembled inline in addEdge. Adding a new entity kind or a new control means editing the monolithic reconcile rather than declaring a component recipe.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneView.swift reconcile re-sets components on every entity every pass (lines 253-264); makeDismissControl is a one-off control builder (lines 295-306); addEdge builds line+port ModelEntities inline (lines 342-373). Collision is recomputed from card.visualBounds every pass (lines 255-264) rather than once from known card dimensions. No factory type exists.
gaps.bestPractice: Compose entities through a small reusable vocabulary (an InteractiveCard factory and a Control factory) so every entity gets attachment + right-sized collision + input-target + hover + typed role consistently; create once, mutate only deltas (MRTK3 composition-over-inheritance; visionOS 26 AGENTS guidance; retained-mode diffing).
gaps.recommendedFix: Extract an EntityFactory (e.g. SpatialEntityBuilder) with makeCard(view:kind:role:) and makeControl(role:) that assemble components from design tokens once at creation. Cache the collision shape from token-defined card dimensions instead of recomputing from recursive/own visualBounds each pass. reconcile then only calls the factory for newly-added ids.

gaps.title: Layout engine only implements type-cluster; workflow step-graph layout is hand-rolled in the ViewModel, bypassing the testable engine
gaps.description: SpatialLayoutEngine handles only the type-cluster (panels row + per-type task columns). The workflow step graph — the second core layout the app needs for pop-out — is computed imperatively in the ViewModel as a naive centered row (stepRowPositions), is MainActor-bound, takes a live SpatialEntity, and is therefore not unit-testable. This splits layout authority across a pure engine and an impure view model, and means the richer topological pop-out layout has no home in the engine.
gaps.evidence: SpatialLayoutEngine.swift line 13 explicitly defers: 'Workflow step-graph layout is added in a later phase.' The step layout instead lives in SpatialSceneViewModel.swift stepRowPositions (lines 489-500), which reads volume.positionX/Y/Z off a live entity and uses magic offsets (0.24, -0.18, 0.08). materializeSteps (lines 468-486) and toggleWorkflowVolume (lines 446-465) both call it.
gaps.bestPractice: There should be ONE layout engine that owns all placement strategies as pure, testable functions taking plain projections (the project's own 'one engine' rule + the engine's own nonisolated/Foundation-only contract). Pop-out layout should be a topological-levels strategy in the engine, not ad-hoc view-model math.
gaps.recommendedFix: Add a stepGraph(strategy) function to SpatialLayoutEngine taking plain step projections (id, dependsOn, volume anchor) and returning [Placement]; delete stepRowPositions from the view model and have materializeSteps/toggleWorkflowVolume call the engine. Then unit-test the new strategy.

gaps.title: Scale-by-property is dead, view-only code that is never consumed
gaps.description: The ViewModel defines ScaleProperty and sizeMultiplier(for:) intended to drive node size by duration/importance/urgency, but nothing reads them. card(for:) builds NodeCardView with no size input, reconcile never sets entity.scale or card.scale from sizeMultiplier, and no toolbar control sets scaleProperty. It is unreachable feature code that also has its own untested normalization math.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneViewModel.swift defines ScaleProperty (lines 25-37) and sizeMultiplier/normalized (lines 287-301). grep shows the only references are the definitions themselves; reconcile in SpatialSceneView.swift (lines 239-268) never applies a scale, and card(for:) (lines 391-427) passes no multiplier. scaleProperty is never assigned anywhere outside its declaration.
gaps.bestPractice: No commented-out or unreachable production code; features that span planning/execution must actually be wired (project CLAUDE.md clean-code rules). Visual encoding of a property should flow data->scene through a System/factory, not sit as an orphaned VM method.
gaps.recommendedFix: Either wire scaleProperty into a toolbar Picker and apply sizeMultiplier to the entity transform via the entity factory/LayoutSystem (with a unit test on normalized()), or delete the dead ScaleProperty/sizeMultiplier/normalized code until the feature is implemented. Do not leave it dangling.

gaps.title: Gesture dispatch by string-prefix name parsing instead of typed components — unscalable and untestable interaction routing
gaps.description: Entity identity AND role are both overloaded onto entity.name: the SpatialEntity id is the name, while controls/edges use 'ctl::<role>::<ownerId>' and 'edge::' prefixes parsed at gesture time. Two global .targetedToAnyEntity() gestures branch on these strings. Every new control kind grows the parser with no compiler help, and the routing logic cannot be unit-tested because it is buried in SwiftUI gesture closures.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneView.swift: controlPrefix/controlRole/isControlOrEdge (lines 83-95), edgePrefix (line 376); dragGesture guards on isControlOrEdge(value.entity.name) (lines 105, 115); tapGesture parses controlRole(name) and compares role.role == "dismiss" (lines 130-132). Control entities are named 'ctl::dismiss::<id>' (line 281); edges 'edge::line'/'edge::port' (lines 359, 370).
gaps.bestPractice: Express role/identity as typed components and route via per-entity GestureComponent (visionOS 26) or component lookups, not name string parsing (WWDC25 274; visionOS 26 AGENTS; ECS cross-engine consensus).
gaps.recommendedFix: Add a SpatialRoleComponent(kind, ownerId) set by the entity factory; replace the two global gestures + string parsing with per-entity GestureComponent handlers (or component lookups on value.entity.components). This makes dispatch type-safe and lets role resolution be tested in isolation.

gaps.title: No separation of interaction-state from visual presentation — no per-entity state machine / design-token layer
gaps.description: Visual feedback decisions are scattered and hardcoded: each card view embeds its own material level, tint, corner radius, and stroke; selection highlight (isPicked) is threaded as a bool through card(for:) and the only 'jitter'/hover affordance is a SwiftUI scaleEffect buried in DismissBadge. There is no central design-token source and no rest/hover/selected/dragging state machine, so growing the UI means editing many views and the reconcile in lockstep.
gaps.evidence: Hardcoded per-view styling with no shared tokens: NodeCardView cornerRadius 16, width 210, lineWidth 5/1.5 (lines 38-46); TypePanelCardView cornerRadius 18, width 240 (lines in TypePanelCardView.swift); WorkflowVolumeCardView .thinMaterial radius 16; NoteCardView .yellow.opacity(0.35) radius 14. Magic meters scattered in reconcile/edges: inset 0.02 (line 287), +0.006 z (line 291), box [0.05,0.05,0.02] (line 302), edge 0.004/0.011/0.07 (lines 348-371). isPicked bool threaded through card(for:) (lines 413, 423). Jitter only as DismissBadge .hoverEffect scaleEffect (lines 463-465).
gaps.bestPractice: Centralize design decisions as tokens (MRTK3/Material-3 token model adapted to meters) feeding composable primitives, and drive visuals from an observed interaction-state value rather than baking state-to-visual logic into reconcile (MRTK3 'interactables are pure state; a separate visual driver renders').
gaps.recommendedFix: Create a nonisolated SpatialTokens enum (Foundation/simd only) for meters spacing/elevation, corner radii, material levels per kind, tints, motion durations/easings, hover styles; have every card view and the collision sizing read from it. Add a NodeStateComponent{rest|hover|selected|dragging|disabled} driven by a small reducer/System mapping state->visual, replacing the isPicked bool and ad-hoc styling.

gaps.title: Almost nothing is testable: only the two pure files have any test surface, and even they have no tests in this target
gaps.description: Layout orchestration, materialization, connect/link/collapse, drag-persist, content lookups, gesture routing, edge derivation, and spawn math are all entangled with @Observable MainActor state, SwiftUI, RealityKit, and async service calls, so none of it can be unit-tested. The only pure, isolatable units are SpatialLayoutEngine and VolumeMetrics — and the single test in the target tests an unrelated AuthManager, so even those have zero coverage here.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneViewModel.swift mixes pure logic (buildLayoutInput lines 206-240, sprintStandaloneTasks/sprintWorkflows lines 107-118, normalized line 297, stepRowPositions lines 489-500) directly into an @Observable class with service I/O and no seams. The only test file, TaskPlannerMobileTests.swift, contains one AuthManager test (lines 4-11) and no SpatialLayoutEngine/VolumeMetrics/view-model tests. CLAUDE.md requires every new function to get tests.
gaps.bestPractice: Extract pure reducers/selectors (intent->data, projection-building, layout strategy selection) into nonisolated testable functions, keeping the @Observable VM as a thin shell; unit-test the layout engine and reducer logic without the simulator (cross-engine ECS guidance: pure dirty-diff reducer is testable; project CLAUDE.md test mandate).
gaps.recommendedFix: Pull buildLayoutInput, sprint filtering, spawn placement, step-graph layout, and a new intent->reduce dispatcher into nonisolated pure functions/types; add Swift unit tests covering them plus SpatialLayoutEngine and VolumeMetrics. Verify visuals separately via xcodebuild/Simulator.

gaps.title: No transform-ownership model — the data model and gesture both write the same transform, with reconcile as de-facto owner every pass
gaps.description: There is no encoded authority over an entity's transform at a given instant. reconcile re-asserts every card to the stored model position on every observed change, guarded only by a SwiftUI @State draggingID that the research and the developer confirm is not reliably visible to the update closure mid-gesture. This is an architectural decomposition gap (no ownership component/system) that directly produces the drag snap-back and blocks smooth animation.
gaps.evidence: SpatialSceneView.swift reconcile re-sets card.position from entity.positionX/Y/Z each pass with only an `if entity.id != draggingID` guard (lines 245-250); draggingID is @State mutated inside the RealityKit gesture .onChanged (lines 20, 108). endDrag persists only on release (SpatialSceneViewModel.swift lines 353-357), so the stored value is stale during the drag. No TransformAuthority component or System exists to arbitrate.
gaps.bestPractice: Exactly one authority (model | gesture/manipulation | animation) owns a transform at any instant; encode it in a scene component a System respects, and never write observed entity transforms in the update closure (WWDC25 274). Prefer ManipulationComponent(releaseBehavior=.stay) which owns the transform during drag.
gaps.recommendedFix: Add a TransformAuthorityComponent and make reconcile write transforms only for entities whose authority is .model (newly added or model-changed). Adopt visionOS 26 ManipulationComponent for movable entities (persist on ManipulationEvents.WillEnd) so RealityKit owns the transform during drag; delete the @State draggingID workaround.

proposals.philosophy: RealityKit-ECS-native. The RealityKit Scene is the single source of RUNTIME truth; the @Observable view model and tRPC store are the source of CANONICAL/PERSISTED truth, and they only SEED and RENDER. Every per-entity runtime fact (who owns the transform right now, where layout wants it, hover/selection state, drag deltas, jitter phase, edge endpoints) lives in a custom RealityKit Component on the entity. Every behavior over time (translating during a drag, gliding to a layout slot, scaling on hover, jittering, repositioning edges) lives in a custom RealityKit System ticked off the render loop. The RealityView `update:` closure shrinks to a pure, identity-only data->entity SYNC that is forbidden by construction from writing any transform a System owns. SwiftUI keeps its locked job — it renders all card CONTENT via ViewAttachmentComponent — and the pure, nonisolated SpatialLayoutEngine keeps its job: it produces TARGET poses that a MainActor LayoutSystem applies, never touching entities itself. This inverts today's design where the reconcile pass is the de-facto transform owner fighting both the gesture and SwiftUI Observation. The mental model: data flows DOWN into components once (seed/sync); behavior flows FORWARD each frame in Systems (own/animate); intents flow UP from gestures into components, and only commit to the store on release. One authority owns a transform at any instant, and that authority is always a System reading a component — never the update closure.
proposals.oneLineThesis: Make components the runtime state and Systems the behavior so exactly one System owns each entity's transform at any instant; the reconcile pass becomes an identity-only data sync that never writes a System-owned transform, which kills the drag snap-back, and a HoverSystem driven by collision-group-isolated child colliders + a dedicated HoverEffectComponent fixes the x-highlight.

layers.name: Canonical / Persistence (unchanged)
layers.responsibility: tRPC SpatialSceneService + the @Observable SpatialSceneViewModel hold persisted SpatialEntity rows, indexed Task/TaskStep/UserTaskType, links. Owns load, create, connect/merge, link, collapse, note edits, and transform PERSISTENCE on drag-release. It is the source of CANONICAL truth and the only thing that talks to the backend. It never reads or writes live entity transforms; it exposes an immutable snapshot (renderedEntities) the sync consumes.
layers.owns: SpatialEntity rows, link list, task/step/type indices, async store actions, manuallyMovedIds.

layers.name: Pure Layout (unchanged, extended)
layers.responsibility: nonisolated SpatialLayoutEngine + VolumeMetrics. Takes plain Sendable projections (TypeInput/TaskInput/manual/metrics) and returns [Placement] (entityId -> target meters). Stays Foundation/simd-only, MainActor-free, fully unit-tested. Extended with a stepGraph(...) strategy so workflow pop-out layout also lives here (deleting the ad-hoc stepRowPositions in the VM). It computes TARGETS only; it never touches an Entity.
layers.owns: Deterministic target poses for auto-flow entities and step graphs.

layers.name: Component model (NEW — runtime truth)
layers.responsibility: Custom RealityKit Components attached per entity that hold ALL mutable runtime state. The Scene becomes the source of runtime truth. Each is a small Codable-where-sensible struct: SpatialRoleComponent (kind + ownerId + entityId — replaces name-prefix parsing), TransformAuthorityComponent (.layout | .gesture | .animating — the ownership token), LayoutTargetComponent (target SIMD3 + needsAnimate flag), DragComponent (startTransform + active), HoverStateComponent (isHovered + intent role), JitterComponent (basePosition + phase + amplitude + enabled), SelectionComponent (.none/.pickedMerge/.pickedLink/.editing), EdgeComponent (stableKey + sourceId + targetId + semantic), EdgeEndpointComponent (sourceEntityId/targetEntityId for the EdgeSystem to follow), DismissControlComponent (ownerId), LifecycleComponent (.entering/.idle/.exiting for animated add/remove).
layers.owns: Per-entity runtime state: ownership, layout target, drag start, hover, jitter, selection, edge keys, lifecycle.

layers.name: System layer (NEW — behavior over time)
layers.responsibility: Custom RealityKit Systems registered once at app launch, ticked on the MainActor render loop via update(context:) over EntityQuery matches. They are the ONLY code that writes entity transforms/scale/visuals. Each respects TransformAuthorityComponent so authorities never collide. DragSystem: while a drag is active (driven by ManipulationComponent or a fallback DragGesture writing DragComponent) it asserts ownership .gesture and clamps; on release it sets authority .animating and hands the final transform up to the VM to persist. LayoutSystem: for entities whose authority is .layout, glides position toward LayoutTargetComponent via Entity.animate; skips .gesture/.animating entities. JitterSystem: integrates context.deltaTime into JitterComponent.phase and offsets a clean base for the playful hover wiggle; gated by reduceMotion. HoverSystem: reads HoverStateComponent to drive selection-lift / scale-pop and arms jitter. EdgeSystem: repositions persistent, stably-keyed edge + port entities from live endpoint transforms (no teardown). LifecycleSystem: animates entrance/exit then removes.
layers.owns: All runtime transform/scale/visual mutation; deltaTime-based motion; edge follow.

layers.name: Entity factory + Sync (NEW — identity-only reconcile)
layers.responsibility: SpatialEntityFactory assembles each entity ONCE from design tokens: a card body entity carrying ViewAttachmentComponent (SwiftUI content), a token-sized CollisionComponent, InputTargetComponent(allowedInputTypes:.indirect), HoverEffectComponent(.highlight), ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(...releaseBehavior:.stay), and all seed components. SceneSync (the shrunken reconcile) runs in update: and does identity-only work: diff VM snapshot vs scene by id, call the factory for added ids, mark removed ids LifecycleComponent.exiting, refresh LayoutTargetComponent for entities whose canonical/layout target changed, and refresh attachment content. It NEVER writes a transform an authority owns. Gesture routing reads SpatialRoleComponent (no string parsing); per-entity GestureComponent handles taps; ManipulationComponent handles drag.
layers.owns: Entity creation recipes, id-diff sync, component seeding, gesture wiring.

layers.name: Design system (NEW — tokens + visual driver)
layers.responsibility: SpatialTokens (nonisolated, Foundation/SwiftUI) is the single source of every visual+spatial constant: per-kind card meters dimensions, corner radii, padding, glass material level, tint roles, border weights per SelectionState, edge thickness/port radius, semantic edge colors (bridged Color<->UIColor from one token), motion durations/easings/jitter amplitudes. A SpatialCard container view + a state->style visual driver render every card kind as one primitive parameterized by kind/tint/interactionState, with glass via .glassBackgroundEffect() and vibrancy tiers; honors reduceTransparency/reduceMotion.
layers.owns: All visual/spatial constants, the card primitive, state->style mapping, accessibility fallbacks.

proposals.transformOwnershipModel: A single TransformAuthorityComponent enum {.layout, .gesture, .animating} is the ownership token on every movable entity, and exactly one System may write a given entity's transform per instant based on it. (1) Default authority is .layout. LayoutSystem queries entities with authority==.layout and a LayoutTargetComponent, and for those whose target changed glides position via Entity.animate(.easeOut(SpatialMotion.reflow)); it never touches .gesture or .animating entities. (2) On grab, ManipulationComponent owns the transform natively for its lifetime; ManipulationEvents.WillBegin sets authority=.gesture (and disarms LayoutSystem). RealityKit itself drives the transform during manipulation, so there is no app-side write to race. For the fallback path, a DragGesture writes DragComponent{startTransform, active=true} and DragSystem (authority .gesture) applies the clamped delta — still a single writer. (3) On release, ManipulationEvents.WillEnd reads the final entity.transform, sets authority=.animating, hands (id, pose) UP to the VM to persist (the VM writes the SpatialEntity row but NEVER pushes it back onto the entity), and after a short settle Entity.animate completion sets authority back to .layout. Crucially, SceneSync (the update closure) is structurally forbidden from writing ANY transform: it only seeds a transform once at creation (when card.parent==nil) and otherwise writes only to LayoutTargetComponent (a request, not a position). Because the layout target and the live transform are separate values, the canonical store can change freely mid-drag without yanking the card. This is the textbook target-vs-presented split: the store/layout own the TARGET; a System owns the PRESENTED transform; the update closure owns NEITHER. There is therefore no instant at which two authorities write the same transform.
proposals.reconciliationModel: SceneSync replaces the 543-line reconcile with an identity-only diff that runs in update: and is pure of transform writes. Steps each pass: (1) Build liveIDs from the VM's renderedEntities snapshot. (2) Removed: entities present in the scene (matched by SpatialRoleComponent.entityId, not name) but absent from liveIDs get LifecycleComponent.phase=.exiting; LifecycleSystem animates them out and removes — never an instant content.remove. (3) Added: ids in liveIDs with no scene entity are built by SpatialEntityFactory, seeded with all components (authority=.layout, role, lifecycle=.entering, hover/jitter/selection), added once, and given an initial transform ONLY here (the single legal transform write in the sync path). (4) Changed identity/content: if a card's underlying Task/step/type content changed, refresh its ViewAttachmentComponent rootView (content, not transform); if its canonical/computed slot changed, write the new value to LayoutTargetComponent (a request the LayoutSystem fulfils only when authority==.layout). (5) Selection/mode: write SelectionComponent from VM mode state so the HoverSystem/visual driver react. Edges are NOT rebuilt here — EdgeComponent-bearing entities are created once by a diff over the derived edge set (keyed by sourceId+targetId+semantic) and the EdgeSystem follows live endpoints every frame. Because the sync only ever writes components and never a transform owned by a System, and because change detection is by stable id + dirty target rather than re-stamping everything, an unrelated @Observable change can fire update: harmlessly: nothing moves unless a target actually changed. This also satisfies the WWDC25 274 rule — no observed entity transform is mutated in the update closure.
proposals.interactionModel: Routing moves from one global gesture pair + string parsing to typed, per-entity dispatch. Drag: ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(card, hoverEffect:.highlight, allowedInputTypes:.indirect, collisionShapes:[tokenSizedBox]) with releaseBehavior=.stay and dynamics set translate-only for cards (free 6DoF for notes/volumes). RealityKit owns the move; subscribe once to ManipulationEvents.WillBegin (authority=.gesture), DidUpdateTransform (clamp to VolumeMetrics by constraining the value), and WillEnd (authority=.animating, read final transform, viewModel.endDrag to persist + mark manuallyMoved). Tap: each card carries a GestureComponent(TapGesture()) whose handler reads the entity's SpatialRoleComponent to dispatch (taskNode/stepNode -> edit; workflowVolume -> toggle; note -> note edit) — no name parsing. The × control carries its OWN GestureComponent(TapGesture()) that reads DismissControlComponent.ownerId, so dismiss is intrinsic, not routed through the card. Merge/Link modes become explicit VM interaction-mode state (an enum + sourceId), reduced from intents; entering a mode flips card taps to 'pick' behavior and writes SelectionComponent.pickedMerge/pickedLink to candidates so the visual driver highlights them; entering a mode also sets ManipulationComponent.isEnabled=false on cards so a pick can't be misread as a drag. CoordinateSpace3D / GeometryProxy3D.coordinateSpace3D() replaces the ad-hoc value.convert(.local,.scene) math in any remaining manual path. Edges/ports carry no InputTargetComponent and sit in a CollisionGroup excluded from all input masks, so they are structurally non-interactive.
proposals.hoverHighlightModel: Hover/highlight/selection are three distinct, decoupled concerns. (1) System gaze hover: every interactive entity gets a real RealityKit HoverEffectComponent(.highlight(HighlightHoverEffectStyle(color: tokenTint, strength:))) — NOT a SwiftUI .hoverEffect buried in an attachment. The card and the × badge each get their OWN HoverEffectComponent with DISTINCT HoverEffectComponent.GroupID so the badge activates independently of the card (hover does not cascade to children, and a shared group would couple them). (2) Playful pop + jitter: a HoverStateComponent is the app-readable hover flag; because the app cannot read system gaze directly, the HoverStateComponent is toggled from the SwiftUI .onHover inside the attachment OR from a hover-state shader path — the HoverSystem then arms JitterComponent and runs a low-amplitude Entity.animate(.spring) scale-pop. JitterSystem integrates context.deltaTime into phase and offsets a CLEAN base position (so jitter never drifts the persisted transform), gated by accessibilityReduceMotion (crossfade fallback). (3) App selection (merge/link picked, edit target): SelectionComponent drives the visual driver's tokenized border/lift — kept entirely separate from hover, never derived from it. The × badge's highlight now works because (a) it has its own non-enclosed collider in its own input/hover group and (b) its highlight is a RealityKit HoverEffectComponent, not a 2D SwiftUI scaleEffect that the parent swallowed.
proposals.collisionModel: Hierarchical collision is designed, not accidental, on three axes. (1) Geometry: the card collider stops being inflated to depth max(z,0.03). It is a THIN token-defined box (~0.006 m) sized from SpatialTokens card dimensions (computed once at creation, cached — no per-pass visualBounds regeneration, killing the recursive-visualBounds drift trap). The × badge is offset a REAL gap (~0.025-0.03 m, not 0.006 m) clearly in front of the card so the parent box cannot enclose it; per Apple DTS an enclosing parent with InputTargetComponent is always hit first, which is exactly why the badge was dead. (2) CollisionGroup/CollisionFilter: define .node, .control, .edge groups. Cards = .node, controls = .control, edges/ports = .edge with an empty input mask (and no InputTargetComponent). Manipulation/drag targets .node; control taps target .control. Routing by mask/role replaces isControlOrEdge name checks. (3) Input precedence: cards set InputTargetComponent(allowedInputTypes:.indirect); the control gets its own InputTargetComponent so it is independently hittable. If any control must overlap its parent, the wrapper's InputTargetComponent.isEnabled=false plus a dedicated child body target makes siblings reachable. Collision shapes derive from tokens, so the visual and the hit region can never disagree, and the valid/invalid visualBounds branch disappears. Where precise frontmost resolution is needed (connect/link targeting) use scene.raycast(..., mask:.node).
proposals.animationModel: All motion is target-vs-presented with one animation authority. Entity.animate(_:) (in gesture/event closures and Systems) and content.animate { } drive transforms with SwiftUI curves; pre-26 fallback is entity.move(to:relativeTo:duration:timingFunction:). LayoutSystem glides .layout entities to LayoutTargetComponent via Entity.animate(.easeOut(SpatialMotion.reflow)) so relayout, return-to-tray, and column repack all become smooth flights rather than teleports. LifecycleSystem animates entrance (scale 0.85->1, opacity 0->1 via the attachment) on .entering and exit (shrink/fade, or fly-to-tray for returnToTray) on .exiting before removal. Drag release runs a short settle spring then completion flips authority back to .layout (treating the animation as a temporary ownership claim so nothing fights it). Workflow expand spawns step entities AT the volume position then Entity.animate's them with a staggered spring to SpatialLayoutEngine.stepGraph slots; collapse reverses. Hover pop and jitter are Entity.animate(.spring) + a deltaTime System as above. Every duration/easing/spring/jitter-amplitude is a SpatialMotion token, and every animation reads accessibilityReduceMotion to substitute a crossfade. Edges animate their opacity in/out on add/remove and follow nodes continuously via EdgeSystem — never recreated, so in-flight animation/hover state survives.
proposals.materialDesignSystem: A nonisolated SpatialTokens enum is the single source of truth (mirroring VolumeMetrics' precedent), holding: per-kind card dimensions (in BOTH points for the SwiftUI .frame and meters for collision, bridged so they can't disagree), corner radii, padding, glass material level per elevation tier (panel=section, node/note=interactive, volume=recede), tint roles, border weights per SelectionState, semantic edge colors (one token bridged to both SwiftUI Color and UIColor), and SpatialMotion (durations/easings/spring params/jitter amplitude+frequency). All four card views collapse into one SpatialCard primitive that takes kind+tint+interactionState and applies tokenized glass via .glassBackgroundEffect() (used consistently, replacing the .regularMaterial/.thinMaterial/color-under-material mix) with vibrancy tiers (.primary/.secondary/.tertiary) and white text — no colored text, no solid color washes. Edge/port meshes use UnlitMaterial(applyPostProcessToneMap:false) so their token colors match the SwiftUI palette exactly (tone mapping is otherwise on by default and shifts UI color). Note color, dependency-edge teal, and link orange become named semantic tokens, eliminating the literal .yellow/.systemTeal/.systemOrange. Accessibility: the glass token swaps to an opaque/high-contrast surface under accessibilityReduceTransparency/increaseContrast, and selection adds a non-color cue (lift + thicker tokenized border) so state is never color-only — important for the ADHD audience.
proposals.howItFixesDragBug: Root cause today: reconcile runs in update: on every @Observable change and re-asserts card.position from the VM's STORED position (stale until drag-release), guarded only by a SwiftUI @State draggingID that the update closure can't reliably see mid-gesture — so the card snaps to the stale slot. The fix removes the cause entirely on three levels. (1) The update closure (now SceneSync) is structurally forbidden from writing any transform a System owns; it writes only LayoutTargetComponent (a request) — there is no card.position re-assert line to fight the drag. (2) Transform ownership is an explicit TransformAuthorityComponent: during a drag authority==.gesture and LayoutSystem skips the entity; on release it goes .animating then .layout. Ownership lives in a Component the Systems read, not in view @State the update closure can't observe in time — so the failed draggingID race is gone. (3) Adopting ManipulationComponent (releaseBehavior=.stay, persist in WillEnd) makes RealityKit itself own the transform during the drag, so there is literally no app-side writer to race, and .stay prevents the framework's own .reset snap-back. The target (store/layout) and the presented transform (System) are separate values, so the store changing mid-drag can never yank the card. Net: no two authorities ever write the same transform; the snap-back cannot occur by construction.
proposals.howItFixesHoverBug: Root cause today: the × is a child only +0.006 m in front of a parent card collider inflated to depth max(z,0.03) (~3 cm), so the parent ENCLOSES the badge and, per Apple DTS, the parent (which has InputTargetComponent) is hit first — the badge never receives gaze/hover/tap; and its highlight is a SwiftUI .hoverEffect{scaleEffect} inside the attachment, which scales 2D content and isn't a reliable RealityKit hover for a placed entity. The fix attacks all three. (1) Geometry: the card collider becomes a THIN token box (~0.006 m, not 0.03) and the badge is pushed a real ~0.025-0.03 m gap forward so the parent box no longer contains it — the badge becomes the frontmost collider and is independently hit-testable. (2) Isolation: the badge gets its own CollisionComponent in CollisionGroup .control plus its own InputTargetComponent, and a HoverEffectComponent with a DISTINCT HoverEffectComponent.GroupID so it activates independently of the card (hover doesn't cascade to children). (3) Visible highlight: highlight is now a real RealityKit HoverEffectComponent(.highlight) on the badge entity (plus an optional HoverSystem-driven Entity.animate scale pop), not a SwiftUI scaleEffect; and the dismiss tap is its own per-entity GestureComponent reading DismissControlComponent — no name routing through the swallowing parent. With the badge frontmost, isolated, and RealityKit-driven, it both highlights and dismisses reliably.
proposals.scalabilityStory: Adding a new entity kind = add a case to SpatialRole + SpatialTokens dimensions + a SpatialCard content branch; the factory wires collision/input/hover/manipulation/components uniformly and every System already operates on it via its components. Adding a new behavior (e.g. magnetic snapping, breathing-on-selected, auto-arrange) = add one Component + one System keyed by EntityQuery; no edits to the sync, gestures, or existing Systems. Adding a new control (port handle, context menu) = the factory's Control recipe (group .control, own collider/hover/GroupID, own GestureComponent) — no central parser to grow. Adding a new edge semantic = a token color + an EdgeComponent semantic case; EdgeSystem already follows it. Replacing tap routing or input modality = swap the per-entity GestureComponent / ManipulationComponent config; nothing else changes because dispatch reads typed components, not names. Performance scales because edges follow in place (no O(E) teardown) and the sync writes only deltas (no O(N) transform re-stamp). The monolith's two megafunctions are replaced by small single-responsibility units, so growth is additive, not edit-in-lockstep.
proposals.testability: The pure layer expands and stays unit-tested with no simulator: SpatialLayoutEngine (type-cluster + new stepGraph strategy) and VolumeMetrics remain nonisolated Foundation/simd, and SpatialTokens/SpatialMotion become nonisolated value types with tested derivations (point<->meter bridge, normalized scale, color bridge). The sync is refactored into a pure reducer: a nonisolated function (previousSceneState snapshot + new VM snapshot) -> [SceneMutation] (.add/.remove/.refreshContent/.retarget) that is fully unit-testable without RealityKit; SceneSync just applies the returned mutation list to RealityViewContent. VM logic worth testing (buildLayoutInput, sprint filtering, spawn placement, interaction-mode reduce, intent->data) is pulled into nonisolated pure functions. Component default/derivation logic (JitterComponent phase math, clamp constraints) is plain value-type code with tests. Systems and visuals are verified by xcodebuild + Simulator (RealityKit/SwiftUI aren't unit-testable here, and SourceKit reports false errors), exactly as the rules require — but the decision-making logic underneath them is pure and covered. This also fixes the current gap where only AuthManager has a test in the target.
risks: ManipulationComponent (visionOS 26) has a reported intermittent bug where translation can stop app-wide until device restart; mitigate by keeping the DragGesture+DragComponent+DragSystem fallback behind a flag that shares the same TransformAuthorityComponent contract, so reconcile treats both paths identically.

The app cannot read system gaze hover directly (privacy), so HoverStateComponent must be fed from the attachment's SwiftUI .onHover or a hover-state shader; if .onHover proves unreliable for a placed attachment, the jitter/pop trigger needs the ShaderGraphMaterial HoverState path, which requires authoring a material in Reality Composer Pro (heavier) — spike this early.

Systems run on the MainActor render loop under SWIFT_DEFAULT_ACTOR_ISOLATION=MainActor; care is needed to keep SpatialLayoutEngine nonisolated and called FROM the LayoutSystem so the engine's isolation doesn't leak — a known footgun if a System property accidentally captures engine types.

content.animate/Entity.animate interacting with the update closure must be confined to Systems/gesture/event closures; an accidental animate inside the sync could reintroduce a feedback loop. Enforce by code review + a lint note in .claude/rules.

This is a large refactor of two files into ~15; staging it (tokens+factory+sync first, then ManipulationComponent, then Systems, then design system) is necessary to keep each xcodebuild green, and each stage must be verified in Simulator since Vitest doesn't cover visionOS UI.
PresentationComponent for in-scene edit popovers is attractive but new; keep the existing .sheet path until PresentationComponent is validated in the volumetric window so editing never regresses.
proposals.tradeoffs: Chose ECS-native (components+Systems own runtime state/behavior) over a lighter 'just stop re-asserting position + add an ownership flag' patch. The patch would fix the two bugs faster and with fewer new files, but it leaves the update closure as the de-facto behavior home, keeps name-prefix routing, and doesn't scale to jitter/edges/animation — the developer explicitly said the foundation is weak, so the heavier investment is warranted. Chose ManipulationComponent as the primary drag (near-trivial, system-owned, .stay) but kept a hand-rolled fallback for its known bug — extra code, but resilient. Chose to keep SwiftUI rendering content + .sheet editing (locked constraint, lower risk) rather than fully migrating to PresentationComponent now. Chose target-vs-presented separation (two values per entity) which costs a little memory/conceptual overhead but is what structurally removes the snap-back and enables smooth animation. Chose typed components over a name protocol — more types, but compiler-checked and test-friendly. Net: more files and more upfront work, but each is small, single-responsibility, and additive, trading short-term surface area for long-term scalability and the elimination of both bugs at the root.

proposals.philosophy: Treat the RealityKit scene as a RETAINED render target that a SwiftUI-driven declarative reconciler keeps in sync with the @Observable data model — exactly the React-Three-Fiber discipline, ported to visionOS 26. The reconciler is a keyed differ that writes ONLY the properties it owns; the single property whose ownership is contested (an entity's transform) is governed by an explicit, per-entity OWNERSHIP TOKEN that lives in the @Observable model (never in SwiftUI @State, never inferred from the scene). At any instant exactly one authority — data, gesture, or animation — owns each transform, and the reconciler defers to whoever holds the token. We split a "target transform" (what the data/layout says) from the "presented transform" (what is drawn) and put a thin interpolation layer between them so layout changes glide instead of snapping and never fight a live drag. Content stays 100% SwiftUI (ViewAttachmentComponent); RealityKit only places entities, routes gestures, and runs the interpolation/jitter/edge Systems. The pure nonisolated SpatialLayoutEngine and VolumeMetrics are untouched — they remain the only source of target positions. Everything else (collision, hover, animation, glass) is derived from one nonisolated SpatialTokens table so the system scales by adding token rows and entity kinds, not by editing two megafiles.
proposals.oneLineThesis: A keyed declarative reconciler that writes only what it owns, arbitrated by per-entity transform-ownership tokens in the @Observable model, with a target-vs-presented interpolation layer — so the data model, live gestures, and animations never fight over an entity's transform, and hover/highlight is a presentation concern decoupled from layout.

layers.name: Data / Model layer (@Observable SpatialSceneViewModel + SpatialSceneStore)
layers.responsibility: Single source of truth: SpatialEntity rows from tRPC, links, indexed Task/TaskStep/UserTaskType, AND the per-entity TransformOwnership tokens + target transforms. Exposes intents (move/dismiss/connect/link/toggle/edit) that reduce to data writes. Holds the keyed diff input (renderedEntities). Owns NO RealityKit types. Stays MainActor (default isolation).
layers.owns: Canonical entity data, ownership tokens, target transforms, interaction mode (idle/connect/link), selection set.

layers.name: Layout layer (pure, nonisolated)
layers.responsibility: Compute TARGET positions only. Unchanged SpatialLayoutEngine (type-cluster) plus a new stepGraph strategy moved out of the view model. Imports only Foundation/simd; deterministic; unit-tested. Produces [Placement]; never touches entities.
layers.owns: Target position computation for auto-flow entities (hand-moved excluded via manual override, as today).

layers.name: Reconciler layer (SceneReconciler, MainActor)
layers.responsibility: The keyed differ called from RealityView.update. Diffs renderedEntities against the live scene by stable id: ADD new entities (via SpatialEntityFactory), REMOVE gone ones (with exit animation), and write target transforms ONLY for entities whose ownership token == .data. Skips .gesture/.animating entities entirely. Updates persistent edge entities in place (no teardown). Sets InputTarget/Collision/Hover ONCE at creation, never per pass.
layers.owns: Entity identity (existence + attachment binding), one-time component wiring, dispatch of target transforms to the interpolation layer for data-owned entities, incremental edge diffing.

layers.name: Entity factory + component vocabulary
layers.responsibility: Build a correctly-wired entity from a kind+role+SwiftUI view exactly once: ViewAttachmentComponent + token-sized CollisionComponent + InputTargetComponent(allowedInputTypes:.indirect) + HoverEffectComponent(style+GroupID) + typed SpatialRoleComponent + TransformTargetComponent. One factory for cards, one for controls (×, ports), one for edges. New entity kinds = new token row + one factory case.
layers.owns: Deterministic entity assembly; the typed-component contract that replaces name-prefix parsing.

layers.name: Interaction layer (per-entity GestureComponent + ManipulationComponent)
layers.responsibility: Route input by entity identity, not strings. Movable cards carry ManipulationComponent(releaseBehavior:.stay); on WillBegin the entity sets its own ownership token to .gesture in the model, on DidUpdateTransform it clamps to VolumeMetrics, on WillEnd it persists and hands ownership back to .data. Controls (×) and tap-to-edit/connect/link use per-entity GestureComponent. Reads SpatialRoleComponent for dispatch.
layers.owns: User intent capture, transform ownership claim/release during direct manipulation.

layers.name: Animation/Systems layer (RealityKit Systems)
layers.responsibility: Continuous behavior that must run over time, off the update closure. TransformFollowSystem glides presented→target (critically-damped) for .data/.animating entities, skipping .gesture. EdgeFollowSystem repositions persistent edge/port entities from live endpoints. JitterSystem reads HoverStateComponent and applies low-amplitude spring jitter, gated by reduceMotion. All keyed by EntityQuery on marker components.
layers.owns: Presented-transform interpolation, edge endpoint following, hover jitter — i.e. all per-frame motion.

layers.name: Presentation / design-system layer (SwiftUI cards + glass tokens)
layers.responsibility: Render all content as SwiftUI. A single SpatialCard container applies tokenized glass + tint + corner radius + selection/hover styling driven by an InteractionState enum. Four card bodies become content-only. Hover/selection visuals are a pure function of observed state, independent of layout/transform.
layers.owns: All visible content, glass material levels, tint from UserTaskType, redundant (non-color) selection cues, reduce-transparency fallback.
keyTypes: InteractionState enum {rest, hover, selected, picked, dragging, disabled}

proposals.transformOwnershipModel: Each entity carries an ownership TOKEN — TransformOwnership { case data, gesture, animating } — stored in the @Observable model (viewModel.ownershipByID[id]) AND mirrored on the entity as TransformTargetComponent.ownership for Systems to read cheaply. Critically the AUTHORITATIVE token lives in the model, NOT in SwiftUI @State, which is exactly why the current draggingID guard fails: @State mutated inside a RealityKit gesture .onChanged is not reliably visible to the next RealityView.update pass, but an @Observable model field set inside the gesture closure IS observed and IS visible to the reconciler. Rules: (1) DATA owns at rest and on creation/relayout — only then may the reconciler write the presented transform (indirectly, by setting target + letting TransformFollowSystem glide). (2) GESTURE takes ownership the instant a drag/manipulation begins (ManipulationEvents.WillBegin → viewModel.claim(id, .gesture)); while held, ManipulationComponent owns the live transform, TransformFollowSystem skips the entity, and the reconciler skips it. (3) ANIMATING owns during an explicit Entity.animate transition (entrance, return-to-tray, expand/collapse), set by whoever starts the animation and cleared on completion. Handoff is explicit and unidirectional: WillEnd persists the final transform, writes it as the new target, then releases to .data. There is never a moment where two authorities write the same transform, because the reconciler's write path is gated `guard ownershipByID[id] == .data`. This single guard, reading observed model state, is the root-cause fix.
proposals.reconciliationModel: A keyed differ (React-Three-Fiber applyProps discipline) run from RealityView.update, but it touches ONLY properties it owns and never re-asserts contested transforms. Algorithm per pass: (1) Compute liveIDs = Set(renderedEntities.id) and existing scene ids. (2) ADD: for each new id, build the entity once via SpatialEntityFactory, attach its ViewAttachmentComponent, set name=id, wire InputTarget/Collision/Hover/role components ONCE, set ownership=.animating and run an entrance Entity.animate (scale 0.85→1, opacity ramp), then set target transform. (3) REMOVE: for ids gone from liveIDs, run an exit animation then content.remove on completion (no instant pop). (4) RETAIN: for surviving entities, write the TARGET transform into TransformTargetComponent.target — but ONLY if ownershipByID[id] == .data; gesture/animating entities are skipped so a live drag is never overwritten. The reconciler NEVER sets entity.position directly and NEVER regenerates CollisionComponent or HoverEffectComponent per pass (they are created once; collision is sized from SpatialTokens card dimensions, not visualBounds, killing the recursive-bounds drift and per-pass churn). Edges are diffed by stable composite key (sourceID+targetID+kind) via EdgeComponent: add only new edge entities, remove only gone ones; endpoints are repositioned every frame by EdgeFollowSystem reading live transforms — replacing the current delete-and-re-add-all teardown. Because the reconciler is now a small pure-ish diff (previous live set + new model → add/remove/target-write list), the diff function is extracted as a nonisolated testable reducer (DiffResult) and unit-tested without the simulator.
proposals.interactionModel: Replace the two top-level .targetedToAnyEntity() gestures + entity.name string parsing with typed, per-entity routing. Every entity carries SpatialRoleComponent {kind, role, ownerId}; entity.name is demoted to being ONLY the stable id. Movable cards (taskNode/stepNode/note/workflowVolume) get ManipulationComponent via ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(entity, hoverEffect:.highlight, allowedInputTypes:.indirect, collisionShapes:[tokenBox]) with releaseBehavior=.stay (default .reset IS a built-in snap-back). Lifecycle: subscribe to ManipulationEvents.WillBegin → viewModel.claim(id,.gesture); DidUpdateTransform → clamp to VolumeMetrics.clamp in place (constrain, don't replace); WillEnd → read final transform, viewModel.endDrag(persist + mark hand-moved), set it as the new target, release to .data. This deletes dragGesture, dragStart, draggingID, and the manual value.convert(.local,.scene) math (ManipulationComponent handles re-orientation and coordinate space). Tap-to-edit, the × dismiss, and connect/link picks are per-entity GestureComponent(TapGesture()) handlers that read SpatialRoleComponent — no central switch, no prefix parsing. Connect/link become an explicit interaction-mode on the @Observable model (idle/connect/link + source id) so entering a mode disables manipulation and tap-to-edit, and candidate highlight is driven by observed state, not a transient @State bool. New control kinds (ports, context menus) attach their own GestureComponent — the dispatcher never grows.
proposals.hoverHighlightModel: Hover/highlight is a PRESENTATION concern that lives in the presented layer and is fully decoupled from layout/target transform — so it composes with (never overwrites) position. Three mechanisms, used deliberately: (1) System gaze hover = RealityKit HoverEffectComponent(.highlight(HighlightHoverEffectStyle(color: typeTint, strength:))) on the entity, set once at creation, with a distinct HoverEffectComponent.GroupID per role so a card and its × badge activate INDEPENDENTLY (hover does not cascade to children and does not bleed between them). The × badge gets its own GroupID — this plus the collision fix is the root cause fix for bug #2. (2) App selection = an InteractionState enum {rest,hover,selected,picked,dragging,disabled} sourced from observed model state (selection set, interaction mode), passed into SpatialCard, which renders a tokenized border/lift — never derived from system hover (which is privacy-applied and unreadable by the app). The current merge-only isHighlighted bool becomes one case of this enum. (3) Playful jitter = NOT a SwiftUI .scaleEffect inside the attachment (that scales flat 2D content and is unreliable for a placed entity). Instead JitterSystem reads HoverStateComponent.isHovered and applies a low-amplitude spring scale/position offset on top of the presented transform via deltaTime integration over a clean base, so jitter and layout coexist. The DismissBadge .hoverEffect is removed. All jitter is gated behind @Environment(\\.accessibilityReduceMotion) with a tint/opacity crossfade fallback (ADHD audience).
proposals.collisionModel: Make hierarchy explicit instead of accidental. Root cause of bug #2: the card's CollisionComponent is inflated to depth max(z,0.03)≈3cm and the × child sits only +0.006m in front, so the parent box geometrically encloses the child and (per Apple DTS forum 747256) the parent with an InputTargetComponent is always hit first — the badge never gets hover/tap. Fixes, all token-driven: (a) Size the card collider from SpatialTokens card dimensions (NOT visualBounds — eliminating the per-pass recursive-bounds drift) with a SHALLOW depth (~0.006m) matching a flat SwiftUI quad, so it cannot enclose forward controls. (b) Offset the × control to a real gap (~+0.025–0.03m, not +0.006m) clearly in FRONT of the card box, with its own small non-enclosed collider, OR move it just outside the card's xy footprint. (c) Define named CollisionGroups (.node, .control, .edge) with CollisionFilter so hit-testing routes by category and decorative edges/ports carry NO InputTargetComponent and are excluded from input masks — gesture correctness stops depending on string-prefix exclusion. (d) Set collision ONCE in the factory; never rebuild per reconcile pass. For precise frontmost resolution (connect/link targeting) use scene.raycast(..., mask:.node) instead of gesture target order.
proposals.animationModel: Two clearly separated animation paths. (1) Implicit transitions own the transform briefly via ownership=.animating: entrance (scale 0.85→1 + opacity, on add), exit (shrink/fade before content.remove), return-to-tray (fly from current to new column slot), expand/collapse pop-out (steps emerge from the volume position to their stepGraph slots with a staggered spring). Each runs Entity.animate(.spring/.easeInOut, duration: SpatialTokens.motion.X) inside the reconciler/event closure (never inside the update body re-reading observed state — that is the WWDC25 274 infinite-loop trap), claims .animating, and releases to .data on completion. (2) Continuous interpolation is the standing layer: TransformFollowSystem glides presented→target with a frame-rate-independent critically-damped follow (deltaTime-based, Rory Driscoll/spring-roll-call), so any change to a data-owned target animates automatically and layout reflow is never a teleport. The wire scaleProperty→sizeMultiplier feature gets wired here (animate scale changes) or deleted as dead code. All durations/easings/spring params come from one SpatialTokens.Motion table; every animation reads reduceMotion and degrades to a crossfade.
proposals.materialDesignSystem: One nonisolated SpatialTokens enum (Foundation/SwiftUI only, mirroring the VolumeMetrics 'single authority, no scattered magic numbers' precedent) is the single source of truth for: per-kind card dimensions (meters AND points, bridging the two domains so collision and layout derive from the same width), corner radii, paddings, border widths (rest vs selected), glass material level per elevation tier (HIG: typePanel=.regular section, node/note=.thin interactive, workflowVolume=.thick recede), tint roles, motion durations/easings, hover styles, and semantic edge colors (dependency, link) derived ONCE so the RealityKit UnlitMaterial(applyPostProcessToneMap:false) edges match the SwiftUI palette instead of today's disconnected .systemTeal/.systemOrange/.yellow literals. A single SpatialCard<Content> container applies glass + tint + radius + InteractionState border/lift; the four card views become content-only and stop hardcoding 16/18/14/16 radii and 210/240/190/200 widths. Glass uses .glassBackgroundEffect()/Liquid Glass consistently with vibrancy tiers (.primary/.secondary/.tertiary) instead of solid color washes; respects accessibilityReduceTransparency/increaseContrast (swap to opaque high-contrast surface) and conveys state redundantly (lift + symbol, not color alone).
proposals.howItFixesDragBug: Root cause today: reconcile() runs from RealityView.update on every @Observable change and unconditionally re-asserts card.position from the STALE stored model position (written only on release), gated only by a SwiftUI @State draggingID that the update closure cannot reliably see mid-gesture (the developer's failed fix). Two compounding faults: the model fights the live gesture, and the guard races. The new architecture eliminates both at the root: (1) Transform ownership is an @Observable model token. The instant a drag begins, ManipulationEvents.WillBegin calls viewModel.claim(id, .gesture) — set in the gesture/event closure, this mutation IS observed, so the very next reconcile pass sees ownershipByID[id]==.gesture and SKIPS the entity (the reconciler's write is `guard ownershipByID[id]==.data`). There is nothing to race because the authority is observed model state, not view @State. (2) The reconciler no longer writes transforms at all during a drag — ManipulationComponent owns the live transform and TransformFollowSystem also skips .gesture entities, so presented==what your hand is doing. (3) On release, WillEnd persists the final transform, writes it as the new TARGET, and releases to .data; the follow system is already at that target so there is no jump. (4) releaseBehavior=.stay prevents ManipulationComponent's own default .reset snap-back. Target-vs-presented separation means even a concurrent relayout only changes a card's target, which it would glide to AFTER the drag — never a snap-back.
proposals.howItFixesHoverBug: Root cause today: the × badge sits +0.006m inside a card collider inflated to depth max(z,0.03)≈3cm, so the parent encloses the child and the parent (with InputTargetComponent) is hit first — the badge never receives gaze, so its SwiftUI .hoverEffect scaleEffect never fires; and even if it did, a SwiftUI scale inside a ViewAttachment is not a dependable RealityKit hover for a placed entity. The fix is geometric + component-based: (1) The card collider is built once from SpatialTokens with a SHALLOW depth (~0.006m, matching a flat quad) so it no longer encloses anything in front of it. (2) The × control is offset to a real ~+0.025m gap clearly in front of (and/or outside) the card box, with its own small non-enclosed collider in CollisionGroup .control, so gaze resolves to the badge. (3) Highlight is driven by a RealityKit HoverEffectComponent(.highlight(...)) on the badge entity with its OWN HoverEffectComponent.GroupID distinct from the card's, so it activates independently and hover does not cascade from/swallow the parent. (4) The SwiftUI .hoverEffect is removed; for the playful 'pop', JitterSystem reads the badge's HoverStateComponent and applies a spring scale on the presented transform — a real, RealityKit-visible 3D effect, gated by reduceMotion. (5) The badge's tap is its own GestureComponent reading SpatialRoleComponent.role==.dismiss, not a name-prefix match in a global gesture, so routing is correct and type-safe.
proposals.scalabilityStory: The system grows by adding TOKEN ROWS and FACTORY CASES, not by editing two megafiles. A new entity kind = a SpatialTokens row (size/material/collision/hover) + a SpatialEntityFactory case + a SwiftUI body in SpatialCard; it automatically gets correct collision, hover GroupID, ownership-aware reconciliation, interpolation, and gesture routing. A new control (port, context menu, resize handle) = a Control factory case with its own CollisionGroup .control collider + GestureComponent + HoverEffectComponent GroupID — the dispatcher never grows because there is no central switch. A new continuous behavior (inertia, gravity, alignment guides) = a new System keyed by a marker Component, with zero change to reconcile. A new layout (the deferred topological pop-out) = a pure strategy function in SpatialLayoutEngine returning [Placement]; the reconciler and follow system animate to it for free. Interaction modes (a future 'schedule overlay') = an enum case on the @Observable model. The reconciler stays a small keyed differ regardless of entity count because it is O(changed), not O(all): edges diff by stable key, transforms write only for changed data-owned targets, collision/hover are one-time. SwiftUI-renders-content and the nonisolated layout engine are preserved verbatim, so the constraints scale with the design.
proposals.testability: The pure, simulator-free surface expands dramatically. (1) SpatialLayoutEngine and VolumeMetrics stay nonisolated Foundation/simd and get the unit tests they currently lack; the deferred workflow stepGraph layout moves OUT of the view model into the engine and becomes testable. (2) The reconciler's core is extracted as a nonisolated pure reducer: diff(previousLiveIDs, newRenderedEntities, ownershipByID) -> DiffResult{added, removed, targetWrites} — testable in isolation, asserting that gesture/animating entities never appear in targetWrites (a direct regression test for the drag snap-back) and that edges diff by stable key. (3) The intent→reduce logic (claim/release ownership, endDrag, connect/link mode transitions, returnToTray clearing manual placement) is pulled into nonisolated functions or a thin store and unit-tested (e.g. WillBegin sets .gesture; WillEnd persists then releases to .data). (4) SpatialTokens is a plain table — snapshot/assertion tested. (5) The frame-rate-independent damping math in TransformFollowSystem is a pure function (presented, target, dt) -> presented, unit-tested for convergence/no-overshoot. RealityKit Systems, gestures, glass, and HoverEffectComponent behavior remain verified by xcodebuild + Simulator (SourceKit reports false errors here — trust xcodebuild), as the constraints require.
risks: ManipulationComponent has a reported intermittent visionOS bug where translation can stop app-wide until device restart; keep the hand-rolled DragGesture (using the canonical translation-delta + start-transform-capture recipe) behind a feature flag as a fallback — but route BOTH through the same ownership-token handoff so the reconciler treats them identically.

These are visionOS 26 APIs (ManipulationComponent, GestureComponent, PresentationComponent, Entity.animate, Observable entities, .highlight/.shader hover, HoverEffectComponent.GroupID, CoordinateSpace3D). Fine for the 26.2 target, but verify each in Simulator since SourceKit indexes the wrong SDK and reports false errors; trust xcodebuild only.

Entity.animate inside the reconcile/update path is safe ONLY if it does not read observed entity state inside the closure (WWDC25 274 infinite-loop trap); the .animating ownership token plus running animations from event/gesture closures rather than the update body must be enforced by code review, or the loop bug re-emerges in a new form.

Frame-rate-independent damping must use SceneUpdateContext.deltaTime (variable/high refresh on visionOS); a fixed per-frame lerp assuming 90fps will desync. The damping function needs the dt-correct form (Rory Driscoll) and a no-overshoot clamp.

The shallow card collider (~0.006m) trades grab-affordance depth for correct child hit-testing; if direct (reach-out) input feels thin, prefer offsetting the collider backward rather than re-inflating its depth, to keep controls reachable.

Migration risk from two megafiles to ~10 files (factory, Systems, tokens, store) is real; sequence it (tokens+card → ownership token + reconciler guard to kill snap-back → collision+hover fix → Systems → ManipulationComponent) so each step is independently buildable and the two bugs are fixed early, before the larger refactor lands.
proposals.tradeoffs: Choosing the declarative-reconciler-with-ownership-tokens approach over fully delegating to ManipulationComponent everywhere keeps a SwiftUI-driven reconcile (matching the locked paradigm and the existing @Observable/relayout flow) and preserves precise control over clamping, type-cluster snapping, and persistence — at the cost of maintaining an explicit ownership protocol that ManipulationComponent would otherwise hide. We still ADOPT ManipulationComponent for the live drag (it is the near-trivial grab the developer wants) but subordinate it to our ownership token so the reconciler and Systems stay the single coordinating authority; this is slightly more wiring than 'just use ManipulationComponent', but it is what lets layout reflow, return-to-tray, and pop-out animate uniformly and lets a custom fallback drag drop in without changing the reconciler. Target-vs-presented separation adds an interpolation layer (one System + one component) that pure delete-and-re-set would avoid, but it is the structural reason snap-back and teleporting are impossible by construction. Per-entity GestureComponent + typed roles is more entities/components than one global gesture, but removes the unscalable string parser. Net: more types, far less coupling — the explicit cost the developer asked for to get a foundation that scales.

proposals.philosophy: HYBRID LAYERED: MVU for state, a thin ECS only for genuinely runtime concerns, and a design-token system for visuals — with one inviolable rule that dissolves both bugs: EXACTLY ONE AUTHORITY OWNS AN ENTITY'S TRANSFORM AT ANY INSTANT, and that authority is encoded in a RealityKit Component (TransformAuthorityComponent { .data | .gesture | .animating }) that a System reads, never in SwiftUI @State and never in the RealityView update closure. Data flows DOWN unidirectionally: the Domain store (@Observable, data only) → a Spatial Scene Model that reconciles data into a KEYED set of SpatialComponent view-models, each carrying an explicit interaction state machine (rest/hover/selected/dragging/disabled) → a thin reconciler that only adds/removes/re-attaches entities and writes a *target* transform + intent into components (never the live transform of a gesture/animation-owned entity) → a small set of MainActor RealityKit Systems that own all continuous transform evolution (DragFollowSystem, LayoutTweenSystem, HoverJitterSystem, EdgeSystem). Intents flow UP: gestures and ManipulationComponent events emit typed intents the Scene Model reduces into store writes. Visuals are a PURE FUNCTION of interaction state via a SpatialTokens design system. The pure SpatialLayoutEngine stays nonisolated/Foundation+simd-only and produces TARGETS, never touching entities. This is MRTK3's 'interactables are pure state, a separate visual driver renders' fused with WWDC25 274's 'mutate transforms only in gestures, Systems, or Entity.animate — never in update'.
proposals.oneLineThesis: Make transform ownership a typed Component that one System honors at a time, shrink the RealityView update closure to identity-only reconciliation, render every visual as a pure function of an explicit per-entity interaction state machine fed by typed intents — and grab-drag plus child-control hover become trivial because nothing fights the gesture and nothing is geometrically swallowed.

layers.name: L1 — Domain Store (DomainStore, @Observable, MainActor)
layers.responsibility: Pure data. Owns the SpatialScene, [SpatialEntity], [SpatialLink], tasksById/stepsById/userTaskTypes, manuallyMovedIds, and active interaction MODE (none/merge/link with picked source). The single source of truth for DATA — it never knows about RealityKit entities, transforms-in-flight, or rendering. All tRPC persistence (createEntity, batchUpdateEntityTransforms, connect, link, collapseWorkflow, setRendered) lives behind async actions here. This is today's SpatialSceneViewModel split in two: the data/CRUD/persist half. Crucially it stores positions only as the *committed* (persisted) transform; the live/animated transform never round-trips through it mid-interaction (kills the snap-back feedback path at the source).
layers.owns: SpatialScene, [SpatialEntity] (committed transforms only), [SpatialLink], task/step/type indices, manuallyMovedIds, InteractionMode, all tRPC writes.
keyTypes: InteractionMode enum { case idle; case merge(sourceId: String?); case link(sourceId: String?) }

layers.name: L2 — Spatial Scene Model (SceneModel, @Observable, MainActor)
layers.responsibility: The MVU 'view' of the scene: reconciles DomainStore data into a stable, KEYED [String: SpatialComponentVM] (id → view-model). Each SpatialComponentVM is the per-entity record holding: kind, refId, content snapshot (title/type/duration/note), the *target* transform (from layout engine or committed position), the InteractionState state machine value, selectionRole, and a contentVersion. SceneModel diffs old vs new keyed set and emits a SceneDiff { added, removed, contentChanged, targetChanged } — a PURE, unit-testable reducer (previous keyed set + new domain snapshot → diff). It also reduces typed Intents (from L3) back into DomainStore actions and InteractionState transitions. It runs the layout engine (L5) to compute targets. Nothing here touches RealityKit.
layers.owns: [String: SpatialComponentVM] keyed view-models, the InteractionState state machines, SceneDiff computation (pure), Intent reduction, layout-target computation.
keyTypes: SpatialComponentVM (struct: id, kind, content, targetTransform, state: InteractionState, selectionRole, contentVersion)
InteractionState enum { rest, hover, selected, dragging, disabled }
SceneDiff struct { added:[id], removed:[id], contentChanged:[id], targetChanged:[(id,SIMD3)] }
Intent enum { tapped(id), dragBegan(id), dragEnded(id,Transform), dismissTapped(id), hoverChanged(id,Bool), volumeToggled(id) }

layers.name: L3 — RealityKit Systems + Components (thin ECS, MainActor)
layers.responsibility: The ONLY code that writes a live entity transform. A tiny component vocabulary + 4 Systems own all continuous/runtime behavior. TransformAuthorityComponent is the linchpin: it marks who owns this entity's transform RIGHT NOW. The reconciler sets a LayoutTargetComponent and SystemS move the entity toward it ONLY when authority == .data. ManipulationComponent (visionOS 26) owns the transform during a drag (authority flips to .gesture on WillBegin, back to .data on WillEnd). HoverJitterSystem applies a low-amplitude spring offset on hover over a clean base. EdgeSystem repositions persistent, stably-keyed edge/port entities in place from live positions (no teardown). All Systems query by marker components via EntityQuery and tick in update(context:) with context.deltaTime.
layers.owns: Live entity TransformComponent (during .gesture/.animating), edge/port entity positions, hover jitter offsets, layout tween interpolation. Component registration + System registration at app launch.
keyTypes: TransformAuthorityComponent: Component { enum Owner { data, gesture, animating }; var owner; var sinceTime }
LayoutTargetComponent: Component { var target: SIMD3<Float>; var animateOnNextChange: Bool }
SpatialRoleComponent: Component { var kind: SpatialEntityKind; var role: EntityRole (node/control/edge/port/volume); var ownerId: String }
HoverStateComponent: Component { var isHovered: Bool; var phase: Float }
JitterComponent: Component { var base: SIMD3<Float>; var amplitude/frequency; var seed }
EdgeEndpointComponent: Component { var fromId/toId: String; var semantic: EdgeSemantic }
LayoutTweenSystem, DragFollowSystem (ManipulationEvents bridge), HoverJitterSystem, EdgeSystem (all : System)

layers.name: L4 — SwiftUI Content + Design Tokens (SwiftUI, MainActor)
layers.responsibility: Renders ALL content (locked paradigm). Every card is one SwiftUI primitive — SpatialCard(kind:tint:state:content:) — whose glass material level, tint, corner radius, border, elevation lift, and hover/selection treatment are PURE FUNCTIONS of (kind, InteractionState) read from SpatialTokens. The four card bodies (Node/TypePanel/Note/WorkflowVolume) become content-only and wrap in SpatialCard. The × control becomes its OWN attachment entity (sibling-level, not buried in the card collider) styled by a Control token. SpatialTokens is a nonisolated enum (Foundation/SwiftUI only) holding meters dimensions, radii, material levels per tier, semantic colors (one source of truth bridged to both SwiftUI Color and RealityKit UIColor with applyPostProcessToneMap:false), motion durations/easings, jitter amplitude, and reduce-motion/reduce-transparency fallbacks.
layers.owns: All SwiftUI views, SpatialCard primitive, DismissControl visual, SpatialTokens design system, edit/create sheets via PresentationComponent.
keyTypes: SpatialTokens (nonisolated enum: card dims, radii, glass levels, colors, motion)

layers.name: L5 — Pure Layout Engine (SpatialLayoutEngine + VolumeMetrics, nonisolated)
layers.responsibility: Unchanged contract, extended scope. Stays nonisolated, imports only Foundation/simd, takes plain projections and returns [Placement] (TARGETS). Gains a stepGraph(strategy:) function for workflow pop-out (topological levels) so the hand-rolled stepRowPositions leaves the view model and becomes a pure, unit-tested engine strategy. VolumeMetrics also gains per-kind card dimensions (bridged from SpatialTokens) so layout spacing, collision sizing, and rendered size all derive from one place. The engine produces targets; L3's LayoutTweenSystem applies them. The engine NEVER touches an entity, satisfying SWIFT_DEFAULT_ACTOR_ISOLATION=MainActor by being explicitly nonisolated.
layers.owns: Type-cluster layout, workflow step-graph layout, clamp/bounds math, target computation. Zero entity/RealityKit/MainActor dependency.

proposals.transformOwnershipModel: Single-writer ownership encoded as TransformAuthorityComponent { owner: .data | .gesture | .animating } on every movable entity. RULE: a layer may write an entity's TransformComponent ONLY when it is the current owner. (1) .data (rest/at-target): the reconciler may set LayoutTargetComponent; LayoutTweenSystem interpolates the entity toward the target via Entity.animate or a critically-damped per-frame follow using context.deltaTime. The reconciler NEVER writes the live transform directly — it writes a target component, and only for .data-owned entities. (2) .gesture (active drag): ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(entity, releaseBehavior: .stay, dynamics: translate-biased, collisionShapes:[tokenBox]) owns the transform; on ManipulationEvents.WillBegin DragFollowSystem flips owner→.gesture; DidUpdateTransform clamps to VolumeMetrics by constraining (not replacing) the value; WillEnd reads the final entity transform, emits Intent.dragEnded(id, transform) which the SceneModel reduces into a DomainStore persist + manuallyMovedIds insert, then flips owner→.data. (3) .animating (entrance/exit/return-to-tray/pop-out): Entity.animate(.spring) owns the transform; owner→.animating for the animation duration, restored to .data on completion. Because ownership lives in a Component the update closure can read but the gesture sets directly on the entity, there is no @State visibility race and no two-writer conflict. The committed position in DomainStore is written ONLY on WillEnd, so it is never stale-read mid-drag.
proposals.reconciliationModel: Identity-only, diff-driven, idempotent. The RealityView update closure calls SceneModel to compute a SceneDiff (pure: previousKeyedVMs + currentDomainSnapshot → {added, removed, contentChanged, targetChanged}) and then performs ONLY structural mutations: (1) removed → animate-out then content.remove on completion; (2) added → one-time EntityFactory.makeCard(vm) wiring ViewAttachmentComponent + a token-sized CollisionComponent (computed ONCE from SpatialTokens dimensions, never from per-pass visualBounds) + InputTargetComponent(allowedInputTypes:.indirect) + HoverEffectComponent(.highlight, GroupID) + SpatialRoleComponent + TransformAuthorityComponent(.data) + LayoutTargetComponent, then entrance-animate; (3) contentChanged → swap only the attachment rootView / re-key its content (no transform touch); (4) targetChanged → write LayoutTargetComponent.target ONLY for .data-owned entities (LayoutTweenSystem does the motion). The closure NEVER writes a live TransformComponent and NEVER re-asserts position every pass — eliminating the WWDC25-274 observed-state feedback loop. Edges are NOT rebuilt here: EdgeSystem maintains persistent, stably-keyed (sourceId+targetId+semantic) edge/port entities and repositions endpoints in place each frame from live positions, gated by an edgesDirty flag the SceneModel sets only on topology change.
proposals.interactionModel: Typed, per-entity, intent-based — no name parsing. SpatialRoleComponent { kind, role, ownerId } replaces the 'ctl::role::owner'/'edge::' string protocol; entity.name is ONLY the stable id. Movement is owned by ManipulationComponent (releaseBehavior:.stay) so there is no hand-rolled DragGesture, no value.convert math, no dragStart/draggingID @State. Discrete actions use visionOS 26 GestureComponent attached to specific entities: a card carries TapGesture→Intent.tapped(id); the × control carries its OWN TapGesture→Intent.dismissTapped(id); edges/ports carry no input. Hits route by CollisionGroup mask (.node/.control/.edge) as a backstop.
