All gesture closures emit a typed Intent (never mutate data or transform directly); SceneModel.reduce(state, intent) is a pure function that updates InteractionMode/InteractionState and dispatches DomainStore actions. Merge/Link cease to be view @State booleans — they become InteractionMode in the DomainStore, reduced from intents, so candidate highlighting is driven by observed selectionRole (not a transient flag) and entering a mode disables tap-to-edit/manipulation so a pick can't be misread.
proposals.hoverHighlightModel: Two strictly separated systems, both RealityKit-visible. (A) SYSTEM HOVER (gaze, applied out-of-process for privacy): every interactive entity gets HoverEffectComponent(.highlight(HighlightHoverEffectStyle(color: typeTint, strength:))) with a distinct HoverEffectComponent.GroupID per entity so a card and its × activate INDEPENDENTLY (hover does not cascade to children). The × control is its own entity with its own non-enclosed collider and its own GroupID — so it actually receives gaze. The SwiftUI .hoverEffect{scaleEffect} buried in DismissBadge is DELETED (it scales flat 2D content, not the placed entity, and is gated by a hit the parent swallowed). (B) APP SELECTION/FOCUS (fully app-owned): InteractionState (rest/hover/selected/dragging/disabled) on each SpatialComponentVM drives the SwiftUI card style (border weight, tint, elevation lift) via SpatialTokens — selectionRole replaces the merge-only isHighlighted bool and now expresses picked-for-merge/picked-for-link/selected/editing uniformly across all four card kinds. The two never read each other (you cannot read system hover in app code).
proposals.collisionModel: Hierarchical hit-testing designed, not accidental. ROOT CAUSE of bug #2 fixed three ways together: (1) the card's CollisionComponent depth is sized to the card's real thickness (~0.006–0.01m) from SpatialTokens, NOT inflated to max(z,0.03) — so it no longer encloses anything in front of it; (2) the × control is promoted to a SIBLING-level entity (not a child buried at +0.006z) positioned with a real z-gap clear of the card box, with its OWN token-sized CollisionComponent + InputTargetComponent + GestureComponent; (3) CollisionGroup/CollisionFilter categories (.node/.control/.edge) plus per-entity GestureComponent give the control intrinsic, type-safe targeting. Card colliders are generated ONCE at creation from token dimensions (no per-pass visualBounds → kills the recursive-bounds drift/feedback trap) and cached. Edges/ports get no InputTargetComponent and live in the .edge group excluded from all input masks, so they are structurally non-interactive rather than excluded by name prefix. allowedInputTypes:.indirect on cards/controls matches the comfortable gaze+pinch modality.
proposals.animationModel: All motion is owned by the .animating authority or by Systems, never by direct assignment in update. (1) Transitions use visionOS 26 Entity.animate(.spring/.easeInOut) { entity.transform = target } in System/event closures — entrance (scale 0.85→1.0 + opacity ramp on add), exit (shrink/fade before content.remove), return-to-tray (fly current→new column slot), workflow expand/collapse (steps emerge from the volume, staggered, with WorkflowVolumeCardView's now-real isExpanded), drag release settle. (2) LayoutTweenSystem glides .data-owned entities toward LayoutTargetComponent.target each frame (frame-rate-independent damped follow with context.deltaTime) so layout reflow animates instead of teleporting. (3) HoverJitterSystem reads HoverStateComponent and applies a low-amplitude sinusoidal offset + small spring pop over a CLEAN base transform (jitter never persisted, no drift) — this is the playful highlight. All durations/easings/amplitudes come from SpatialTokens.Motion; every animation is gated behind @Environment(\\.accessibilityReduceMotion) with a crossfade/static fallback, and JitterSystem is suppressed when reduce-motion is on (important for the ADHD audience). scaleProperty/sizeMultiplier is either wired through LayoutTweenSystem (animated scale on change) or deleted as dead code.
proposals.materialDesignSystem: SpatialTokens: a single nonisolated enum (Foundation/SwiftUI only, mirroring the VolumeMetrics 'no scattered magic numbers' precedent) that is the source of truth for visuals, replacing the per-view hardcoded 16/18/14 radii, 210/240/190/200 widths, 0.25/0.35/0.5/0.6 opacities, and literal .yellow. It holds: per-kind card dimensions (in points AND derived meters, bridging L4↔L5), corner radii, glass material LEVEL per elevation tier (HIG semantics: typePanel=.regular section, node/note=.thin interactive, volume=.thick recede, all via .glassBackgroundEffect() for spatial grounding), semantic colors (selection, dependencyEdge, linkEdge, noteAccent) exposed as both SwiftUI Color and RealityKit UIColor (edges use UnlitMaterial(color:, applyPostProcessToneMap:false) so mesh colors match cards), border weights per InteractionState, elevation lift per state, and Motion (durations/easings/spring params/jitter amplitude). A pure InteractionState→CardStyle function makes the SpatialCard primitive render any state consistently. Honors accessibilityReduceTransparency (swap to opaque/high-contrast surface) and conveys state redundantly (lift + border, never color alone).
proposals.howItFixesDragBug: Root cause today: reconcile() re-asserts card.position from the stale stored position on EVERY update pass, guarded only by a @State draggingID that the update closure cannot reliably see mid-gesture (SpatialSceneView.swift:246–250). Three layers kill it: (L3) ManipulationComponent owns the transform during the drag (authority=.gesture) so RealityKit, not the model, drives position — and releaseBehavior=.stay means no built-in reset either. (L2/L3) the reconciler is forbidden from writing a live transform; it writes LayoutTargetComponent only for .data-owned entities, and a .gesture-owned entity is structurally skipped by ownership, not by a racy flag — so there is nothing to fight the gesture. (L1) the committed DomainStore position is written ONLY on ManipulationEvents.WillEnd, so it is never stale-read during the drag and the on-release re-assert/feedback path is gone entirely. Snap-back is eliminated by construction: at every instant exactly one writer owns the transform.
proposals.howItFixesHoverBug: Root cause today: the × badge is a child only +0.006m in front of a card whose CollisionComponent is inflated to depth max(z,0.03)≈3cm, so the parent collider geometrically encloses the badge and (per Apple DTS forum 747256) the parent is always hit first — the badge never receives gaze/tap, so its SwiftUI .hoverEffect scaleEffect never fires. Fixed at the root: (collisionModel) the card collider is sized to real card thickness (~0.006–0.01m) so it stops enclosing anything; the × is promoted to a sibling-level entity with a real z-gap and its OWN token-sized non-enclosed CollisionComponent + InputTargetComponent. (hoverHighlightModel) the × gets a RealityKit HoverEffectComponent(.highlight) with its OWN HoverEffectComponent.GroupID so it activates independently of the card (hover does not cascade), and the unreliable SwiftUI .hoverEffect inside the attachment is deleted. (interactionModel) the × carries its own GestureComponent(TapGesture)→Intent.dismissTapped(id), routed by CollisionGroup .control mask — so its tap is intrinsic, not dependent on the parent or on name parsing. It now both highlights and dismisses reliably.
proposals.scalabilityStory: Adding a new entity kind or control is declarative, not surgical. A new kind = one SpatialComponentVM case + one SwiftUI content view wrapped in SpatialCard (tokens give it consistent glass/state visuals for free) + one EntityFactory recipe; it automatically gets collision/input/hover/authority/manipulation wiring, participates in the diff reconciler, and is laid out by adding a strategy to the pure engine. A new control = a sibling entity with a SpatialRoleComponent(role:.control) + GestureComponent emitting a new Intent case — no edits to a megafunction or a name parser. New interaction modes are InteractionMode cases reduced purely. New runtime behavior is a new System + marker Component, queried by EntityQuery, with zero changes to reconcile. Because behavior is sliced by lifetime (data→store, structure→reconciler, continuous→Systems, visuals→tokens) and dispatch is typed, complexity grows linearly with features instead of concentrating in two 600-line files. Edges scale via in-place EdgeSystem updates (O(changed) not O(all) per pass).
proposals.testability: High, because the load-bearing logic is pure and nonisolated. (1) SceneReducer.diff(old,new) and reduce(state,intent) are pure functions (keyed VMs + snapshot → SceneDiff; state + Intent → state + actions) unit-tested with no RealityKit/simulator — the dirty-diff and the merge/link/dismiss/drag-end state transitions all get regression tests. (2) SpatialLayoutEngine (type-cluster + new stepGraph topological layout) and VolumeMetrics stay nonisolated/Foundation+simd-only and get full coverage (currently zero in-target). (3) InteractionState transitions and InteractionState→CardStyle token mapping are pure and tested. (4) DomainStore actions are tested against a mock SpatialSceneService. RealityKit Systems and visuals are verified via xcodebuild -scheme TaskPlannerVision -destination 'generic/platform=visionOS Simulator' + Simulator (trusting xcodebuild over SourceKit), since Systems/hover/manipulation are not Vitest-able. Every new pure function gets a test per CLAUDE.md; the bug fixes get regression tests on the reducer (e.g. 'dragEnded persists exactly once and flips authority back to .data', 'reconciler never targets a .gesture-owned id').
risks: ManipulationComponent has a reported intermittent visionOS-26 bug where translation can stop app-wide until device restart — keep the hand-rolled DragGesture+ownership path behind a feature flag as a fallback, sharing the same TransformAuthorityComponent so the reconciler treats both uniformly.

visionOS 26-only APIs (ManipulationComponent, GestureComponent, PresentationComponent, ViewAttachmentComponent-as-component, Entity.animate, HoverEffect .highlight/GroupID, CoordinateSpace3D) are correct for the 26.2 target but newer/less battle-tested; the ECS foundations (System/Component/EntityQuery/HoverEffectComponent/InputTarget/Collision) are stable since visionOS 1, so anchor on those and isolate the 26-only pieces behind small adapters.
SourceKit reports false errors against the wrong SDK — every build claim must be validated with xcodebuild, not the editor.

Promoting the × to a sibling entity adds entity count and a second attachment per card; keep controls lightweight and pooled, and only attach when dismissEligible to avoid scene churn as the graph grows.

Migrating from the current two-file monolith is sizable; sequence it (Phase A: TransformAuthority + ManipulationComponent + identity-only reconcile to kill both bugs; Phase B: SpatialTokens + SpatialCard + state machine; Phase C: Systems for jitter/edges/tween + engine stepGraph) so each phase is independently shippable and verifiable.

Entity.animate ownership windows must restore authority to .data on completion or a crashed/cancelled animation could strand an entity as .animating; add a watchdog in LayoutTweenSystem that reclaims stale .animating entities past a max duration.
proposals.tradeoffs: More types and files than the current two-file approach (DomainStore, SceneModel, 4 Systems, ~6 Components, SpatialTokens, SpatialCard, EntityFactory) — deliberate: the cost buys single-writer transform safety, typed dispatch, and pure testability, and the research is unanimous that the monolith is the root of both bugs and the scaling wall. Choosing ManipulationComponent over a hand-rolled gesture trades some fine-grained control (its translation/clamping is partly internal) for correctness and triviality, mitigated by DidUpdateTransform clamping and the DragGesture fallback flag. Using a thin ECS only for genuinely-runtime concerns (not a full ECS-for-everything) keeps MVU's predictable unidirectional flow while getting per-frame Systems where they're actually needed (jitter/edges/tween) — avoiding the opposite failure of pushing identity/data into Systems. Tokens add an indirection layer over raw .background calls, but that indirection is exactly what makes global glass/accessibility/elevation changes one-line instead of four-file. Keeping the layout engine pure and nonisolated under SWIFT_DEFAULT_ACTOR_ISOLATION=MainActor requires explicit nonisolated annotations and plain-projection inputs (a small ergonomic tax) but preserves deterministic unit testing — a constraint we honor rather than fight.

judgments.lens: Correctness & bug elimination: does each proposal ACTUALLY fix the drag-snap-back and x-highlight bugs at the root with a mechanism that genuinely works in RealityKit/visionOS? fixesKnownBugs and idiomaticVisionOS weighted highest; hand-waving and any design that recreates the transform-ownership conflict penalized. Verified against the live code: SpatialSceneView.swift reconcile() re-asserts card.position in update: guarded only by @State draggingID (lines 20, 108, 246-249) — confirmed unreliable; card collider inflated to depth max(z,0.03) with the × child only +0.006m forward (lines 260, 291) — confirmed enclosure; × highlight is a SwiftUI .hoverEffect scaleEffect in the attachment (lines 463-465). All three proposals diagnose these correctly. Layout engine + VolumeMetrics confirmed nonisolated/pure; ManipulationComponent/GestureComponent currently unused; test target has exactly one trivial test file. Every factual claim the proposals make about the codebase checks out.

rankings.proposal: Proposal 3 (Hybrid Layered: MVU + thin ECS + design tokens)
strengths: Deepest root-cause framing of the drag bug: at the DATA layer the committed/persisted transform is the ONLY thing stored, and the live/animated transform never round-trips through the store mid-interaction — this severs the snap-back feedback path at its source, not just at the reconciler guard. Verified against endDrag/persistTransform (VM lines 353-380), which today writes position only on release; the proposal correctly keeps it that way and forbids stale mid-drag reads.

Promotes the × control to a SIBLING-level entity, not a child. This is the strongest geometric fix for bug #2: a sibling cannot be enclosed by the card's collision box at all, so it is independent of however the card depth is sized — more robust than merely offsetting a child forward.

Correctly scopes the thin ECS to ONLY genuinely-runtime concerns (DragFollowSystem, LayoutTweenSystem, HoverJitterSystem, EdgeSystem) while keeping identity/data in MVU. Avoids the over-ECS failure mode where data/identity get pushed into Systems and ownership gets murky.

Best testability story with concrete regression assertions: SceneReducer.diff(old,new) and reduce(state,intent) are pure nonisolated functions; named regression tests like 'reconciler never targets a .gesture-owned id' and 'dragEnded persists exactly once and flips authority back to .data' directly cover the two bugs. Fills the verified gap (test target has one trivial test).

Phasing explicitly fixes BOTH bugs in Phase A (TransformAuthority + ManipulationComponent + identity-only reconcile) before the larger token/Systems refactor — satisfies the lens's preference for fixing bugs early and independently shippable.
Adds an .animating watchdog in LayoutTweenSystem to reclaim entities stranded by a cancelled/crashed animation — the only proposal that closes this real failure mode.

Sibling × + own GroupID + distinct HoverEffectComponent(.highlight) + own GestureComponent->Intent.dismissTapped is a fully type-safe, RealityKit-native fix for both the hit-test and the highlight halves of bug #2; deletes the unreliable SwiftUI scaleEffect.
weaknesses: Promoting × to a sibling adds entity count and a second attachment per card; the proposal acknowledges this (pool/attach-only-when-eligible) but it is real scene churn as the graph grows.

Two new store layers (DomainStore + SceneModel) plus 4 Systems + ~6 Components + tokens + factory is a meaningfully larger type surface than the two-file status quo; more moving parts than P2 for the same bug outcome.
InteractionMode/InteractionState living in two places (DomainStore mode + per-VM state machine) is conceptually richer than needed for the immediate fix and could drift if not disciplined.

rankings.proposal: Proposal 2 (Declarative reconciler + per-entity ownership tokens, R3F discipline)
strengths: Sharpest causal explanation of WHY the existing fix fails and HOW the new one works: the authoritative ownership token lives in the @Observable model (NOT @State), so the very next reconcile pass observes ownershipByID[id]==.gesture and skips via `guard ownershipByID[id]==.data`. Directly addresses the verified failure (SpatialSceneView.swift line 20 @State draggingID set in a gesture closure at line 108 is not seen by update: at line 246). This is the most precise fix and it works even on the hand-rolled fallback drag path, because the guard reads observed state regardless of ManipulationComponent.

Lowest migration risk of the three: keeps the existing SwiftUI-driven reconcile and @Observable/relayout flow (matching the verified VM at lines 94-128) rather than introducing a second store layer; the change is additive (a token field + a guard) at the smallest blast radius that still fixes the bug at the root.

Most numerically honest animation model: explicitly calls out frame-rate-independent critically-damped follow using SceneUpdateContext.deltaTime (variable/high refresh on visionOS) with a no-overshoot clamp, and flags that a fixed 90fps lerp would desync. The TransformFollowSystem damping is a pure (presented,target,dt)->presented function it commits to unit-testing for convergence.

Correct, idiomatic × fix: shallow token-sized card collider, × offset to a real ~+0.025m gap with its own non-enclosed collider in CollisionGroup .control, own HoverEffectComponent GroupID, SwiftUI .hoverEffect deleted, tap via own GestureComponent reading role==.dismiss.

Extracts the reconciler core as a nonisolated pure reducer diff(previousLiveIDs,newRenderedEntities,ownershipByID)->DiffResult with a direct regression test that gesture/animating entities never appear in targetWrites.

Smart targetTransform vs presentedTransform split with interpolation between them means a concurrent relayout only changes a target the card glides to AFTER release — snap-back impossible by construction even without ManipulationComponent.
weaknesses: Stores the authoritative token in the @Observable model AND mirrors it on a TransformTargetComponent for Systems — a dual-home for the same fact that must be kept in sync; if the mirror lags, a System could mis-skip. P3's single Component home (read by both reconciler and Systems) is marginally cleaner here.

Keeps the live transform reasoning closer to the store than P3 does; while it persists only on WillEnd, it does not make the data-layer 'committed-only, never round-trips live' invariant as explicit as P3, so a future contributor could reintroduce a mid-drag store read.
Slightly less ambitious on edges/lifecycle than P1's full Systems treatment, though adequate for the requirements.

rankings.proposal: Proposal 1 (RealityKit-ECS-native: components are runtime truth, Systems are behavior)
strengths: Most idiomatically RealityKit-pure mental model: components ARE the runtime truth, Systems ARE the behavior, update: is identity-only — the textbook target-vs-presented split with one System owning the presented transform and the store/layout owning only the target. The 'one authority owns a transform at any instant, always a System reading a component, never the update closure' framing is exactly right for RealityKit.

Strongest scalability story: new kind = SpatialRole case + tokens + factory branch; new behavior = one Component + one System keyed by EntityQuery with zero edits to sync/gestures/existing Systems; new edge semantic = token + EdgeComponent case. Growth is genuinely additive, replacing the two megafunctions with small single-responsibility units.

EdgeSystem follows live endpoints every frame from stably-keyed (sourceId+targetId+semantic) edge/port entities — no teardown — directly replacing the current delete-and-re-add-all drawEdges (lines 311-374). O(changed) not O(E) is the best performance posture of the three.

Correct, complete × fix on all three axes (thin token collider, real forward gap, own collider + InputTargetComponent + HoverEffectComponent with distinct GroupID, RealityKit highlight replacing the SwiftUI scaleEffect, own GestureComponent reading DismissControlComponent).

Most thorough risk register, including the ManipulationComponent app-wide-translation-stall bug with a shared-contract DragGesture fallback, the MainActor-isolation-leak footgun for nonisolated SpatialLayoutEngine, and the content.animate-inside-sync feedback-loop trap.
weaknesses: Largest refactor (2 files -> ~15) and most state pushed into Components/Systems. Under the lens's penalty for designs that risk recreating the ownership conflict, the heaviest migration surface is the most likely to transiently reintroduce a two-writer conflict during partial staging — even though the FINAL design is conflict-free.

Most exposed to the gaze-privacy problem for the playful jitter/pop: it cannot read system gaze, so HoverStateComponent must be fed from the attachment's SwiftUI .onHover or a ShaderGraphMaterial HoverState authored in Reality Composer Pro. It flags this as a real spike risk — the heaviest unproven dependency among the three for a secondary (non-bug) feature.

Lowest simplicity: 11 Components + 6 Systems is more machinery than the two bugs require; the bug fix itself (authority component + ManipulationComponent + identity-only sync) is a subset that P2/P3 reach with far less surface.
Pushes the most behavior onto MainActor render-loop Systems under SWIFT_DEFAULT_ACTOR_ISOLATION=MainActor, raising the isolation-leak footgun it itself names; correct but operationally demanding.

bestIdeasToGraft: From P3: store ONLY the committed/persisted transform in the data layer and forbid the live/animated transform from round-tripping through the store mid-interaction. This severs the snap-back feedback path at the source (deeper than a reconciler guard) and matches the verified VM where endDrag/persistTransform already write only on release (VM lines 353-380).

From P2: put the AUTHORITATIVE transform-ownership token in the @Observable model (not @State, not only on the entity). This is the precise fix for the verified failure — @State draggingID set in a gesture closure (SpatialSceneView line 108) is not observed by the next update: pass (line 246) — and it works even on the fallback drag path because the reconciler guard `ownershipByID[id]==.data` reads observed state. (Best combined with P1/P3's component mirror for cheap System reads.)

From P3: promote the × control to a SIBLING entity rather than a forward-offset child. A sibling cannot be geometrically enclosed by the card's collision box under ANY depth, making the bug-#2 hit-test fix robust to future collider sizing changes.

From P3: phase the work so Phase A (TransformAuthority + ManipulationComponent(.stay) + identity-only reconcile + collision/×/GroupID fix) lands and fixes BOTH bugs before any token/Systems/store refactor — independently shippable and verifiable in Simulator.
From P3/P1: an .animating watchdog (max-duration reclaim) so a cancelled or crashed Entity.animate cannot strand an entity in the .animating authority and freeze its layout follow.

From P1: EdgeSystem that follows live endpoints every frame from stably-keyed (sourceId+targetId+semantic) persistent edge/port entities — eliminates the current O(E) delete-and-re-add drawEdges teardown (SpatialSceneView lines 311-374) and preserves in-flight edge animation/hover state.

From P2: frame-rate-independent critically-damped follow using SceneUpdateContext.deltaTime with a no-overshoot clamp (not a fixed 90fps lerp), as a pure (presented,target,dt)->presented function that is unit-testable for convergence.

Universal across all three (already grounded): size the card CollisionComponent ONCE from SpatialTokens card dimensions instead of per-pass visualBounds (kills the recursive-bounds drift trap at SpatialSceneView lines 255-264); give the × its own HoverEffectComponent.GroupID and a real RealityKit .highlight, deleting the SwiftUI .hoverEffect scaleEffect (lines 463-465).

Universal: extract the reconcile diff as a nonisolated pure reducer (previous live set + new model -> add/remove/target-write list) and assert in tests that gesture/animating ids never appear in transform/target writes — a direct regression test for the snap-back, filling the verified one-test gap.

From all three: move stepRowPositions (VM line 489) out of the view model into the pure SpatialLayoutEngine as a stepGraph strategy so workflow pop-out layout becomes unit-testable like the existing nonisolated layout().
judgments.recommendedWinner: Proposal 3 (Hybrid Layered: MVU + thin ECS + design tokens)

judgments.lens: Scalability & idiomaticity — which architecture best supports growing complexity (more entity kinds, richer interactions, workflow graph layouts, a real component/design system) and is most idiomatic to RealityKit+SwiftUI. Scalability and idiomaticVisionOS weighted highest (~0.27 each); fixesKnownBugs ~0.18; testability ~0.13; simplicity ~0.10; ambition ~0.05. Grounded against the real code: SpatialSceneView.swift (543 lines, reconcile re-asserts card.position guarded by @State draggingID at L245-250; card collider inflated to max(e.z,0.03) at L260; × badge at +0.006 at L291; SwiftUI .hoverEffect at L463-465 which project memory confirms 'did NOT visibly scale'), SpatialSceneViewModel.swift (605 lines, stepRowPositions ad-hoc layout L489-500, scaleProperty/sizeMultiplier never-wired view-only feature L25-37/287-301, manuallyMovedIds override mechanism), SpatialLayoutEngine + VolumeMetrics (both already nonisolated/Foundation-only/testable — the precedent all three cite for SpatialTokens), card views (hardcoded 190/200/240/210 widths, 14/16/18 radii, .yellow/.systemTeal/.systemOrange literals, mixed materials), and the test target (only AuthManager covered). None of the visionOS 26 APIs (ManipulationComponent/GestureComponent/custom Systems/Entity.animate) exist in the codebase yet — all three are net-new adoptions, so the visionOS-26-API risk is real and uniform across all three.

rankings.proposal: Proposal 3 — Hybrid Layered (MVU + thin ECS + design tokens)
strengths: Best calibration of the lens: a thin ECS used ONLY for genuinely-runtime concerns (DragFollow/LayoutTween/HoverJitter/Edge Systems) layered over MVU, so behavior is sliced by lifetime (data->DomainStore, structure->reconciler, continuous->Systems, visuals->tokens). This is exactly the axis along which complexity grows, so growth is additive (new kind = VM case + factory recipe + SwiftUI body; new behavior = one Component + one System) without touching megafunctions.

Highest testability of the three and the most concretely specified: SceneReducer.diff(old,new) and reduce(state,intent) are pure nonisolated functions yielding SceneDiff/actions, with NAMED regression tests ('reconciler never targets a .gesture-owned id', 'dragEnded persists exactly once and flips authority back to .data'). Directly closes the verified zero-coverage gap and matches CLAUDE.md's 'every bug fix gets a regression test'.

Splitting today's monolithic SpatialSceneViewModel into DomainStore (data/CRUD/persist, committed transforms only) + SceneModel (keyed VMs + interaction state machine) is the cleanest articulation of why snap-back dies at the source: the live transform never round-trips through committed data mid-drag.

Only proposal to harden the .animating ownership window with a watchdog in LayoutTweenSystem that reclaims stranded entities past a max duration — a concrete answer to the real 'cancelled animation strands authority' failure mode the other two leave implicit.

Strongest phasing plan tied to the verified bugs: Phase A (TransformAuthority + ManipulationComponent + identity-only reconcile) fixes BOTH bugs early and is independently shippable before the larger refactor lands — matches the project's 'each xcodebuild green' constraint.

Cites MRTK3 'interactables are pure state + separate visual driver' and WWDC25 274 ('mutate transforms only in gestures/Systems/Entity.animate, never in update') — both directly applicable and the 274 rule is independently confirmed by the project's reconcile-pitfalls memory.
weaknesses: Most surface area of the three at the data layer: a DomainStore + SceneModel split plus a SpatialComponentVM keyed map is more indirection than the current single @Observable VM; the two-layer MVU 'view of the scene' adds a translation step that proposals 1/2 keep flatter.

The keyed [String: SpatialComponentVM] is a second in-memory projection alongside the SpatialEntity rows and the RealityKit entities — three representations of the same thing to keep coherent; contentVersion helps but is one more invariant.
InteractionMode living in DomainStore (data layer) is arguably a UI concern leaking into the data store; proposal 2 keeps mode in the model more cleanly as observed interaction state.
Marginally more ambitious-than-needed for Milestone 2's actual scope (the deferred topological pop-out, scheduling overlay) — some of the scalability story is for features not yet on the roadmap.

rankings.proposal: Proposal 2 — Declarative reconciler (React-Three-Fiber) with ownership tokens in the @Observable model
strengths: Most precisely targets the documented root cause: the authoritative ownership token lives in the @Observable model (mirrored to a component for Systems), which is EXACTLY the project memory's recommended fix ('hold the dragging flag in the @Observable view model rather than view @State'). The 'guard ownershipByID[id] == .data' write gate is the single cleanest one-line expression of the snap-back fix.

Best fit with the project's LOCKED paradigm (SwiftUI renders content; @Observable + relayout drives state; nonisolated layout engine untouched). It keeps a SwiftUI-driven reconcile rather than inverting authority toward the Scene, so it adds the least friction against existing constraints — the lowest-risk path to idiomatic-for-THIS-codebase.

Highest simplicity-per-capability: ~10 files, one interpolation System + a few components, a small extractable pure diff reducer (previousLiveIDs + renderedEntities + ownershipByID -> DiffResult). Adds only what is needed to kill the bugs and enable smooth reflow.

The R3F 'write only props you own / keyed differ' discipline is a proven, well-understood mental model and maps cleanly onto RealityView.update; the target-vs-presented + critically-damped TransformFollowSystem (dt-correct, Rory Driscoll cited) is the right idiom for frame-rate-independent glide on visionOS variable refresh.

Scales the right way for the stated growth axes: new kind = token row + factory case + SwiftUI body; new behavior = a System keyed by a marker Component; new layout = a pure strategy in the untouched engine — all without growing a central switch.
weaknesses: Mirroring the ownership token in BOTH the @Observable model AND a TransformTargetComponent introduces a dual-source-of-truth that must be kept in sync; if the mirror drifts (e.g. a System reads the component while the model already changed), a subtle race can reappear — the very class of bug being eliminated. Needs a strict single-writer discipline the proposal asserts but doesn't fully mechanize.

Slightly less ECS-idiomatic than Proposal 1: keeping the authority in the model means RealityKit Systems are subordinate readers rather than the Scene being the runtime truth — a deliberate, defensible tradeoff for paradigm-fit, but it leaves some RealityKit-native leverage (Scene as runtime source) on the table for the highest-weighted idiomaticVisionOS axis.

Less explicit than Proposal 3 about the data/scene-model separation, so the reconciler still reads a fairly rich @Observable VM; the pure diff reducer is extracted but the surrounding VM stays larger than Proposal 3's DomainStore.
No watchdog/recovery for a stranded .animating entity (cancelled/crashed animation) — a gap Proposal 3 closes.

rankings.proposal: Proposal 1 — RealityKit-ECS-native (components are runtime truth, Systems are behavior)
strengths: Most scalable and most RealityKit-idiomatic at the limit: making per-entity runtime facts Components and all over-time behavior Systems is the textbook ECS pattern Apple's RealityKit is built around. Adding a behavior (magnetic snap, breathing-on-selected) is genuinely one Component + one System with zero edits to sync/gestures/existing Systems — the cleanest additive-growth story of the three.

Strongest articulation of the single-writer invariant: TransformAuthorityComponent is read by Systems off the render loop (not view @State the update closure can't see in time), and the update closure is 'structurally forbidden' from writing any System-owned transform — only LayoutTargetComponent (a request). This is the most rigorous expression of 'one authority owns a transform at any instant.'

Richest behavior catalog (LifecycleSystem for animated add/remove, JitterSystem with dt-integrated phase over a clean base so jitter never drifts the persisted transform, EdgeSystem following live endpoints with no teardown) — directly replaces the verified O(E) delete-and-re-add edge churn and the recursive-visualBounds drift trap.
Highest ambition and the most future-proof foundation if the spatial scene becomes the product's primary surface (many kinds, dense graphs, scheduling overlays).

Correctly identifies and keeps the nonisolated SpatialLayoutEngine called FROM the LayoutSystem so engine isolation doesn't leak under SWIFT_DEFAULT_ACTOR_ISOLATION=MainActor — a real footgun the others mention more briefly.
weaknesses: Lowest simplicity by a wide margin: ~15 files, ~11 component types, 6 Systems for a Milestone-2 scope where two specific bugs and a token system are the actual asks. Highest migration risk against the 'keep each xcodebuild green' constraint, and the most that can go wrong in a target with essentially no existing Swift test coverage.

Greatest tension with the project's LOCKED paradigm: declaring 'the Scene is the source of RUNTIME truth' inverts authority away from the @Observable model toward RealityKit, which is philosophically further from the established 'SwiftUI renders content; @Observable + relayout drives everything' design than Proposals 2/3. Idiomatic to RealityKit-in-the-abstract, but a larger departure from THIS codebase's idiom.

Testability is good but lower than 2/3 in practice: most of the value lives in Systems/visuals that are simulator-only (not Vitest/XCTest-able); the proposal does extract a pure SceneMutation reducer, but a larger fraction of behavior than the others sits behind the simulator-verification-only boundary.

Heavier dependence on the least-proven visionOS 26 surface (the HoverStateComponent feed from .onHover OR a ShaderGraphMaterial HoverState authored in Reality Composer Pro) — the proposal itself flags this needs an early spike; if .onHover is unreliable the jitter trigger requires material authoring, a real cost the others don't take on.
The 'inversion of authority' plus 11 components is more conceptual overhead for contributors than the bug-fix-driven framing of Proposal 3's phased plan.

bestIdeasToGraft: Transform-ownership encoded as a typed value with a single hard write gate. Whatever wins, the snap-back fix MUST be: authoritative ownership lives somewhere the RealityView update closure can read synchronously (NOT view @State — the project memory documents this exact failure), and the reconcile write path is literally `guard owner == .data` before touching any transform. Proposal 2's one-line gate is the clearest statement of it; Proposal 1's 'update closure is structurally forbidden from writing System-owned transforms' is the strongest framing of the invariant.

Adopt ManipulationComponent (releaseBehavior: .stay) as primary drag but KEEP the hand-rolled DragGesture+ownership path behind a feature flag that shares the same ownership token, so the reconciler treats both identically. All three independently propose this; it is the correct hedge against the reported intermittent visionOS-26 ManipulationComponent translation bug.

Fix the × bug as a TRIPLE fix, not one change: (a) shrink the card collider to real card thickness (~0.006m) sized from tokens — NOT visualBounds — killing both the enclosure and the recursive-bounds drift; (b) move the × to a real ~0.025-0.03m front gap (Proposal 3's promotion to a sibling entity is the cleanest); (c) make highlight a RealityKit HoverEffectComponent(.highlight) with its OWN distinct HoverEffectComponent.GroupID and DELETE the SwiftUI .hoverEffect (project memory confirms it does not visibly scale).

SpatialTokens as a nonisolated value-type design system, explicitly modeled on the existing VolumeMetrics 'single authority, no scattered magic numbers' precedent, with dimensions bridged in BOTH points (SwiftUI .frame) and meters (collision) from one source so the visual and hit region can never disagree. Collapse the four card views into one SpatialCard(kind:tint:state:) primitive. This is the highest-leverage scalability+idiomaticity win and is nearly identical across proposals 2 and 3.

Identity-only, diff-driven reconcile that NEVER re-stamps transforms and creates collision/input/hover components ONCE at creation (never per pass). Extract the diff as a pure nonisolated reducer (previous live set + new snapshot -> {added, removed, contentChanged, targetChanged}) so it is unit-testable without the simulator. Proposal 3's named regression tests ('reconciler never targets a .gesture-owned id', 'dragEnded persists exactly once') should be written verbatim.

Persistent, stably-keyed (sourceId+targetId+semantic) edge entities repositioned in place each frame by an EdgeSystem from live endpoints — replacing the verified O(E) delete-and-re-add-all teardown in drawEdges. Gate rebuilds behind an edgesDirty/topology-change flag (Proposal 3) so per-frame work is O(changed).

Move the ad-hoc stepRowPositions out of the VM into the pure SpatialLayoutEngine as a stepGraph/topological strategy (all three) so workflow pop-out layout becomes deterministic and unit-tested like the existing type-cluster layout — and wire (or delete) the dead scaleProperty/sizeMultiplier feature through the same target-vs-presented animation path.

Target-vs-presented split with a frame-rate-independent critically-damped follow System (dt-correct, no-overshoot clamp) so layout reflow, return-to-tray, and column repack glide instead of teleporting — and a concurrent relayout can only change a card's TARGET, never yank its presented transform.

Proposal 3's watchdog that reclaims a stranded .animating entity past a max duration — cheap insurance against a cancelled/crashed Entity.animate leaving an entity permanently un-layoutable; graft this regardless of winner.

Make merge/link an explicit interaction-MODE on the model (enum + source id) rather than transient view @State booleans, and disable manipulation/tap-to-edit while in a mode so a pick can't be misread as a drag — replacing the current connectMode/linkMode @State pair and driving candidate highlight from observed selection state.
judgments.recommendedWinner: Proposal 3 — Hybrid Layered (MVU + thin ECS + design tokens)

judgments.lens: Simplicity & testability (weighted highest): which proposal is most understandable, lowest-risk to implement incrementally from the current SpatialSceneView + SpatialSceneViewModel code (~1150 lines across the two contested files, with the pure SpatialLayoutEngine/VolumeMetrics already extracted), and most unit-testable — preserving the nonisolated pure layout engine and adding MORE pure, simulator-free logic. Scalability/idiomaticVisionOS/fixesKnownBugs/ambition weighted lower; ambition is mildly penalized when it inflates surface area and Simulator-only (non-unit-testable) code. Grounded against the actual code: drag snap-back root cause confirmed at SpatialSceneView.swift:245-250 (reconcile re-asserts card.position from stale VM, guarded only by @State draggingID that the update closure cannot reliably observe mid-gesture); hover/× root cause confirmed at :255-264 (collider inflated to max(z,0.03)) + :291 (badge only +0.006 in front, so the parent encloses it) + :463-465 (highlight is a SwiftUI .hoverEffect{scaleEffect} inside the attachment); edges torn down and rebuilt every pass at :311-314; name-prefix 'ctl::'/'edge::' parsing at :83-95; hardcoded radii 14/16/18 + widths 190/240/210/200 + literal .yellow/.systemTeal/.systemOrange in the four card views; spatial target test coverage is effectively zero. All three proposals correctly diagnose every one of these.

rankings.proposal: Proposal 2 — Declarative reconciler with ownership tokens in the @Observable model (R3F discipline)
strengths: Cleanest root-cause fix for the drag bug under my lens: it puts the AUTHORITATIVE ownership token in the @Observable model (ownershipByID), and explicitly explains WHY the current @State draggingID guard fails — @State mutated inside a RealityKit gesture closure is not reliably visible to the next RealityView.update pass, but an @Observable field set in that same closure IS observed by the reconciler. The fix is a single `guard ownershipByID[id] == .data` in the write path. This is the most direct, lowest-machinery correction of the actual race at SpatialSceneView.swift:245-250.

Most testable expression of the fix: the reconcile core is extracted as a nonisolated pure reducer `diff(previousLiveIDs, newRenderedEntities, ownershipByID) -> DiffResult{added, removed, targetWrites}`, with the precise regression assertion that gesture/animating ids never appear in targetWrites — a unit test that directly encodes the snap-back fix with no Simulator. Plus the dt-correct damping math as a pure `(presented, target, dt) -> presented` function tested for convergence/no-overshoot.

Lowest conceptual departure from the existing architecture: it KEEPS the SwiftUI-driven reconcile called from RealityView.update (the locked paradigm and the current relayout flow), so the migration is an evolution of the existing reconcile rather than an inversion of authority. This is the lowest-risk incremental path.

Preserves and correctly extends the pure layer: SpatialLayoutEngine/VolumeMetrics stay nonisolated Foundation/simd, the deferred workflow stepGraph layout MOVES OUT of the view model into the engine (becoming testable), matching the existing extraction pattern and the rules' nonisolated constraint.

Fewer moving parts than P1/P3: 3 Systems (TransformFollow/EdgeFollow/Jitter), ~10 files — every bug-fixing piece is small and the contested transform is governed by one observed token, which is the most understandable mental model of the three.

Correctly fixes the hover bug at the geometry + component level (thin token collider, real ~0.025m gap or footprint offset for the ×, own non-enclosed collider, own HoverEffectComponent.GroupID, delete the SwiftUI scaleEffect) and adopts ManipulationComponent releaseBehavior:.stay to also kill the framework's built-in .reset snap-back.
weaknesses: Keeps an explicit ownership protocol that ManipulationComponent would otherwise hide — slightly more wiring than 'just use ManipulationComponent', though it is what enables uniform layout/return-to-tray/pop-out animation.

Mirrors the token onto the entity (TransformTargetComponent.ownership) for cheap System reads in addition to the model — two homes for the same fact, a small consistency surface to keep in sync (handoff must update both).
Migration sequencing is described but less explicitly staged-to-ship-each-step than P3's Phase A/B/C; the developer must derive the safe ordering from the risks list.

Target-vs-presented interpolation layer (TransformFollowSystem) is Simulator-verified for its visual behavior even though its damping math is unit-tested — a small irreducible non-unit-testable surface.

rankings.proposal: Proposal 3 — Hybrid layered: MVU for state, thin ECS for runtime, design tokens for visuals
strengths: Best incremental de-risking story of all three: an explicit Phase A (TransformAuthorityComponent + ManipulationComponent + identity-only reconcile) that fixes BOTH bugs first and is independently shippable, BEFORE the larger Phase B (tokens/card/state machine) and Phase C (Systems/stepGraph). This is the safest path off the current code and directly serves the 'lowest-risk to implement incrementally' lens.

Broadest pure-testability surface: it tests BOTH the structural diff (SceneReducer.diff(old,new) -> SceneDiff) AND the intent reduction (reduce(state, intent) -> state + actions), so the merge/link/dismiss/drag-end STATE MACHINES get regression tests, not just the transform-write filter. This is the most complete simulator-free coverage of behavior, including 'dragEnded persists exactly once and flips authority back to .data'.

Cleanest separation of concerns by lifetime: data->DomainStore, structure->reconciler/SceneModel, continuous->Systems, visuals->tokens. Splitting the 605-line view model into DomainStore (data/CRUD/persist) + SceneModel (keyed VM diff + intent reduce) is a principled decomposition of the actual god-object, and the DomainStore stores only the COMMITTED transform so the snap-back feedback path is severed at the source (L1).
Most defensively engineered: a watchdog in LayoutTweenSystem reclaims entities stranded as .animating after a cancelled/crashed animation — a real failure mode the others don't address.

Strong design-system + accessibility framing for the ADHD audience (redundant non-color selection cues, reduce-motion/reduce-transparency fallbacks), with the one-source-of-truth SpatialTokens correctly replacing the confirmed hardcoded radii/widths/colors.
Idiomatic restraint: 'thin ECS only for genuinely-runtime concerns' avoids P1's over-rotation into ECS-for-everything while still getting per-frame Systems where actually needed (jitter/edges/tween).
weaknesses: Highest conceptual layer count of the practical two: DomainStore + SceneModel split + thin ECS + tokens + EntityFactory + SpatialCard is more to learn up front than P2's single-model-token model, so on raw simplicity it trails P2 even though every individual unit is small.

Puts the AUTHORITATIVE ownership token in a Component (TransformAuthorityComponent on the entity) rather than the observed model — functionally correct, but marginally less clean than P2 for testing the ownership DECISION in pure Swift, and slightly closer to the entity-as-truth idea that the failed @State guard cautions against (mitigated because the update closure can read the Component, unlike @State).
Two @Observable objects (DomainStore + SceneModel) plus a keyed VM map is a richer state graph than the current single VM; correct, but more invalidation surface to reason about during the migration.
Largest number of new types overall after P1; the value is real but the upfront cost is the highest among the non-ECS-native options.

rankings.proposal: Proposal 1 — RealityKit-ECS-native: Components are runtime truth, Systems are behavior
strengths: Most scalable and most idiomatic to a mature RealityKit codebase: new kind = role case + token row + card branch; new behavior = one Component + one System keyed by EntityQuery, with zero edits to the sync, gestures, or existing Systems. Growth is purely additive — the strongest long-horizon architecture.

Most rigorous transform-ownership model: the target-vs-presented split with a TransformAuthorityComponent read by exactly one System at a time is the textbook correct elimination of the snap-back, and the WWDC25-274 'never mutate observed transforms in update' rule is honored by construction.

Cleanest long-term reconcile: identity-only SceneSync that writes only LayoutTargetComponent (a request, never a transform a System owns), with edges followed in place (O(changed), not the current O(all) teardown at :311-314).

Extracts a pure reducer (previousSceneState + new snapshot -> [SceneMutation]) so the decision logic under the sync is unit-testable even though the Systems are not — the best testability achievable for a fully ECS design.

Thorough, honest risk register (ManipulationComponent app-wide-translation bug + fallback flag, gaze-hover privacy forcing .onHover/ShaderGraph HoverState, nonisolated-engine leak under MainActor default isolation, animate-in-sync feedback risk).
weaknesses: Worst on the weighted lens: largest surface area (~15 files, 6 Systems), and the most load-bearing behavior (Drag/Layout/Jitter/Hover/Edge/Lifecycle Systems) is verifiable ONLY in the Simulator — RealityKit Systems are not Vitest/unit-testable, so the most code is the least unit-covered, which the lens penalizes.

Largest mental departure from the current SwiftUI-data-driven code: 'the Scene is the source of RUNTIME truth' inverts today's model. That is powerful but the highest-risk incremental migration from the existing two files; staging it green at each step is acknowledged as hard.

Authoritative runtime state living on entities (Components) is the least pure-testable of the three for the ownership DECISION itself — the very thing the failed @State guard suggests should live in observed model state, not on the entity.

Highest accidental-complexity risk: ~11 Components + 6 Systems is a lot of intertwined runtime state for a workspace that currently renders a few dozen cards; the playful-jitter trigger additionally depends on the unresolved gaze-hover privacy path (possibly requiring Reality Composer Pro ShaderGraph authoring), a heavyweight spike.

Ambition actively works against the prime directive here: the developer wants the two bugs killed and a testable foundation, and this delivers the most architecture for the least incremental safety and unit coverage.

bestIdeasToGraft: From P3: the explicit phased migration — Phase A (TransformAuthority/ownership token + ManipulationComponent releaseBehavior:.stay + identity-only reconcile guard) that fixes BOTH bugs and ships independently, BEFORE the larger token/card/state-machine and Systems refactors. This is the single most important risk-reducer and should be adopted regardless of winner.

From P3: test BOTH the structural diff AND the intent/state-machine reduction as pure functions — i.e. add SceneReducer.reduce(state, intent) regression tests for merge/link/dismiss/drag-end ('dragEnded persists exactly once and flips authority back to .data'), not just the transform-write filter. Broadest simulator-free behavior coverage.
From P3: the watchdog that reclaims entities stranded as .animating past a max duration after a cancelled/crashed animation — a real correctness gap the others miss.

From P3: DomainStore stores ONLY the committed (persisted) transform; the live/animated transform never round-trips through the model mid-interaction — severs the snap-back feedback path at the data source, complementing the ownership guard.

From P2: keep the AUTHORITATIVE ownership token in the @Observable model (not solely on the entity). It directly explains and fixes why the current @State draggingID guard races, and it makes the ownership decision unit-testable in pure Swift. The entity Component mirror should be a derived cache, not the source of truth.

From P2 (and P3): the precise unit-test regression — assert the reconcile reducer NEVER writes a target/transform for a .gesture- or .animating-owned id. This encodes the snap-back fix as a permanent test.

Universal (all three): move the deferred workflow stepGraph layout OUT of the view model INTO the nonisolated SpatialLayoutEngine as a pure strategy, deleting the ad-hoc stepRowPositions and gaining unit tests — preserves and extends the existing pure-engine pattern.

Universal: bridge SpatialTokens dimensions to BOTH points (SwiftUI .frame) and meters (CollisionComponent), size the card collider from tokens (NOT per-pass visualBounds, killing the recursive-bounds drift), and use UnlitMaterial(applyPostProcessToneMap:false) so edge mesh colors match the SwiftUI palette.

From P1: edges as persistent, stably-keyed (sourceId+targetId+semantic) entities followed in place by an EdgeFollowSystem — replacing the O(all) delete-and-re-add teardown at SpatialSceneView.swift:311-314; adopt even in a lighter design.
From P1: the SceneMutation list as the pure reducer output type ([.add/.remove/.refreshContent/.retarget]) is a clean, testable boundary the winner's reconciler should emit.
judgments.recommendedWinner: Proposal 2 — Declarative reconciler with ownership tokens in the @Observable model (R3F discipline)

designDoc:
I'll synthesize the definitive architecture document. This is a pure writing/synthesis task — my output is the deliverable itself.

# Production-Grade visionOS Spatial UI Architecture — Task Planner Spatial Scene

> Status: Lead-architect synthesis. Target: visionOS 26.2, Xcode 16, SwiftUI + RealityKit. Verify all builds with `xcodebuild -scheme TaskPlannerVision -destination 'generic/platform=visionOS Simulator'` (SourceKit reports false errors against the wrong SDK — trust xcodebuild only).

---

## 1. Executive summary & design thesis

The current spatial scene works by accident, not by architecture. A single `reconcile(content:attachments:)` runs inside the RealityView `update:` closure on *every* observed `@Observable` change and re-asserts the full state of every entity from scratch — re-stamping each card's transform from a stale data-model position, regenerating its collision box from `visualBounds`, rebuilding the `x` control, and tearing down and re-adding every edge. This makes the reconcile pass the *de-facto owner* of every entity transform at every instant — including instants when a live gesture or an animation is the legitimate owner. The two headline bugs are not isolated defects; they are the two places where this missing-foundation pathology becomes visible to the user.

**Design thesis.** Every serious cross-engine spatial/UI stack — RealityKit's own ECS, React-Three-Fiber, Unity XRI, MRTK3, Bevy — converges on the same three rules:

1. **Exactly one authority owns an entity's transform at any instant**, and that authority is encoded somewhere a per-frame reader can see synchronously — never in SwiftUI `@State`, which is not reliably visible to the `update:` closure mid-gesture.
2. **The reconcile pass writes only identity** (add/remove/attach/retarget), never a transform owned by a gesture or animation. Continuous behavior lives in **RealityKit Systems**, the discrete one-shot belongs to **gesture/event closures**, and reactive identity belongs to the **declarative sync**.
3. **Visuals are a pure function of an explicit interaction state**, driven by a centralized **design-token** system — not hand-coded per card view.

This document specifies **one** architecture built on the highest-scoring proposal — **Proposal 3 (Hybrid Layered: MVU + a thin ECS for runtime concerns + a design-token system)** — grafting the highest-value ideas the judges identified from Proposals 1 and 2:

- **From P2 (R3F discipline):** the authoritative transform-ownership token lives in the `@Observable` model with a single hard write-gate (`guard owner == .data`), so the fix works even on the hand-rolled fallback drag path; a frame-rate-independent critically-damped follow using `SceneUpdateContext.deltaTime`; and a nonisolated pure diff reducer with a regression test asserting gesture/animating ids never appear in transform writes.
- **From P1 (ECS-native):** a persistent, stably-keyed `EdgeSystem` that follows live endpoints in place (O(changed), not O(E) teardown); the rigorous "the update closure is structurally forbidden from writing System-owned transforms" framing.
- **From P3 itself:** the data layer stores *only* the committed transform (the live transform never round-trips through the store mid-drag — severing the snap-back feedback path at its source); the `x` control promoted to a **sibling** entity (robust to any future collider sizing); a phased plan whose Phase A fixes both bugs before any large refactor; and an `.animating` watchdog.

The result: grab-and-drag becomes near-trivial (RealityKit's `ManipulationComponent` owns the transform during a drag, with releaseBehavior `.stay`), the `x` highlights and dismisses reliably (sibling collider + its own `HoverEffectComponent` `GroupID` + its own `GestureComponent`), and the system grows by adding token rows, components, and Systems rather than by editing two 600-line megafiles.

---

## 2. Root-cause analysis the architecture is built to fix

Each root cause is tied to file:line evidence from the gap analysis against the live `SpatialSceneView.swift` (543 lines) and `SpatialSceneViewModel.swift` (605 lines).

### 2.1 Transform / state ownership (the drag snap-back)

**Symptom.** Dragging a card snaps it to its tray slot / back wall and it jerks back on release.

**Root cause.** The `update:` closure is the de-facto transform owner. Each pass it re-asserts every non-dragged card to the data-model's *stored* position, which is only written on release and therefore stale mid-drag.

- `SpatialSceneView.swift:245-250` — `if entity.id != draggingID { card.position = VolumeMetrics.standard.clamp(SIMD3(positionX, positionY, positionZ)) }`, executed from the update closure (`:25-26`) which reads `viewModel.renderedEntities` (`:231`).
- The only guard is `@State var draggingID` (`:20`), set in the gesture `onChanged` (`:108`), read in reconcile (`:246`), cleared in `onEnded` (`:114`). **`@State` mutated inside a RealityKit gesture closure is not reliably visible to the `update:` closure before the next pass** — confirmed by the developer's failed fix and by WWDC25 274 (the update closure is body evaluation, not gesture timing). The guard races; the card is re-stamped to its stale slot.
- Two unsynchronized write paths for the same value: live transform (gesture, `:110`) and stored `positionX/Y/Z` (VM `:139-141`, `:344-349`, `:354`). The origin `(0,0,0)` is overloaded as both a "needs placement" sentinel (VM `:137`, `abs(axis) < 1e-4`) and a legal coordinate.
- WWDC25 274 is explicit: **never mutate observed entity state inside the update closure** — it creates a feedback loop. The current design does exactly that.

### 2.2 Hierarchical collision / hit-testing (the `x` is unreachable)

**Symptom.** The `x` dismiss control never receives hover or tap.

**Root cause.** The parent card's collision volume geometrically *encloses* the child badge, and (per Apple DTS, forum 747256) **an enclosing parent with an `InputTargetComponent` is always hit first**, so the child is unreachable.

- Card collider depth is inflated to `max(e.z, 0.03)` ≈ 3 cm (`SpatialSceneView.swift:259-260`), centered on the card → spans `center.z ± 0.015`.
- The badge is a *child* parented at only `+0.006 m` in front (`:291`) with its own 0.02-deep box (`:302`, `[0.05,0.05,0.02]`), so its volume `[center.z−0.004 … center.z+0.016]` is almost entirely inside the parent's `[center.z−0.015 … center.z+0.015]`.
- The card collider is regenerated from `card.visualBounds(recursive:false)` *every pass* (`:255-264`) — wasteful churn and the documented recursive-visualBounds drift trap.

### 2.3 Hover / highlight / focus (no visible highlight, no subsystem)

**Symptom.** The `x` doesn't visibly highlight on hover; there is no general hover/selection/focus feedback.

**Root cause.** Highlight is attempted with a SwiftUI `.hoverEffect { effect.scaleEffect(...) }` *inside* the `ViewAttachmentComponent` rootView (`SpatialSceneView.swift:463-465`). That scales flat 2D SwiftUI content in its own space; it is not a dependable RealityKit-visible signal for a placed entity, and it can only fire if the entity is hover-hit-tested at all (it isn't — see 2.2). Apple's gaze hover is applied *out of process* for privacy, so app code can never read a live "is hovered" boolean to drive a transform.

- Cards get a bare default-style `HoverEffectComponent()` re-`set` every pass (`:254`); the badge gets a bare one once (`:303`). No `.highlight`/`.shader` style, **no `GroupID`** (so hover propagates ambiguously between card and child).
- The only app-level "highlight" is `isHighlighted` on `NodeCardView` (`NodeCardView.swift:10, 43-44`), wired solely to merge/link `isPicked` (`SpatialSceneView.swift:186-188`). No rest/hover/selected/dragging/disabled vocabulary exists; the VM exposes no hover/selection fields.

### 2.4 Absent animation

**Symptom.** Every transition is an instant teleport.

**Root cause.** There is no animation layer at all — a repo-wide grep for `animate`/`move(to:`/`content.animate`/spring/`AnimationResource` returns zero hits. The data-model position *is* the presented position (`:246-250`); there is no target-vs-presented split, so any change is by construction a jump.

- Create: `content.add(card)` then hard position set, no entrance (`:242-250`).
- Remove: instant `content.remove` (`:235-236`).
- Layout reflow / return-to-tray: hard re-stamp (VM `:139-141`, `:522-526`).
- Workflow expand/collapse: toggles `isRendered` + sets positions, no transition (VM `:438-465`); `WorkflowVolumeCardView(isExpanded:)` is hardcoded `false` (`:404`).
- `reduceMotion` is never referenced (ADHD-audience requirement unmet).

### 2.5 Ad-hoc materials / no design system

**Symptom.** Inconsistent glass, scattered magic numbers, split color sources.

**Root cause.** No token/theme file exists anywhere under `TaskPlannerVision/`. Each of the four card views hardcodes its own constants.

- Corner radii diverge: 16 (`NodeCardView.swift:39,41`), 18 (`TypePanelCardView.swift:22,23,25`), 14 (`NoteCardView.swift:12,13`), 16 (`WorkflowVolumeCardView.swift:19,21`). Widths: 210 / 240 / 190 / 200.
- Split color source: cards use `UserTaskType.swiftUIColor` (hex), but edges use `UnlitMaterial(color: .systemTeal/.systemOrange)` (`SpatialSceneView.swift:330,337,357,368`) — a disconnected pipeline. The note is the literal `.yellow` (`NoteCardView.swift:12`). Violates CLAUDE.md's "use `getTypeColor()`; zero hardcoded colors."
- Glass is inconsistent: `.regularMaterial` (Node, TypePanel), `.thinMaterial` (Volume), raw color-under-material (Note, TypePanel); `.glassBackgroundEffect()` is used only for the mode banner (`:211`). No `reduceTransparency`/`increaseContrast` handling.

### 2.6 The monolith

**Symptom.** All behavior crammed into one View + one ViewModel; near-zero testability.

**Root cause.** No ECS layer and no reusable component abstraction. Grep for `System`/`registerSystem`/`EntityQuery`/`: Component`/`ManipulationComponent`/`GestureComponent`/`entity.animate` across `TaskPlannerVision` → **no matches**. The 543-line `SpatialSceneView` owns gestures, name-prefix dispatch (`:83-95`), the full reconcile (`:230-271`), control construction (`:280-306`), edge meshes (`:311-374`), card selection (`:391-427`), spawn math, and four embedded sheets. The 605-line VM owns data, layout orchestration, materialization, CRUD, connect/link/collapse, dead `ScaleProperty`/`sizeMultiplier` (`:25-37`, `:287-301`), and hand-rolled `stepRowPositions` (`:489-500`) that bypasses the pure engine. The only decomposed, testable units are `SpatialLayoutEngine` (103 lines) and `VolumeMetrics` (54 lines); the only test in target is an unrelated `AuthManager` test.

---

## 3. Layered architecture

Behavior is sliced by **lifetime**: canonical data flows *down*, behavior runs *forward* each frame in Systems, intents flow *up* from gestures, and visuals are a pure function of state. Data flows down through layers; gestures emit intents back up.

```
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │ L1 Domain Store (@Observable, MainActor)  ── canonical/persisted truth    │
 │    SpatialEntity rows (COMMITTED transform only), links, task/step/type   │
 │    indices, manuallyMovedIds, InteractionMode, ownershipByID token map    │
 └───────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────▲─────────────┘
   seed/snapshot │                                            │ intents reduce
 ┌───────────────▼───────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────┐
 │ L2 Scene Model (@Observable, MainActor)  ── declarative "view" of scene   │
 │    [id: SpatialComponentVM] keyed map (kind, content, targetTransform,    │
 │    InteractionState, selectionRole, contentVersion)                       │
 │    SceneReducer (NONISOLATED PURE): diff(old,new)->SceneDiff,             │
 │                                     reduce(state,intent)->state+actions    │
 └───────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────▲─────────────┘
   SceneDiff +    │                                            │ typed Intent
   target writes  │                                            │
 ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────┴───────────┐
 │ L3 RealityKit Systems +        │     │ L4 SwiftUI content + design tokens │
 │    Components (thin ECS)        │◄────┤    SpatialCard(kind:tint:state:)   │
 │    ── ONLY writer of live       │     │    SpatialTokens / SpatialMotion   │
 │       transforms ──             │     │    (renders ALL content)           │
 │  DragSystem/LayoutTweenSystem/  │     └────────────────────────────────────┘
 │  HoverJitterSystem/EdgeSystem/  │
 │  LifecycleSystem                │
 └───────────────┬────────────────┘
   reads targets  │  computes targets
 ┌───────────────▼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │ L5 Pure Layout Engine (NONISOLATED, Foundation/simd only)                 │
 │    SpatialLayoutEngine (type-cluster + stepGraph), VolumeMetrics          │
 │    → produces TARGETS; never touches an Entity                            │
 └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### L1 — Domain Store (`DomainStore`, `@Observable`, MainActor)

- **Responsibility.** Canonical/persisted truth and the only thing that talks to tRPC. Owns load, create, connect/merge, link, collapse, note edits, and transform **persistence on release**.
- **Owns.** `SpatialEntity` rows holding only the **committed** transform; `SpatialLink`; task/step/type indices; `manuallyMovedIds`; `InteractionMode` (`idle`/`merge(sourceId)`/`link(sourceId)`); **`ownershipByID: [String: TransformOwnership]`** (the authoritative token, grafted from P2).
- **Key types.** `DomainStore`, `SpatialEntity`, `SpatialLink`, `InteractionMode`, `SpatialAction`, `TransformOwnership`.
- **Must NEVER.** Read or write a live entity transform; let the live/animated transform round-trip through a committed `SpatialEntity` row mid-interaction; import any RealityKit type.

### L2 — Scene Model (`SceneModel`, `@Observable`, MainActor)

- **Responsibility.** The declarative "view" of the scene. Reconciles `DomainStore` data into a stable keyed `[String: SpatialComponentVM]`; computes a `SceneDiff`; reduces typed `Intent`s back into `DomainStore` actions and `InteractionState` transitions; runs L5 to compute targets.
- **Owns.** Keyed VMs; the per-entity interaction state machines; the **pure** `SceneReducer`.
- **Key types.** `SceneModel`, `SpatialComponentVM`, `InteractionState {rest,hover,selected,dragging,disabled}`, `SelectionRole {none,pickedMerge,pickedLink,editing}`, `SceneDiff {added, removed, contentChanged, targetChanged}`, `Intent`, `SceneReducer` (nonisolated).
- **Must NEVER.** Touch a RealityKit `Entity`; write a live transform; perform tRPC I/O directly (it dispatches `DomainStore` actions).

### L3 — Systems + Components (thin ECS, MainActor render loop)

- **Responsibility.** The **only** code that writes a live entity `TransformComponent`/scale/visual. Each System queries by marker components via `EntityQuery` and ticks in `update(context:)`. Every System respects the ownership token so authorities never collide.
- **Owns.** Live transforms during `.gesture`/`.animating`; layout-tween interpolation for `.data` entities; edge endpoint positions; hover jitter offsets; entrance/exit lifecycle. Component + System registration at app launch.
- **Key types.** Systems: `DragSystem` (bridges `ManipulationEvents`), `LayoutTweenSystem`, `HoverJitterSystem`, `EdgeSystem`, `LifecycleSystem`. Components: see §13.
- **Must NEVER.** Be invoked from the `update:` closure as a function; write a transform for an entity it does not currently own; import SwiftUI view types or `DomainStore`.

### L4 — SwiftUI content + design tokens (SwiftUI, MainActor)

- **Responsibility.** Render **all** content (locked paradigm). One `SpatialCard(kind:tint:state:content:)` primitive whose glass level, tint, radius, border, elevation lift, and hover/selection treatment are pure functions of `(kind, InteractionState)` read from `SpatialTokens`.
- **Owns.** All SwiftUI views; `SpatialCard`; `DismissControl` visual; `SpatialTokens`; `SpatialMotion`; edit/create UI.
- **Key types.** `SpatialCard<Content>`, `SpatialTokens`, `SpatialMotion`, `SpatialColor` (Color↔UIColor bridge), `ControlStyle`.
- **Must NEVER.** Hold business logic; drive a RealityKit entity transform directly; read system gaze hover (impossible) to compute selection.

### L5 — Pure Layout Engine (`SpatialLayoutEngine` + `VolumeMetrics`, nonisolated)

- **Responsibility.** Compute **target** positions only. Unchanged contract, extended with a `stepGraph(strategy:)` function so workflow pop-out layout leaves the VM and becomes testable.
- **Owns.** Type-cluster layout, workflow step-graph layout, clamp/bounds math, target computation.
- **Key types.** `SpatialLayoutEngine`, `VolumeMetrics`, `Placement`, `Input`.
- **Must NEVER.** Touch an `Entity`; import RealityKit/SwiftUI; become MainActor-isolated (explicit `nonisolated`, Foundation/simd only — required under `SWIFT_DEFAULT_ACTOR_ISOLATION=MainActor`).

### Data flow, end to end

1. **Down (seed/render):** tRPC → `DomainStore` rows → `SceneModel.reconcile` builds keyed VMs and runs `SpatialLayoutEngine` for targets → `SceneReducer.diff` emits a `SceneDiff` → the RealityView `update:` closure applies the diff as **identity-only mutations** (add/remove entities, swap attachment content, write `LayoutTargetComponent.target` only for `.data`-owned entities).
2. **Forward (per frame):** `LayoutTweenSystem` glides `.data` entities toward their targets; `EdgeSystem` repositions persistent edges from live endpoints; `HoverJitterSystem` applies hover jitter; `LifecycleSystem` runs entrance/exit.
3. **Up (intents):** a gesture / `ManipulationEvents` emits a typed `Intent` → `SceneModel.reduce(state, intent)` (pure) updates `InteractionState`/`InteractionMode` and dispatches a `DomainStore` action (persist on release).

---

## 4. The transform & state OWNERSHIP MODEL (the heart)

This is the load-bearing section: it is what makes the snap-back impossible by construction.

### 4.1 Single authority, encoded as a token

```swift
enum TransformOwnership: Equatable { case data, gesture, animating }
```

The **authoritative** value lives in `DomainStore.ownershipByID[id]` — an `@Observable` field, **not** SwiftUI `@State` (grafted from P2; this is precisely why the old `draggingID` guard raced). A mutation to it inside a gesture/event closure *is* observed and *is* visible to the next reconcile pass. It is **mirrored** onto each entity as `TransformAuthorityComponent.owner` purely as a cheap, render-loop-readable cache for Systems — the model is the source of truth, the component is a derived cache, and the handoff updates both atomically.

### 4.2 Target vs presented transform

Two distinct values per movable entity:

- **Target** — "where it should be," owned by L1/L5 (committed position or layout output), stored in `LayoutTargetComponent.target`.
- **Presented** — "where it is drawn," the live `TransformComponent`, owned by exactly one System/authority at a time.

`SpatialLayoutEngine` and the store only ever change the *target*. A System interpolates *presented → target*. Because they are separate values, **the store can change freely mid-drag without yanking the card**.

### 4.3 The single hard write-gate

There is exactly one rule, enforced everywhere a transform might be written:

> A layer may write an entity's `TransformComponent` **only when it is the current owner.**

- **Reconcile/`update:` closure** (`SceneDiff` apply): may set a transform **once** at creation, and otherwise writes **only** `LayoutTargetComponent.target`, and only for entities whose owner is `.data`. It is *structurally forbidden* (grafted framing from P1) from writing any live transform. This is the literal one-line gate (grafted from P2): `guard store.ownershipByID[id] == .data else { return }` before any target write.
- **`LayoutTweenSystem`:** queries `TransformAuthorityComponent.owner == .data` + `LayoutTargetComponent`; glides presented → target. Skips `.gesture`/`.animating`.
- **`DragSystem`:** owns presented during `.gesture`.
- **Entity.animate transitions:** own presented during `.animating`.

### 4.4 How drag, layout tween, and hover-scale compose without fighting

- **Drag** sets `owner = .gesture`. `LayoutTweenSystem` skips the entity; the reconcile gate skips it. `ManipulationComponent` (or the fallback `DragSystem`) is the sole writer of the presented transform.
- **Layout tween** runs only for `.data` entities; a concurrent relayout during a drag only changes that card's *target*, which it will glide to *after* the drag completes — never a snap-back.
- **Hover scale / jitter** is applied by `HoverJitterSystem` as an **offset over a clean base** (`JitterComponent.base`), never written into the persisted/target transform — so jitter never drifts the saved layout, and it composes additively with whatever authority owns the base.

### 4.5 The precise drag-snap-back kill

```
WillBegin  → store.claim(id, .gesture)         // observed; next reconcile skips it
           → mirror TransformAuthorityComponent.owner = .gesture
(drag)     → ManipulationComponent owns presented transform (RealityKit-internal)
DidUpdate  → clamp to VolumeMetrics by CONSTRAINING the value (not replacing it)
WillEnd    → read final entity.transform
           → emit Intent.dragEnded(id, finalTransform)
           → SceneModel.reduce → DomainStore.persist(id, transform) + manuallyMovedIds.insert(id)
           → set LayoutTargetComponent.target = finalTransform   // tween already there: no jump
           → store.claim(id, .animating) for a brief settle spring, then → .data
```

Three independent reasons the bug cannot recur:

1. The reconcile path **never writes a live transform** — there is no `card.position = stale` line to fight the gesture.
2. Ownership lives in observed model state, read synchronously by the gate and mirrored for Systems — **the `@State` visibility race is gone**.
3. `ManipulationComponent` owns the transform during the drag with **`releaseBehavior = .stay`** (the default `.reset` *is itself* a snap-back), so even the framework can't reset it; and the committed position is written **only** on `WillEnd`, so it is never stale-read mid-drag.

A **watchdog** in `LayoutTweenSystem` (grafted from P3) reclaims any entity stranded as `.animating` past `SpatialMotion.maxAnimationDuration` (e.g. a cancelled animation), returning it to `.data` so it can never freeze its layout follow.

---

## 5. Interaction & gesture routing

### 5.1 ManipulationComponent as primary drag

Movable entities (`taskNode`, `stepNode`, `note`, `workflowVolume`) are configured once at creation:

```swift
ManipulationComponent.configureEntity(
    entity,
    hoverEffect: .highlight,
    allowedInputTypes: .indirect,          // gaze+pinch: comfortable primary modality
    collisionShapes: [tokenSizedBox]       // from SpatialTokens, not visualBounds
)
var m = entity.components[ManipulationComponent.self]!
m.releaseBehavior = .stay                  // default .reset == snap-back
m.dynamics.scalingBehavior = .none         // cards: translate-only (notes/volumes may allow more)
m.dynamics.secondaryRotationBehavior = .none
entity.components.set(m)
```

`configureEntity` auto-adds `CollisionComponent` + `InputTargetComponent` + `HoverEffectComponent`. Lifecycle is bridged by `DragSystem` subscribing once to `ManipulationEvents.WillBegin` / `DidUpdateTransform` / `WillEnd` (see §4.5). This deletes the hand-rolled `dragGesture`, `dragStart`, `draggingID`, and the `value.convert(.local,.scene)` math — `ManipulationComponent` handles re-orientation and coordinate space natively.

### 5.2 Custom DragSystem fallback (behind a flag)

`ManipulationComponent` has a reported intermittent visionOS-26 bug where translation can stop app-wide until device restart. A hand-rolled `DragGesture` path (canonical translation-delta recipe: capture start transform on first `onChanged`, apply `value.convert(value.translation3D, from: .local, to: entity.parent!)`, clamp, persist on `onEnded`) is kept behind a feature flag and **shares the same `TransformOwnership` token contract**, so the reconcile gate and Systems treat both paths identically.

### 5.3 Typed, per-entity routing — no name parsing

`entity.name` is demoted to **only** the stable id. Role/identity live in `SpatialRoleComponent {kind, role, ownerId}`. The two top-level `.targetedToAnyEntity()` gestures and the `ctl::`/`edge::` string parsing (`:83-95`) are deleted. Each entity carries its own `GestureComponent`:

- Card: `GestureComponent(TapGesture())` → `Intent.tapped(id)` (the reducer dispatches edit / toggle-volume / note-edit / pick-for-merge-or-link by reading `SpatialRoleComponent` + current `InteractionMode`).
- `x` control: its **own** `GestureComponent(TapGesture())` → `Intent.dismissTapped(id)` reading `DismissControlComponent.ownerId`.
- Edges/ports: no gesture, no `InputTargetComponent`.

### 5.4 Connect/link modes as explicit interaction state

Merge/link cease to be view `@State` booleans. They become `InteractionMode` on `DomainStore` (enum + source id), reduced from intents. Entering a mode (a) disables `ManipulationComponent` + tap-to-edit on cards so a pick can't be misread as a drag, and (b) writes `SelectionRole.pickedMerge`/`pickedLink` to candidates so the visual driver highlights them from observed state.

### 5.5 Near/far input

Default `allowedInputTypes: .indirect` (gaze+pinch). Edges/ports are structurally non-interactive (no `InputTargetComponent`, placed in the `.edge` `CollisionGroup` excluded from all input masks). Where precise frontmost resolution is needed (connect/link targeting), use `scene.raycast(..., query: .nearest, mask: .node)` rather than gesture target order.

---

## 6. Hierarchical COLLISION & hit-testing

The `x` fix is a **triple** fix (geometry + isolation + visibility), grounded in Apple DTS forum 747256 (an enclosing parent with `InputTargetComponent` is always hit first).

### 6.1 Geometry — stop enclosing the child

- Card collider depth is sized from `SpatialTokens` to the card's **real** thickness (~0.006 m), **not** inflated to `max(z, 0.03)`. A flat SwiftUI quad has near-zero z; a 3 cm slab serves no purpose and is exactly what swallowed the badge.
- Card collider is computed **once at creation** from token dimensions and cached — never per-pass `visualBounds`, killing the recursive-bounds drift trap and per-frame churn.
